Students must remember that **the STL file format only stores geometry** — not color, material, or texture. ## 4.2 Classification of 3D Printing Processes (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD Root[3D Printing Processes] --> S1[Based on Raw Material State] Root --> S2[ISO ASTM 52900 Seven Category] S1 --> M1[Liquid Resin] S1 --> M2[Solid Filament or Wire] S1 --> M3[Powder] M1 --> SLA[SLA DLP] M2 --> FDM[FDM FFF LOM] M3 --> PBF[SLS SLM DMLS EBM BJ] S2 --> Cat1[Vat Photopolymerization] S2 --> Cat2[Material Extrusion] S2 --> Cat3[Material Jetting] S2 --> Cat4[Binder Jetting] S2 --> Cat5[Powder Bed Fusion] S2 --> Cat6[Directed Energy Deposition] S2 --> Cat7[Sheet Lamination] Cat1 --> SLA Cat2 --> FDM Cat5 --> PBF style Root fill:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style S1 fill:#334155,color:#ffffff style S2 fill:#334155,color:#ffffff style SLA fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff style FDM fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff style PBF fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff ``` > **Reading Guide:** The same process (e.g., SLA) can be classified under *Liquid State* (raw material axis) AND under *Vat Photopolymerization* (ISO axis). This dual classification is a common KTU Part-A conceptual question. ## 4.3 FDM Machine Architecture (Functional Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR Filament[Filament Spool] --> Extruder[Extruder Assembly
Drive Gear and Stepper Motor] Extruder --> HotEnd[Hot End
Heater Block 200 to 260 C] HotEnd --> Nozzle[Brass Nozzle
Diameter 0.2 to 0.8 mm] Nozzle --> BuildPlat[Build Platform
Heated Bed 60 to 110 C] Controller[Main Controller Board
Arduino Mega or Duet] -->|G Code| Extruder Controller -->|G Code| XMotor[X Y Stepper Motors] Controller -->|G Code| ZMotor[Z Axis Stepper] Controller -->|PWM Signal| HotEnd Controller -->|PWM Signal| BuildPlat style Controller fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style HotEnd fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000 style Nozzle fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff ``` > **Reading Guide:** The Controller is the brain. The Hot End is the only component above the polymer's glass transition temperature. The Build Platform is heated to prevent warping — a frequent source of print failure that examiners love to ask about. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Additive Manufacturing. List any four advantages of 3D Printing over conventional manufacturing.** **Model Answer:** Additive Manufacturing (AM), as per ISO/ASTM 52900, is the process of joining materials to make parts from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing. **Four Advantages:** 1. **High material utilization** — waste is minimized (often > 90% efficiency) since material is added only where needed. 2. **Design freedom** — complex internal geometries, lattices, and undercuts that are impossible with conventional machining can be produced directly. 3. **No tooling required** — eliminates the cost and lead time of moulds, dies, and fixtures; ideal for prototypes and mass customization. 4. **Short lead time** — a digital CAD model can be converted to a physical part within hours, accelerating product development cycles. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is an STL file? Why is slicing necessary before 3D printing?** **Model Answer:** An **STL (Standard Tessellation Language)** file is the standard data format used to describe the surface geometry of a 3D object as a collection of triangular facets. Each facet is defined by three vertices in 3D space and a unit normal vector pointing outward. STL files contain only geometric data (no color, material, or units). **Why Slicing is Necessary:** - 3D printers cannot interpret a continuous 3D surface directly; they require discrete instructions. - The slicer converts the STL mesh into **horizontal cross-sectional layers** of thickness $t_l$ and generates **G-code** that specifies the precise toolpath, speed, temperature, and extrusion for each layer. - Slicing also enables the addition of **support structures**, **infill patterns**, and **build orientation** optimization. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the ISO/ASTM 52900 classification of Additive Manufacturing processes. Describe any **three** powder-bed-based processes with neat sketches and their typical applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cuboidal part of dimensions $100 \text{ mm} \times 60 \text{ mm} \times 40 \text{ mm}$ is to be printed on an FDM machine with a layer thickness of $0.2$ mm, nozzle diameter $0.4$ mm, print speed $60$ mm/s, and per-layer recoating time of $1.5$ s. Machine setup time is $180$ s. Estimate the total build time. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(a) The ISO/ASTM 52900 standard classifies AM into **seven** categories: 1. Vat Photopolymerization 2. Material Extrusion 3. Material Jetting 4. Binder Jetting 5. Powder Bed Fusion 6. Directed Energy Deposition 7. Sheet Lamination **Three Powder-Bed-Based Processes:** **1. Selective Laser Sintering (SLS):** A CO₂ laser selectively sinters thermoplastic powder (typically PA12 / Nylon) by heating it just below its melting point. The build platform lowers by one layer thickness, a roller deposits a fresh powder layer, and the process repeats. **Applications:** functional prototypes, low-volume production of complex plastic parts, aerospace ducts. **2. Selective Laser Melting (SLM) / Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS):** A high-power fiber laser (200 W – 1 kW) fully melts metal powder (Ti-6Al-4V, AlSi10Mg, Inconel 718, 316L SS) in an inert argon or nitrogen atmosphere. **Applications:** aerospace structural brackets, medical implants (cranial plates, spinal cages), conformal cooling inserts for injection moulds. **3. Electron Beam Melting (EBM):** An electron beam (instead of a laser) is used as the energy source, requiring a vacuum chamber. The high energy density allows very fast build speeds but coarser surface finish. **Applications:** aerospace Ti-6Al-4V components, orthopaedic implants. > All three use a **powder bed** that simultaneously supports overhanging features, eliminating the need for dedicated support structures (except for SLM/DMLS, which use metal supports anchored to the build plate). --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(b) **Given:** - $L = 100$ mm, $W = 60$ mm, $H = 40$ mm - $t_l = 0.2$ mm, $d = 0.4$ mm, $v = 60$ mm/s - $t_{recoat} = 1.5$ s, $t_{setup} = 180$ s **Step 1: Number of layers** $$ n = \dfrac{H}{t_l} = \dfrac{40}{0.2} = 200 \text{ layers} $$ **Step 2: Scan length per layer** $$ l_{path} = \dfrac{L \cdot W}{d} = \dfrac{100 \times 60}{0.4} = 15000 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 3: Time per layer** $$ t_{layer} = \dfrac{l_{path}}{v} + t_{recoat} = \dfrac{15000}{60} + 1.5 = 250 + 1.5 = 251.5 \text{ s} $$ **Step 4: Total build time** $$ T_{build} = n \cdot t_{layer} + t_{setup} = 200 \times 251.5 + 180 = 50300 + 180 = 50480 \text{ s} $$ **Converting to hours:** $$ T_{build} = \dfrac{50480}{3600} \approx 14.02 \text{ hours} $$ **Final Answer:** $\boxed{T_{build} \approx 14.02 \text{ hours}}$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) process. Discuss the role of **support structures** and **build orientation** on part strength and surface finish. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **staircase effect** in 3D printing. An inclined surface at $30°$ to the horizontal is printed with a layer thickness of $0.1$ mm. Estimate the maximum vertical staircase error. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(a) **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) — Block Diagram:** ``` Filament Spool → Extruder Stepper → Hot End (heater) ↓ Brass Nozzle (0.2–0.8 mm) ↓ Layer deposition on Heated Build Plate ↓ Z-axis step-down → Repeat ``` A thermoplastic filament (ABS, PLA, PETG) is fed into a heated nozzle (typically 200–260 °C). The material melts and is extruded as a thin bead onto the build platform. After each layer, the platform descends by $t_l$ and the next layer is deposited on top, fusing with the previous one. **Role of Support Structures:** - Overhangs exceeding **45°** from the vertical cannot be self-supported and would sag or collapse during printing. - Support structures are sacrificial scaffolds (typically printed in a lower-density pattern or a dissolvable material like PVA) that hold up overhangs. - They must be manually removed in post-processing, leaving witness marks that affect surface finish. **Role of Build Orientation:** - **Z-direction strength is weaker** due to the layer-bond interface (anisotropic mechanical behavior). - Surfaces facing **up** (away from the build plate) show the **staircase effect** and are rougher. - Optimal orientation minimizes support usage, maximizes strength along load paths, and reduces print time. --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(b) **Staircase Effect — Explanation:** Because AM builds objects in discrete horizontal layers of thickness $t_l$, any surface that is not perfectly vertical or horizontal is approximated by a series of steps. This introduces a geometric deviation from the intended CAD surface, known as the **staircase effect**. The deviation is most pronounced on shallow-sloped surfaces and on downward-facing surfaces. **Numerical Estimation:** **Given:** Layer thickness $t_l = 0.1$ mm, surface inclination $\theta = 30°$. **Formula for maximum staircase error:** $$ e_{max} = t_l \cdot \cos(\theta) $$ **Calculation:** $$ e_{max} = 0.1 \times \cos(30°) = 0.1 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.1 \times 0.866 = 0.0866 \text{ mm} $$ **Final Answer:** $\boxed{e_{max} \approx 0.0866 \text{ mm} = 86.6 \text{ µm}}$ **Engineering Insight:** To reduce the staircase error on inclined surfaces, one can decrease the layer thickness (e.g., from 0.2 mm to 0.05 mm), re-orient the part so that critical surfaces face downward or are made vertical, or apply post-processing (sanding, machining, coating). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Mixing up "resolution" with "accuracy"** — Resolution is the smallest reproducible feature; accuracy is deviation from intended dimension. Always specify both. > 2. **Confusing SLM and DMLS** — Both are powder-bed fusion of metals, but SLM fully melts the powder whereas DMLS sinters it with partial melting (some binder phase). Examiners deduct marks for using them interchangeably. > 3. **Forgetting units in numerical derivations** — Always write units at every step; losing a unit mark is heartbreaking for a 14-mark question. > 4. **Omitting the build direction / Z-strength discussion** — A 7-mark FDM question that ignores anisotropic strength is incomplete. > 5. **Stating "3D printing is cheaper than machining" without qualification** — Only true for *low volumes, complex geometry, or custom parts*. Always qualify the comparison. > 6. **Wrong STL expansion** — "STL" does NOT stand for "Stereolithography" in modern usage; the modern origin is "Standard Tessellation Language" (sometimes still colloquially attributed to "STereoLithography"). KTU 2024 prefers **Standard Tessellation Language**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **AM definition** — ISO/ASTM 52900, layer-by-layer addition of material from 3D model data. - **Seven ISO categories** — VPP, MEX, MJT, BJT, PBF, DED, SHL (memorize the abbreviations). - **Three states of raw material** — Liquid (resin), Solid (filament/wire/sheet), Powder. - **Process chain** — CAD → STL → Slicer → G-code → Print → Post-process. - **STL format** — Triangular mesh; only stores surface geometry. - **Two resolutions** — X-Y (nozzle / spot size) and Z (layer thickness). - **Build-time formula** — $T_{build} = (H/t_l) \cdot (A / (d \cdot v) + t_{recoat}) + t_{setup}$. - **Staircase error** — $e_{max} = t_l \cdot \cos(\theta)$; surface roughness $R_a \approx t_l / 2$. - **FDM** — Filament-based, requires supports, anisotropic strength, low cost. - **SLA** — UV-cured resin, high resolution (~25 µm), supports required, brittle. - **SLS** — Powder-bed polymer, **no supports needed**, functional parts, PA12 nylon. - **SLM / DMLS** — Metal powder fully/partially melted, aerospace & medical, expensive. - **EBM** — Electron beam in vacuum, faster but rougher than SLM. - **Advantages** — Design freedom, low waste, no tooling, customization. - **Limitations** — Slow for large batches, limited material range, anisotropic, post-processing often needed. - **Key numbers to remember** — Layer thickness 0.02–0.4 mm; material efficiency > 90%; typical FDM speed 60 mm/s. - **KTU buzzwords** — "Additive", "layer-by-layer", "STL", "G-code", "support structures", "staircase effect", "build orientation".

topics.title: Material Jetting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET832 - 3D PRINTING AND TOOLING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Material Jetting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Jetting (MJ)** is an **additive manufacturing (AM) process** classified under **ASTM F2792-12** as a *droplet-based fabrication* technique in which **build material is selectively deposited in the form of discrete liquid droplets** through one or multiple **inkjet-style printheads**, and subsequently **solidified by ultra-violet (UV) light curing** layer-by-layer to construct a three-dimensional object.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEMET832)** syllabus context, Material Jetting is positioned as one of the seven primary **AM process categories**, alongside Vat Photopolymerisation, Material Extrusion, Powder Bed Fusion, Binder Jetting, Sheet Lamination, and Directed Energy Deposition.

The build material is typically a **photopolymer resin** (liquid monomer + photoinitiator) with viscosity in the range of **10–14 mPa·s** at jetting temperature, formulated specifically to allow stable droplet formation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Precision Painting Machine"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Multicolour Plotter Pen**
> Imagine a **flatbed inkjet photo printer** (the kind that prints your colour lab photographs). Instead of squirting coloured ink dots onto paper, this machine now squirts **liquid plastic droplets** onto a build platform. After every stripe is deposited, a **UV lamp (like a sun-lamp in a nail salon)** flashes across the surface to instantly **harden (cure) the droplets into a solid skin**. The platform then drops down by **one paper-thin layer (~14–32 µm)**, and the printer paints the next cross-section on top of the cured one. After hundreds of such passes, you can lift off a fully solid, smooth, multi-coloured 3D object.
> 
> *That is exactly how a Stratasys PolyJet or 3D Systems MultiJet printer works.*

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Board Key Values)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| **Droplet diameter** | **15–50 µm** | Defines minimum feature size |
| **Layer thickness** | **14–32 µm** | Highest Z-resolution among AM processes |
| **Build material viscosity** | **10–14 mPa·s** at jetting temp | Required for stable droplet ejection |
| **Surface roughness (Ra)** | **1–5 µm** | Near-injection-moulded finish |
| **Build speed** | Up to **6× faster** than SLA for small parts | Due to multi-nozzle parallel jetting |
| **Positioning accuracy** | ± **0.02 mm** | On par with CNC machining |

### 1.4 ASTM F2792-12 Classification (KTU Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **ASTM F2792-12**, Material Jetting is defined as:
> *"An additive manufacturing process in which droplets of build material are selectively deposited (e.g., jetted) to form a part."*
> 
> This places MJ in the **droplet-based** sub-class of binder-free material addition processes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Process Architecture — Step-by-Step Operating Logic

> [!NOTE]
> Material Jetting is fundamentally a **layer-wise, droplet-wise, photopolymerisation-based** process. Its working is governed by **fluid mechanics of droplet formation** at the nozzle and **photochemistry of free-radical polymerisation** in the build volume.

**Stage 1 — Build Preparation (Pre-Processing)**
- The 3D CAD model is sliced into **2D cross-sections** of thickness $\Delta z$ (typically **16 µm or 32 µm**).
- For each cross-section, a **bit-map (raster image)** is generated specifying which nozzle must fire at which X–Y coordinate.
- **Support structures** are auto-generated for overhangs > **45°** and downward-facing features.

**Stage 2 — Droplet Generation (Inkjet Actuation)**
- Build material is held in a temperature-controlled reservoir at **~60–80 °C** to maintain low viscosity.
- A controlled pressure pulse forces a **single droplet of volume $V_d \approx 1$–$30$ pL** out of each micro-nozzle orifice (diameter $d_n \approx 10$–$50$ µm).
- Droplets travel a stand-off distance of typically **1–3 mm** to the build platform.

**Stage 3 — UV Photo-Curing (Solidification)**
- Immediately after jetting, a **passive UV curing lamp** (typically a mercury-arc or LED lamp emitting at **365–405 nm**) cures the layer to a **gel-point** so it is dimensionally stable.
- A separate **high-power UV flood lamp** performs a final post-cure after the build.

**Stage 4 — Layer Repetition (Z-Step)**
- The build platform translates downward by exactly $\Delta z$.
- A **rotating / wiping roller** spreads a fresh thin film of build material.
- The next layer is jetted on top of the cured layer, forming a chemical bond (covalent cross-linking) at the inter-layer interface.

**Stage 5 — Post-Processing**
- Parts are removed, supports manually broken off, and **post-cured in a UV oven** for final mechanical properties.

### 2.2 The Two Sub-Families of Inkjet Actuation (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **classification of inkjet actuation mechanisms**. Memorise the diagram below.

**A. Continuous Inkjet (CIJ)**
- Liquid is forced under **continuous pressure** through a nozzle.
- A **piezo crystal** induces periodic Rayleigh-Plateau instabilities that break the jet into uniform droplets.
- **Charging electrodes** selectively **deflect unwanted droplets into a gutter** for recycling.
- Used in industrial marking and large-area graphics; rarely used in 3D printing.

**B. Drop-on-Demand (DOD) Inkjet** — *Dominant in MJ*
- Droplet is ejected **only when needed** (no continuous flow, no recycling gutter).
- Two actuation sub-types:

| DOD Variant | Mechanism | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|-------------|-----------|------------|----------------|
| **Thermal (Bubble-Jet) DOD** | Micro-heater rapidly vaporises a tiny volume → expanding vapour bubble pushes droplet out | Simple, cheap nozzle fabrication | High temperatures (≈300 °C) limit material choice |
| **Piezoelectric DOD** | Voltage-induced deformation of PZT/lead-zirconate-titanate crystal ejects droplet | Wide material compatibility, room-temperature operation, precise volume control | Higher nozzle cost |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write *"Material Jetting uses thermal heating to melt plastic pellets"* — this is **FALSE**. MJ uses **photopolymer resins that cure by UV light**, not thermal melting. Don't confuse it with Material Extrusion (FDM).

### 2.3 Multi-Material & Full-Color Capability

> [!NOTE]
> The standout feature of MJ systems such as **Stratasys PolyJet** and **3D Systems MultiJet Printing (MJP)** is the use of **multiple printheads (up to 8+)** in parallel:
> - Different photopolymer resins of varying **rigidity (Shore A to Shore D)** can be jetted in a single build.
> - **CMYK-style colour resins** allow over **500,000 colour combinations**.
> - **Digital materials** are created by mixing base resins *voxel-by-voxel* (e.g., a rigid-flexible gradient hinge in one print).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **dimensionless fluid-mechanics numbers** KTU examiners favour for numerical problems. Note the use of `\vert` instead of literal `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula Name | LaTeX Expression | Physical Meaning | Typical Range for MJ |
|---|--------------|------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Reynolds Number** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, L}{\mu}$ | Inertia vs. viscous forces in jet | $Re \approx 1$–$50$ |
| 2 | **Weber Number** | $We = \dfrac{\rho \, v^{2} \, L}{\sigma}$ | Inertia vs. surface-tension forces | $We \approx 1$–$20$ |
| 3 | **Ohnesorge Number** | $Oh = \dfrac{\mu}{\sqrt{\rho \, \sigma \, L}}$ | Viscous dissipation vs. capillary forces | $Oh \approx 0.1$–$1$ |
| 4 | **Inverse Ohnesorge (Z)** | $Z = \dfrac{1}{Oh} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\rho \, \sigma \, L}}{\mu}$ | Inverse Ohnesorge — printability index | $1 \lt Z \lt 10$ for stable droplets |
| 5 | **Capillary Number** | $Ca = \dfrac{\mu \, v}{\sigma}$ | Viscous vs. surface tension | $Ca \ll 1$ |
| 6 | **Droplet Volume** | $V_{d} = \dfrac{\pi \, d_{n}^{\,3}}{6}$ | Geometric volume of ejected droplet | $1$–$30$ pL |
| 7 | **Droplet Ejection Velocity (Fromm)** | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Bernoulli-derived jet velocity | $1$–$10$ m/s |
| 8 | **Layer Resolution** | $\Delta z = \dfrac{V_{d}^{1/3}}{R_{z}}$ | Z-axis layer thickness | $14$–$32$ µm |

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of liquid resin (kg/m³)
- $v$ = droplet ejection velocity (m/s)
- $L$ = characteristic length = nozzle diameter $d_n$ (m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\sigma$ = surface tension (N/m)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure pulse applied to nozzle (Pa)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Investment casting wax-pattern production** — MJ wax-like resins replace traditional injection-moulded wax patterns, cutting lead time from weeks to hours.
- **Dental and audiology industry** — high accuracy enables production of **crowns, bridges, hearing-aid shells** with sub-25 µm accuracy.
- **Medical anatomical models** — full-colour, multi-material patient-specific models from CT/MRI scans for surgical planning.
- **Soft robotics and over-moulded prototypes** — digital mixing of Shore A and Shore D resins enables a single print with rigid-soft transitions.
- **Optical prototyping** — transparent resins (e.g., Stratasys VeroClear) allow optical-component prototype validation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Printability Window (Derby Criterion)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** KTU frequently asks: *"Given a resin with viscosity $\mu$, density $\rho$, surface tension $\sigma$, and nozzle diameter $d_n$, determine whether it is jettable."* The answer is given by the **inverse Ohnesorge number $Z$**.

**Given Data:**
- Resin density: $\rho = 1100 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$
- Resin viscosity: $\mu = 0.012 \text{ Pa·s}$
- Surface tension: $\sigma = 0.035 \text{ N/m}$
- Nozzle diameter: $d_n = 30 \, \mu\text{m} = 30 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Identify the three dominant forces on the forming droplet**

Three forces act on the droplet emerging from the nozzle:

1. **Inertial force** (driving the droplet out): $F_{i} \propto \rho \, v^{2} \, L^{2}$
2. **Viscous force** (resisting ejection): $F_{\mu} \propto \mu \, v \, L$
3. **Surface-tension force** (holding droplet at orifice): $F_{\sigma} \propto \sigma \, L$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio of inertial to viscous forces (Reynolds Number)**

$$
Re = \frac{\rho \, v \, L}{\mu}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio of inertial to surface-tension forces (Weber Number)**

$$
We = \frac{\rho \, v^{2} \, L}{\sigma}
$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate velocity $v$ using the ratio $We/Re$ to obtain a material-only parameter**

$$
\frac{We}{Re} = \frac{\dfrac{\rho v^{2} L}{\sigma}}{\dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}} = \frac{\mu \, v}{\sigma} = Ca
$$

But we want to **eliminate $v$** entirely. So instead form:

$$
\frac{We^{1/2}}{Re} = \frac{\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho v^{2} L}{\sigma}}}{\dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}} = \frac{\mu}{\sqrt{\rho \, \sigma \, L}} = Oh
$$

Therefore, the **Ohnesorge number** is a velocity-independent material-nozzle constant:

$$
Oh = \frac{\mu}{\sqrt{\rho \, \sigma \, L}}
$$

**Step 5 — Invert to obtain the printability parameter $Z$**

$$
Z = \frac{1}{Oh} = \frac{\sqrt{\rho \, \sigma \, L}}{\mu}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Derby's empirical printability window**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derby (2010) Criterion:** A fluid is *jettable* in DOD mode iff
> 
> $$1 \lt Z \lt 10$$
> 
> - $Z \lt 1$ → viscous dissipation prevents droplet detachment (dripping, no ejection).
> - $Z \gt 10$ → secondary satellite droplets form, ruining resolution.

**Step 7 — Substitute numerical values**

First compute $\sqrt{\rho \, \sigma \, L}$:

$$
\sqrt{\rho \, \sigma \, L} = \sqrt{(1100)(0.035)(30 \times 10^{-6})}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{1.155 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.03398 \text{ kg/(m·s)}
$$

Then compute $Z$:

$$
Z = \frac{0.03398}{0.012} = 2.832
$$

**Step 8 — Decision**

Since $1 \lt Z = 2.832 \lt 10$, the resin is **jettable** in DOD mode at the given nozzle diameter. **No droplet detachment failure is expected.** Satellites are also unlikely.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of MJ Sub-Processes

| Feature | Thermal DOD | Piezoelectric DOD | Continuous Inkjet (CIJ) |
|---------|-------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Actuation energy** | Thermal (~300 °C pulse) | Mechanical (PZT strain) | Continuous pressure + electrostatic deflection |
| **Droplet volume** | 8–30 pL | 1–30 pL | 50–500 pL |
| **Max frequency** | ~30 kHz per nozzle | ~40 kHz per nozzle | ~100 kHz per nozzle |
| **Satellite droplets** | Common | Rare (well-tuned) | None (deflected away) |
| **Material compatibility** | Limited (thermal-sensitive) | Very broad | Moderate |
| **Cost per nozzle** | Low | Medium–High | High (charging electrodes) |
| **Use in MJ 3D printers** | Obsolete (early Objet) | **Dominant** (PolyJet, MJP) | Not used |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Layer Thickness Calculation

**Problem:** A PolyJet printer jets droplets of volume $V_d = 28$ pL with packing density $R_z = 0.95$. Compute the layer thickness $\Delta z$ assuming circular droplets of diameter $d_d = 50 \, \mu\text{m}$ arrange in a hexagonal close-packed layer.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Volume of one droplet**

$$
V_d = 28 \text{ pL} = 28 \times 10^{-12} \text{ L} = 28 \times 10^{-15} \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area of one droplet footprint**

$$
A_d = \frac{\pi \, d_d^{\,2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (50 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate volume of droplet to volume of a flat layer-patch of area $A_d$ and thickness $\Delta z$**

$$
V_d = A_d \cdot \Delta z \cdot R_z
$$

$$
\Delta z = \frac{V_d}{A_d \cdot R_z} = \frac{28 \times 10^{-15}}{(1.963 \times 10^{-9})(0.95)}
$$

$$
\Delta z = \frac{28 \times 10^{-15}}{1.865 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.501 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \approx 15 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret**

This matches the typical **14–16 µm layer thickness** advertised by Stratasys PolyJet systems. The computed $\Delta z$ confirms that the input droplet volume and packing density are consistent with the manufacturer's specifications.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process-Block Architecture of a Material Jetting System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CAD Model Import] --> A2[Slicer Software]
    A2 --> A3[Bitmap per Layer]
    A3 --> A4[Build File Upload]
    A4 --> B1[Print Controller]
    B1 --> B2[Reservoir A Rigid Resin]
    B1 --> B3[Reservoir B Flexible Resin]
    B1 --> B4[Reservoir C Support Material]
    B1 --> B5[Reservoir D Color Pigments]
    B2 --> C1[Heated Manifold 60 to 80 C]
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Piezo DOD Printhead XJet 1]
    C1 --> D2[Piezo DOD Printhead XJet 2]
    C1 --> D3[Piezo DOD Printhead XJet 3]
    D1 --> E1[Build Platform]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[UV Curing Lamp 365 nm]
    F1 --> G1[Z Stage Translation Downward by Delta Z]
    G1 --> E1
    E1 --> H1[Roller Recoating]
    H1 --> E1
    E1 --> I1{All Layers Done}
    I1 --> J1[Part Removal]
    I1 --> E1
    J1 --> K1[Support Dissolution]
    K1 --> L1[Final UV Post Cure]
    L1 --> M1[Finished 3D Part]
```

### 4.2 Droplet Ejection Physics — Fromm / Derby Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Resin Reservoir Heated] --> R2[Microchannel under Pressure]
    R2 --> R3[PZT Piezo Actuator Voltage Pulse]
    R3 --> R4[Pressure Wave Travels to Nozzle]
    R4 --> R5[Liquid Column Ejected from Nozzle]
    R5 --> R6[Surface Tension Pinches Column]
    R6 --> R7[Primary Droplet Detaches]
    R6 --> R8[Satellite Droplet Forms only if Z less than 1]
    R7 --> R9[Travels to Build Platform]
    R9 --> R10[UV Lamp Triggers Photopolymerisation]
    R10 --> R11[Solid Voxel Cured]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Is a Resin Jettable?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Given mu rho sigma d_n] --> S2[Compute Z equals sqrt rho sigma L over mu]
    S2 --> S3{Z less than 1}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[REJECT Too Viscous Dripping]
    S3 -- No --> S5{Z greater than 10}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[REJECT Satellite Droplets Form]
    S5 -- No --> S7[ACCEPT Resin is Jettable]
    S4 --> S8[End]
    S6 --> S8[End]
    S7 --> S8[End]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Multi-Material Voxel Mixing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Nozzles
        N1[Nozzle A Rigid Shore D]
        N2[Nozzle B Flexible Shore A]
        N3[Nozzle C Color Cyan]
        N4[Nozzle D Color Magenta]
        N5[Nozzle E Color Yellow]
        N6[Nozzle F Support Wax Like]
    end
    N1 --> VOX[Single Voxel in 3D Space]
    N2 --> VOX
    N3 --> VOX
    N4 --> VOX
    N5 --> VOX
    N6 --> VOX
    VOX --> MIX[Digital Material Composition per Voxel]
    MIX --> PART[Final Part with Gradient Properties]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Material Jetting. Mention any two build materials used in this process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Material Jetting is an ASTM F2792-12 classified additive manufacturing process in which **droplets of build material are selectively deposited** through inkjet-style printheads and **solidified layer-by-layer**, typically by UV photopolymerisation, to form a 3D object.

Two commonly used build materials:
1. **Photopolymer resins** (acrylates / methacrylates with photoinitiators) — used in Stratasys PolyJet and 3D Systems MultiJet systems.
2. **Wax-like resins** — used for investment-casting pattern production (e.g., 3D Systems Visijet M3).

*(1 Mark for definition, 1 Mark each for the two materials = 3 Marks)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Continuous Inkjet (CIJ) and Drop-on-Demand (DOD) inkjet methods.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Continuous Inkjet (CIJ) | Drop-on-Demand (DOD) |
|---------|--------------------------|-----------------------|
| Droplet generation | Continuous jet broken into droplets by Rayleigh instability | Droplet ejected only when needed |
| Droplet control | Electrostatic deflection of unwanted droplets to gutter | Direct on/off actuation |
| Recycling required | Yes (un-jetted droplets recirculated) | No recycling |
| Use in 3D printing | Rarely used | **Dominant in MJ systems** |

*(2 Marks for tabular distinction, 1 Mark for the final usage conclusion = 3 Marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE pattern requires **internal choice** — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **step-by-step working principle of the Material Jetting process**. Highlight the role of UV curing and recoating. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Material Jetting process works as follows:

1. **CAD to Slicing (1 Mark):** A 3D CAD model is sliced into 2D cross-sectional bitmaps of thickness $\Delta z = 14$–$32$ µm.

2. **Resin Heating (1 Mark):** Photopolymer resin in the reservoir is heated to $60$–$80$ °C to reduce viscosity to the jettable range (10–14 mPa·s).

3. **Droplet Generation (2 Marks):** A piezo-DOD printhead with thousands of micro-nozzles (10–50 µm orifice diameter) ejects single droplets of volume 1–30 pL at each pixel location specified by the bitmap.

4. **UV Curing (2 Marks):** As soon as a layer is deposited, a **passive UV lamp (365 nm)** partially cures the layer to a gel state, providing dimensional stability for the next layer. The **role of UV** is to trigger **free-radical photopolymerisation** that converts liquid monomer into cross-linked solid polymer.

5. **Z-Translation & Recoating (1 Mark):** The build platform lowers by $\Delta z$. A **roller recoater** spreads a fresh uniform thin film of resin, ensuring no air gaps and uniform thickness for the next layer.

**(b)** A photopolymer resin has the following properties at the jetting temperature: $\mu = 0.013$ Pa·s, $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³, $\sigma = 0.030$ N/m. The nozzle diameter is $d_n = 25 \, \mu\text{m}$. Determine the inverse Ohnesorge number $Z$ and comment on whether the resin is jettable in DOD mode. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- $\mu = 0.013 \text{ Pa·s}$
- $\rho = 1050 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$
- $\sigma = 0.030 \text{ N/m}$
- $L = d_n = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Compute $\sqrt{\rho \, \sigma \, L}$** *(2 Marks)*

$$
\sqrt{\rho \, \sigma \, L} = \sqrt{(1050)(0.030)(25 \times 10^{-6})}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{7.875 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.02807 \text{ kg/(m·s)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $Z$** *(2 Marks)*

$$
Z = \frac{\sqrt{\rho \, \sigma \, L}}{\mu} = \frac{0.02807}{0.013} = 2.159
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Derby's printability criterion** *(2 Marks)*

For stable DOD droplet ejection:
$$1 \lt Z \lt 10$$

Since $Z = 2.159$ lies inside this window, **the resin is jettable**.

**Step 4 — Final comment** *(1 Mark)*

The droplet is expected to detach cleanly from the nozzle without satellites and without viscous dripping. This formulation is therefore suitable for use in PolyJet-class Material Jetting equipment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Compare **Thermal DOD** and **Piezoelectric DOD** actuation mechanisms used in Material Jetting. List two advantages of Piezoelectric DOD over Thermal DOD. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Thermal DOD (Bubble-Jet) | Piezoelectric DOD |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| **Actuation principle** | Rapid heating of resistor vaporises a small volume of liquid, forming a bubble that ejects the droplet. | Voltage-induced deformation of a piezoelectric (PZT) crystal ejects the droplet by direct mechanical displacement. |
| **Local temperature** | ~300 °C (brief) | Room temperature |
| **Droplet volume control** | Limited by bubble dynamics | Precise, controlled by pulse amplitude and waveform |
| **Material compatibility** | Poor (heat-sensitive polymers degrade) | Very broad |
| **Nozzle cost** | Low (silicon micromachining) | Medium (PZT crystal) |
| **Frequency** | Up to ~30 kHz/nozzle | Up to ~40 kHz/nozzle |
| **Typical application in MJ** | Early 1990s Objet printers | **Current PolyJet, MJP systems** |

*(5 Marks for comparison table)*

**Two advantages of Piezoelectric DOD** *(2 Marks)*:
1. **Wider material compatibility** — piezo DOD works at room temperature, allowing heat-sensitive resins and waxes to be jetted without thermal degradation.
2. **Better droplet volume control** — droplet volume is precisely tunable by the voltage waveform, enabling consistent 1–30 pL droplets with minimal satellite formation.

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages, limitations, and major applications** of Material Jetting. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages** *(3 Marks)*:
1. **Highest dimensional accuracy and surface finish** in AM (down to 14 µm layer thickness and Ra ≈ 1–5 µm).
2. **Multi-material and full-colour capability** in a single build (digital material voxel mixing).
3. **High build speed** for small parts (multi-nozzle parallel jetting).

**Limitations** *(2 Marks)*:
1. **Restricted material range** — primarily limited to photopolymers and waxes; cannot process metals or engineering thermoplastics directly.
2. **Support material required** for overhangs, and post-processing (support removal + UV post-cure) adds time and labour.

**Major Applications** *(2 Marks)*:
1. **Investment casting patterns** (wax-like photopolymers).
2. **Dental and audiology** (crowns, bridges, hearing-aid shells).
3. **Full-colour anatomical models** for surgical planning.
4. **Soft-touch and rigid-flexible prototyping** (digital materials).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Material Jetting (Module 2)**
> 1. **Confusing Material Jetting with Material Extrusion (FDM):** MJ uses **liquid photopolymer + UV**, NOT thermoplastic pellets + heater. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the recoating step:** A layer-by-layer description that misses the **roller recoater** loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing "Thermal DOD is used in modern PolyJet":** This is wrong. Modern PolyJet is **piezo DOD**. Thermal DOD is obsolete in 3D printing.
> 4. **Skipping the printability criterion:** When solving numericals, students compute $Z$ but forget to **state Derby's window $1 < Z < 10$** and check it — losing 1 mark.
> 5. **Not specifying the unit of viscosity as Pa·s or mPa·s:** Always quote units to avoid 0.5 mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Material Jetting"**

- **ASTM classification:** ASTM F2792-12 — droplet-based deposition.
- **Core definition:** Selective deposition of liquid droplets that solidify via UV photopolymerisation.
- **Two inkjet mechanisms:** Continuous Inkjet (CIJ) vs Drop-on-Demand (DOD). **DOD dominates** in MJ.
- **Two DOD types:** Thermal (bubble-jet, obsolete) and Piezoelectric (current industry standard).
- **Build materials:** Photopolymer resins (acrylates, methacrylates), wax-like resins, color pigments. NOT thermoplastics, NOT metals.
- **Solidification mechanism:** UV light at ~365 nm initiates free-radical polymerisation.
- **Key process parameters:**
  - Layer thickness $\Delta z = 14$–$32$ µm.
  - Droplet volume $V_d = 1$–$30$ pL.
  - Nozzle diameter $d_n = 10$–$50$ µm.
  - Viscosity at jetting = 10–14 mPa·s.
- **Printability index (Derby criterion):** $1 \lt Z \lt 10$, where $Z = \dfrac{\sqrt{\rho \, \sigma \, L}}{\mu}$.
- **Major OEMs:** Stratasys (PolyJet), 3D Systems (MultiJet / MJP), Xjet (NanoParticle Jetting).
- **Unique selling point:** Multi-material voxel mixing → **digital materials**, **full-colour**, **rigid-flexible gradient in one print**.
- **Major applications:** Investment casting wax patterns, dental, hearing aids, surgical models, optical prototypes.
- **Limitations:** Limited material palette, support material mandatory, post-cure step required, slow for large structural parts.
- **Always mention in long answers:** recoating roller + UV pre-cure + UV post-cure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET832 - 3D PRINTING AND TOOLING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)** is a material extrusion-based additive manufacturing process, originally patented by **S. Scott Crump** in 1989 and commercialized by **Stratasys Inc.** It is a layer-by-layer manufacturing process in which a thermoplastic filament is fed into a heated liquefier (print head/nozzle), melted to a semi-liquid state, and then extruded through a fine nozzle onto a build platform in a precisely controlled path. The material is deposited in discrete cross-sectional layers, where each newly extruded layer fuses with the previously solidified layer beneath it through **thermal bonding**, gradually building a complete 3D solid object from a digital CAD model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** FDM is a cornerstone topic of Module 2. According to the official KTU syllabus, students must understand the working principle, process parameters, materials, advantages, limitations, and applications. FDM is classified under the **ASTM F2792-12a** standard as a **"Material Extrusion"** process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonym Note:** FDM is the trademarked name by Stratasys. The generic, non-proprietary industry term used by **ISO/ASTM 52900** is **Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF)**. Both terms refer to the same process technology.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of FDM like **decorating a cake with an icing gun** 🍰:

- The **icing gun nozzle** = the FDM **print head** that squeezes out material
- The **icing (buttercream)** = the **molten thermoplastic filament** that has been heated to flow
- Your **steady hand movement** in patterns = the **computer-controlled X-Y motion** of the printer head
- The **layered icing** that builds up the design = the **stacked 2D layers** that create the final 3D object

The cake decorator controls a 2D path to draw a 3D shape — exactly what an FDM machine does! Each decorative stroke hardens slightly before the next is added on top, and together they form a structural shape — this is **additive layer manufacturing** at its simplest.

## 1.3 Why FDM is Important in Modern Engineering

FDM dominates the **desktop 3D printing market** because of its:
- **Low cost** (entry-level printers start from ₹15,000–₹50,000)
- **Material versatility** (PLA, ABS, PETG, TPU, Nylon, PC)
- **Ease of operation** and minimal post-processing
- **Office-safe operation** (low toxicity, enclosed variants available)
- **Rapid prototyping** turnaround (hours, not days)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Adoption Rate:** As per **Wohlers Report 2024**, FDM/FFF systems represent the **largest installed base of industrial AM systems globally**, accounting for over **70%** of all polymer 3D printers sold annually. The global market is valued at over **USD 4.2 billion** in 2024.

## 1.4 Classification under AM Process Family

| Classification Standard | Category |
|--------------------------|----------|
| ASTM F2792-12a | Material Extrusion |
| ISO/ASTM 52900:2021 | Material Extrusion |
| KTU OEMET832 Module 2 | Polymer-Based AM Process |
| Process Energy Source | Thermal (heated nozzle) |
| Material State at Deposition | Molten/Semi-molten thermoplastic |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

FDM works on the principle of **controlled material extrusion** through a heated nozzle, where:

1. A **solid thermoplastic filament** (typically **1.75 mm** or **2.85 mm** diameter) is fed from a spool via a **drive gear system** (hobbed gear + idler).
2. The filament enters a **heating chamber** (liquefier) maintained at a temperature between **180°C to 320°C**, depending on the polymer.
3. The polymer transitions to a **viscous melt** (viscosity ~ **100–10,000 Pa·s**).
4. A precisely controlled force pushes the molten polymer through a **brass or hardened steel nozzle** (diameter typically **0.2 mm to 1.0 mm**).
5. The nozzle is moved in the **X-Y plane** by a gantry or delta robot, depositing material along the **toolpath** (sliced from the CAD model).
6. After each layer, the **Z-axis (build platform or nozzle)** is raised/lowered by the **layer thickness** (typically **0.05 mm to 0.4 mm**).
7. The process repeats layer by layer until the part is complete.

## 2.2 Critical Components of an FDM System

| S.No | Component | Function | Key Specifications |
|------|-----------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Filament Spool** | Stores raw thermoplastic | 1 kg / 750 g spools, diameter 1.75 or 2.85 mm |
| 2 | **Drive System** | Feeds filament into heater | Hobbed gear with stepper motor |
| 3 | **Liquefier (Hot End)** | Melts the polymer | Heater block with thermistor |
| 4 | **Nozzle** | Extrudes molten material | Brass, 0.2–1.0 mm orifice |
| 5 | **Build Platform** | Supports part during printing | Heated bed (50°C–110°C) |
| 6 | **X-Y Gantry** | Positions nozzle in plane | Stepper motors + belts/lead screws |
| 7 | **Z-Axis Assembly** | Vertical layer stepping | Lead screw or ball screw |
| 8 | **Enclosure** | Temperature stability | Optional, 30°C–60°C ambient |
| 9 | **Firmware/Controller** | Coordinates motion | Marlin, Klipper, Smoothieware |
| 10 | **Slicer Software** | Generates G-code | Cura, PrusaSlicer, Simplify3D |

## 2.3 Process Parameter Categories

### A. Process Parameters (User Controllable)
- **Nozzle Temperature** ($T_n$): 180°C–320°C
- **Bed Temperature** ($T_b$): 50°C–110°C
- **Layer Height** ($h$): 0.05–0.4 mm
- **Print Speed** ($v$): 30–150 mm/s
- **Nozzle Diameter** ($d$): 0.2–1.0 mm
- **Infill Density** ($\rho_{infill}$): 0%–100%
- **Infill Pattern**: Grid, Triangular, Honeycomb, Gyroid
- **Build Orientation**: X, Y, Z axes alignment

### B. Material Parameters
- **Glass Transition Temperature** ($T_g$)
- **Melting Temperature** ($T_m$)
- **Viscosity** ($\mu$)
- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion** (CTE)
- **Young's Modulus** ($E$)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet for FDM — Master these equations for ESE!**

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v$ | **Volumetric Flow Rate** from nozzle (ideal) | $Q$ = m³/s, $d$ = nozzle dia (m), $v$ = filament feed velocity (m/s) |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v_f$ | **Volumetric Flow Rate** from filament side | $D$ = filament dia (m), $v_f$ = filament feed rate (m/s) |
| 3 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | **Mass Flow Rate** | $\dot{m}$ = kg/s, $\rho$ = material density (kg/m³) |
| 4 | $t_{layer} = \dfrac{L_{path}}{v_{print}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{N_{nozzle}}$ | **Time per layer** | $L_{path}$ = path length (mm), $v_{print}$ = print speed (mm/s), $N$ = number of nozzles |
| 5 | $t_{total} = \dfrac{H}{h} \cdot t_{layer}$ | **Total build time** | $H$ = part height (mm), $h$ = layer height (mm) |
| 6 | $\sigma_{interlayer} = \dfrac{F}{A_{contact}} = \dfrac{F}{w \cdot L}$ | **Inter-layer bond stress** | $w$ = road width, $L$ = bond length |
| 7 | $R_a \approx \dfrac{h}{2}$ (for 45° step) | **Surface roughness estimate** | $R_a$ = arithmetic mean roughness (µm) |
| 8 | $E_{anisotropic} = f(\theta, \rho_{infill})$ | **Effective Young's Modulus** | $\theta$ = build angle, $\rho$ = infill density |
| 9 | $V_{part} = \pi r^2 \cdot h_{road} \cdot L_{path}$ | **Bead/Road volume** | $r = d/2$ (nozzle radius), $h_{road}$ = layer height |
| 10 | $S = \dfrac{w - h}{h} \cdot 100\%$ | **Road Overlap / Stair-stepping** | $w$ = road width, $h$ = layer height |

## 2.5 Bonding Mechanism Between Layers

The strength of an FDM part depends critically on **inter-layer adhesion**, governed by:
1. **Thermal bonding (Welding theory)**: Adjacent layers remain above $T_g$ during deposition → molecular diffusion across the interface.
2. **Polymer chain interdiffusion** characterized by reptation time ($\tau_d$).
3. **The stronger the thermal gradient control, the better the weld strength** between layers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** FDM parts are inherently **anisotropic** — strength along Z-axis (build direction) is typically **40–60% lower** than in the X-Y plane because layer interfaces form planes of weakness.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Boeing uses FDM-printed jigs and fixtures certified under **FAA qualification** for the 787 Dreamliner.
- **Medical:** Custom anatomical models, surgical guides, prosthetics (e.g., **e-NABLE** Foundation).
- **Automotive:** Custom fixtures, low-volume functional parts.
- **Architecture:** Conceptual scale models.
- **Education:** STEM lab tool — KTU colleges widely use FDM for student projects.
- **Defence:** Field-deployable spare parts via portable FDM units.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Volumetric Flow Rate Equivalence (Conservation of Mass)

The fundamental mass balance across the FDM extrusion system states that the volume of material entering the liquefier (from filament) must equal the volume leaving the nozzle — assuming **incompressible melt flow** and **no leakage**.

### Given:
- Filament diameter = $D$ (m)
- Filament feed velocity = $v_f$ (m/s)
- Nozzle diameter = $d$ (m)
- Nozzle exit velocity = $v_n$ (m/s)

### Step 1: Volume flow at filament inlet

$$Q_{in} = A_{filament} \cdot v_f = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v_f$$

### Step 2: Volume flow at nozzle outlet

$$Q_{out} = A_{nozzle} \cdot v_n = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v_n$$

### Step 3: Apply Conservation of Mass (Incompressible flow)

Since density is constant and there is no accumulation inside the liquefier:

$$Q_{in} = Q_{out}$$

$$\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v_f = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v_n$$

### Step 4: Solve for nozzle exit velocity

$$v_n = \left(\dfrac{D}{d}\right)^2 \cdot v_f$$

### Step 5: Physical Interpretation

> This is the famous **"die swell / draw-down" relationship** in FDM. Since $D \gg d$ (e.g., 1.75 mm vs 0.4 mm), the **velocity amplification factor** is:

$$\dfrac{v_n}{v_f} = \left(\dfrac{1.75}{0.4}\right)^2 \approx 19.14$$

This means the molten polymer exits the nozzle at **~19 times** the filament feed speed — a critical factor in controlling extrusion pressure and bead quality.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Build Time Estimation

### Given:
- Part height = $H$ (mm)
- Layer height = $h$ (mm)
- Total path length per layer = $L$ (mm)
- Print speed = $v$ (mm/s)
- Number of nozzles = $n$ (1 for single extrusion)

### Step 1: Number of layers

$$N_{layers} = \dfrac{H}{h}$$

### Step 2: Time to print one layer (one full pass)

$$t_{layer} = \dfrac{L_{path}}{v_{print}}$$

### Step 3: Total build time (ignoring acceleration/deceleration losses)

$$t_{total} = N_{layers} \cdot t_{layer} = \dfrac{H}{h} \cdot \dfrac{L_{path}}{v_{print}}$$

### Step 4: Real-world correction factor

In practice, acceleration, deceleration, retraction, and Z-hops add **30–60%** overhead. Modified equation:

$$t_{actual} = t_{total} \cdot k_{eff}$$

where $k_{eff}$ is the **effective efficiency factor** (typically **1.4 to 1.8** for Cartesian FDM printers).

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A cube of side 50 mm is printed with $h = 0.2$ mm layer height. Each layer has approximately 250 mm of perimeter path + infill equivalent to 1500 mm. Print speed = 60 mm/s. Estimate the build time.

**Solution:**

$$N_{layers} = \dfrac{50}{0.2} = 250 \text{ layers}$$

$$L_{path} = 250 + 1500 = 1750 \text{ mm}$$

$$t_{layer} = \dfrac{1750}{60} \approx 29.17 \text{ s}$$

$$t_{total} = 250 \cdot 29.17 \approx 7292 \text{ s} \approx 2.03 \text{ hours}$$

Applying $k_{eff} = 1.6$:

$$t_{actual} \approx 2.03 \times 1.6 \approx 3.25 \text{ hours}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Surface Roughness from Stair-Stepping

### Given:
- Layer height = $h$ (mm)
- Build surface angle = $\theta$ (degrees from horizontal)

### Step 1: Stair-step geometry

Each layer creates a vertical step of height $h$ and a horizontal run of $h/\tan\theta$.

### Step 2: Theoretical $R_a$ approximation

For surfaces inclined at angle $\theta$:

$$R_a \approx \dfrac{h}{2} \cdot \cos\theta$$

### Step 3: Limiting cases

- **Vertical wall** ($\theta = 90°$): $R_a = 0$ (perfectly smooth)
- **Horizontal surface** ($\theta = 0°$): $R_a = h/2$ (maximum stair-stepping)
- **45° surface** ($\theta = 45°$): $R_a = h/(2\sqrt{2})$

### Step 4: Practical implication

> For a smooth surface finish, either: (a) reduce layer height $h$, or (b) orient the part such that critical surfaces are vertical or near-vertical.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Mass of Filament Consumed

### Given:
- Part volume = $V_{part}$ (m³)
- Infill density = $\phi$ (0 to 1)
- Support material volume = $V_{sup}$ (m³)
- Wall/perimeter volume = $V_{wall}$ (m³)
- Material density = $\rho$ (kg/m³)

### Step 1: Total material volume required

$$V_{total} = V_{wall} + \phi \cdot V_{infill\_region} + V_{support}$$

### Step 2: Mass of filament

$$m_{filament} = \rho \cdot V_{total}$$

### Step 3: Filament length (for purchasing 1.75 mm spool)

$$L_{filament} = \dfrac{V_{total}}{\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}} = \dfrac{4 V_{total}}{\pi D^2}$$

### Step 4: Worked Example

**Problem:** A part requires 50 cm³ of PLA material. PLA density = 1240 kg/m³. Find filament length for 1.75 mm diameter filament.

**Solution:**

$$m = 1240 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6} = 0.062 \text{ kg} = 62 \text{ g}$$

$$L_{filament} = \dfrac{4 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \cdot (1.75 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

$$L_{filament} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{9.621 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 20.79 \text{ m}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Parameter-Stress-Strain Influence

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix maps the directional influence of FDM parameters on the mechanical behavior of printed parts. Memorize this for KTU ESE.

| Parameter ↑ (Increased) | Tensile Strength | Surface Finish | Build Time | Material Cost |
|--------------------------|------------------|----------------|------------|----------------|
| **Layer Height** | ↓ (slightly) | ↓ (rougher) | ↓ (faster) | ↓ (less material) |
| **Nozzle Temperature** | ↑ (better bonding) | ↑ (smoother flow) | = (no effect) | = (no effect) |
| **Print Speed** | ↓ (poor bonding) | ↓ (vibration) | ↓ (faster) | = (no effect) |
| **Infill Density** | ↑↑ (strong) | = (no effect) | ↑ (slower) | ↑↑ (costly) |
| **Bed Temperature** | ↑ (less warping) | ↑ (better adhesion) | = (no effect) | = (no effect) |
| **Nozzle Diameter** | ↓ (wider beads weaker bond?) | ↓ (visible lines) | ↓ (faster) | = (no effect) |
| **Build Orientation (Z-aligned load)** | ↑ (X-Y is stronger) | = (no effect) | = (no effect) | = (no effect) |

## 3.6 Mathematical Boundary Conditions for Successful FDM Printing

For a successful FDM print, the following conditions must be satisfied simultaneously:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{nozzle} &> T_{melt} \text{ (polymer must be molten)} \\
T_{bed} &> T_{glass\, transition} - 20°C \text{ (to prevent warping)} \\
T_{ambient} &< T_{glass\, transition} \text{ (rapid solidification required)} \\
P_{extrusion} &< P_{max,\,liquefier} \text{ (no clogging)} \\
v_{print} &\leq v_{max,\,nozzle} \text{ (avoid starvation)} \\
h &\geq 0.05 \text{ mm (minimum layer thickness)}
\end{aligned}$$

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 FDM Working Principle — Block-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **functional flow architecture** of the FDM process — from digital input to final physical part.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CAD Model STL File] --> A2[Slicer Software Cura PrusaSlicer]
    A2 --> A3[G code Generation Layer by Layer]
    A3 --> A4[Firmware Controller Marlin Klipper]
    A4 --> B1[Stepper Motor Drive X Y Z Axes]
    A4 --> B2[Extruder Drive Gear Feeds Filament]
    B2 --> B3[Heated Liquefier 180 to 320 C]
    B3 --> B4[Nozzle Extrudes Molten Polymer]
    B1 --> B4
    B4 --> C1[Deposition on Build Platform]
    C1 --> C2[Layer Solidification Below Tg]
    C2 --> C3[Z axis Increment Layer Height h]
    C3 --> C4{Layer Count Complete?}
    C4 -->|No| A4
    C4 -->|Yes| D1[Final 3D Part]
    C1 --> E1[Heated Bed 50 to 110 C]
    E1 --> C2
    D1 --> F1[Post Processing Support Removal]
    F1 --> F2[Final Part Ready]
```

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, OEMET832]

**Define Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM). List any four materials commonly used in FDM printing.**

**Model Answer:**

Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) is a material extrusion-based additive manufacturing process in which a thermoplastic filament is melted in a heated liquefier and extruded through a nozzle onto a build platform in a layer-by-layer manner to create a 3D object from a digital CAD model.

**Four commonly used FDM materials:**

1. **PLA (Polylactic Acid)** — biodegradable, easy to print, used for prototypes.
2. **ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene)** — tough and heat-resistant, used for functional parts.
3. **PETG (Polyethylene Terephthalate Glycol)** — good chemical resistance and toughness.
4. **TPU (Thermoplastic Polyurethane)** — flexible elastomer for rubber-like parts.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, OEMET832]

**Explain the role of the build platform in FDM. Why is the bed heated?**

**Model Answer:**

The **build platform** (also called the print bed) is the flat surface on which the FDM part is built layer by layer. It must be **flat, level, and stable** to ensure accurate deposition of the first layer and proper adhesion of subsequent layers.

The **bed is heated** for the following reasons:

1. **Reduces warping** by keeping the bottom layers above the polymer's glass transition temperature, preventing differential cooling.
2. **Improves first-layer adhesion**, ensuring the part does not detach mid-print.
3. **Maintains thermal uniformity**, which is critical for materials with high CTE (e.g., ABS).

Typical bed temperatures range from **50°C (PLA)** to **110°C (ABS/PC)**.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 MARKS

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, OEMET832, Module 2]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the working principle of Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) with a neat block diagram. List the major components of an FDM system and explain any three in detail.

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**

Fused Deposition Modelling works on the principle of **controlled material extrusion of molten thermoplastic**. A digital 3D CAD model is first converted into STL format and then sliced into 2D horizontal cross-sections by slicer software. Each slice is converted into G-code, which is sent to the printer's controller.

A thermoplastic filament (typically 1.75 mm) is fed from a spool into a heated liquefier by an extruder drive system. The heater block melts the polymer to a temperature between **180°C and 320°C** depending on the material. The molten polymer is then forced through a brass nozzle (typically 0.4 mm) onto a heated build platform. The nozzle traces the cross-sectional path in the X-Y plane. After completing one layer, the build platform (or nozzle) is moved down (or up) by the layer height, and the next layer is deposited. Each new layer bonds with the previous one through thermal welding. The process repeats until the entire 3D part is fabricated.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[CAD Model] → [Slicer] → [G-code] → [Controller] → [Stepper Motors]
                ↓
        [Extruder] → [Liquefier] → [Nozzle] → [Build Platform] → [3D Part]
```

**Major Components of FDM System:**

1. **Extruder Assembly**
2. **Hot End (Liquefier + Nozzle)**
3. **Build Platform (Heated Bed)**
4. **Motion System (X-Y Gantry, Z-Axis)**
5. **Controller Electronics (Firmware)**
6. **Enclosure (optional)**
7. **Filament Spool and Feed System**

**Detailed Explanation of Three Components:**

**1. Extruder Assembly:**
The extruder consists of a **stepper motor**, a **hobbed drive gear** (knurled steel wheel), and an **idler** (spring-loaded bearing). The stepper motor rotates the hobbed gear which grips the filament and pushes it into the hot end. There are two configurations: **Bowden** (motor remote, uses PTFE tube) and **Direct Drive** (motor mounted directly above hot end). Direct drive offers better control for flexible filaments, while Bowden enables faster movement due to reduced moving mass.

**2. Hot End (Liquefier + Nozzle):**
The hot end consists of a **heater block** (aluminum or copper) with a **cartridge heater** (typically 40W or 50W) and a **thermistor** for temperature feedback. The block maintains the polymer above its melting point. The **brass nozzle** at the bottom has a precisely drilled orifice (0.2–1.0 mm). Nozzle material choices include brass (standard), hardened steel (for abrasive composites), and ruby-tipped (for medical applications). Nozzle temperature is the most critical process parameter.

**3. Motion System:**
The X-Y gantry is driven by **stepper motors** via **GT2 timing belts** or **lead screws**. Each stepper motor is paired with a driver (e.g., A4988, TMC2208) that receives step and direction signals. Linear guides (smooth rods, MGN rails) ensure precise positioning. The Z-axis typically uses a single lead screw or dual lead screws. Resolution is typically **0.05 mm to 0.1 mm** per step in X-Y and **0.0125 mm** in Z.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Discuss the process parameters of FDM. Explain how layer height, print speed, and nozzle temperature affect the quality of the printed part.

**Model Answer:**

**FDM Process Parameters:**

Process parameters are the controllable variables that directly determine the **dimensional accuracy, surface quality, mechanical strength, and build time** of FDM-printed parts.

**1. Layer Height (h):**

Layer height is the vertical distance by which the build platform is incremented after each layer. Common values range from **0.05 mm (high detail)** to **0.4 mm (fast draft)**.

- **Smaller layer height (e.g., 0.1 mm):** → Better surface finish, smoother curves, higher resolution, longer build time.
- **Larger layer height (e.g., 0.3 mm):** → Faster build, more visible layer lines, weaker inter-layer bonding, less detail.

Layer height also affects **mechanical strength**: thinner layers provide more layer interfaces and can either improve or degrade strength depending on bonding quality.

**2. Print Speed (v):**

Print speed is the velocity at which the nozzle traverses the X-Y plane. Typical range: **30 to 150 mm/s**.

- **Low speed (30–50 mm/s):** → Better inter-layer bonding, higher strength, no vibration artifacts, longer build time.
- **High speed (80–150 mm/s):** → Faster build, but risk of **vibration, ringing/ghosting, under-extrusion, and weak bonding** due to insufficient melt flow.

Print speed is closely tied to **volumetric flow rate** $Q = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v$. The printer's firmware must ensure that the extruder can supply material at the required rate to avoid starvation.

**3. Nozzle Temperature ($T_n$):**

Nozzle temperature is the temperature of the heater block/liquefier. It varies with material:

| Material | Typical Nozzle Temp |
|----------|---------------------|
| PLA | 190°C–220°C |
| PETG | 220°C–250°C |
| ABS | 230°C–260°C |
| TPU | 210°C–230°C |
| PC | 260°C–300°C |
| Nylon | 250°C–280°C |

- **Higher temperature** → Lower polymer viscosity, better flow, improved inter-layer bonding, stronger part. However, excessive heat causes **stringing, oozing, and degradation**.
- **Lower temperature** → Higher viscosity, poor bonding, weak parts, but cleaner details.

The temperature differential between nozzle and bed must be carefully controlled. The combination of **nozzle temperature, bed temperature, and ambient enclosure temperature** determines the cooling profile and final part quality.

**Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Increase Effect on Strength | Increase Effect on Surface | Increase Effect on Time |
|-----------|------------------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Layer Height | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ (faster) |
| Print Speed | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ (faster) |
| Nozzle Temp | ↑ | ↑ | = (no effect) |

---

### QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEMET832, Module 2]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compare FDM with SLA and SLS processes. List at least six comparative points. State two advantages and two limitations of FDM.

**Model Answer:**

**Comparative Table: FDM vs. SLA vs. SLS**

| S.No | Feature | FDM | SLA (Stereolithography) | SLS (Selective Laser Sintering) |
|------|---------|-----|--------------------------|----------------------------------|
| 1 | **Process Type** | Material Extrusion | Vat Photopolymerization | Powder Bed Fusion |
| 2 | **Material State** | Molten thermoplastic filament | Liquid photopolymer resin | Powdered thermoplastic (Nylon PA12) |
| 3 | **Energy Source** | Thermal (heated nozzle) | UV Laser (355–405 nm) | CO₂ Laser (10.6 µm IR) |
| 4 | **Surface Finish** | Poor (visible layers) | Excellent (< 5 µm Ra) | Good (grainy texture) |
| 5 | **Accuracy** | ±0.2 mm (typical) | ±0.1 mm (high) | ±0.3 mm |
| 6 | **Mechanical Strength** | Moderate, anisotropic | Brittle, weak | Strong, isotropic |
| 7 | **Support Structure** | Required for overhangs | Required for overhangs | Not required (powder bed) |
| 8 | **Material Cost** | Low (₹1500–₹3000/kg) | High (₹5000–₹15000/L) | Moderate (₹4000–₹8000/kg) |
| 9 | **Machine Cost** | Low (₹15K–₹5L) | Moderate (₹2L–₹20L) | High (₹30L–₹2Cr) |
| 10 | **Post-Processing** | Minimal | Required (curing, sanding) | Required (powder removal) |
| 11 | **Build Speed** | Slow to Moderate | Fast (cured in seconds) | Moderate |
| 12 | **Typical Materials** | PLA, ABS, PETG, TPU, Nylon | Standard/Tough/Clear Resins | Nylon PA12, TPU, PA11 |

**Two Advantages of FDM:**

1. **Low Cost:** FDM machines are the most affordable AM systems. Desktop FDM printers start at ~₹15,000, and industrial FDM systems are still 5–10× cheaper than comparable SLA/SLS systems. Filament is also inexpensive.

2. **Material Versatility:** FDM supports a wide range of thermoplastics (PLA, ABS, PETG, TPU, Nylon, PC, PEEK), composites (carbon-fiber-filled, wood-filled, metal-filled), and engineering-grade polymers for functional applications.

3. **Ease of Use & Office Safety:** Unlike SLA (toxic resin) or SLS (high-power laser), FDM is safe to operate in office/educational environments. No chemical handling required.

**Two Limitations of FDM:**

1. **Poor Surface Finish:** The layer-by-line deposition produces visible **stair-stepping artifacts** and layer lines, with typical surface roughness of **5–20 µm Ra**, requiring post-processing for aesthetic parts.

2. **Anisotropic Mechanical Properties:** FDM parts are significantly weaker along the Z-axis (build direction) due to weak inter-layer adhesion. Strength in Z is typically **40–60% of X-Y strength**, limiting use for load-bearing applications.

3. **Limited Resolution:** Nozzle diameter restricts the minimum feature size to **~0.4 mm**, much coarser than SLA's **~0.05 mm** laser spot.

4. **Slow Build Speed:** Deposition is point-by-point, making large parts take many hours compared to SLA's volumetric curing.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the various defects encountered in FDM printing. Discuss their causes and remedies.

**Model Answer:**

**Common FDM Defects, Causes, and Remedies:**

| S.No | Defect | Description | Causes | Remedies |
|------|--------|-------------|--------|----------|
| 1 | **Warping** | Corners/edges of the part lift off the bed | High CTE, low bed temp, drafts, no enclosure | Use heated bed, apply brim/raft, enclose printer, use adhesion promoters (glue stick) |
| 2 | **Layer Delamination** | Layers separate from each other | Insufficient nozzle temp, too high print speed, poor inter-layer bonding | Increase nozzle temp, reduce print speed, increase infill |
| 3 | **Stringing/Oozing** | Thin plastic threads between parts of the print | Filament leaks from nozzle during travel moves | Enable retraction, lower nozzle temp, increase travel speed, dry filament |
| 4 | **Under-Extrusion** | Missing material, gaps, weak walls | Clogged nozzle, low temperature, drive gear slippage, partial clog | Clean nozzle, raise temp, adjust extruder E-steps, replace PTFE tube |
| 5 | **Over-Extrusion** | Blobby, rough walls, excess material | Incorrect flow rate, nozzle diameter miscalibration | Reduce flow %, calibrate E-steps, use correct slicer profile |
| 6 | **Elephant's Foot** | First layer bulges outward | First layer too squished, bed too close, bed temp too high | Increase Z-offset, lower bed temp for first layer, use chamfer |
| 7 | **Layer Shift** | Layers misaligned in X-Y | Loose belts, skipped steps, driver overheating | Tighten belts, reduce print speed, add motor cooling, check current |
| 8 | **Z-Banding** | Periodic variation in layer height | Wobbly Z lead screw, inconsistent Z movement | Use dual Z screws, lubricate, switch to belt-driven Z |
| 9 | **Ghosting/Ringing** | Echoes near sharp features | Vibration from rapid acceleration | Reduce acceleration, use input shaping, tighten belts |
| 10 | **Holes & Gaps in Top Layer** | Incomplete top surface | Low infill %, low top layer count, under-extrusion | Increase top layers, increase infill %, calibrate extrusion |
| 11 | **Bubbling/Popping** | Air bubbles in the print | Wet filament, excessive temperature | Dry filament (filament dryer at 50°C for 4–6 hrs), lower temperature |
| 12 | **Clogging** | Extruder stops extruding | Cold nozzle clog, foreign particle, heat creep | Hot pull / cold pull cleaning, replace nozzle, check cooling fan |

**Most Critical Defect for KTU Exam: Warping and Delamination**

Warping is the most common and economically significant FDM defect, particularly for **ABS** and **PC** due to their high coefficient of thermal expansion. The differential cooling between the bottom (cooled by the bed) and the top (still warm) layers creates internal stresses that lift the corners. Solutions include: (1) heated bed at 100–110°C, (2) enclosed chamber at 40–60°C, (3) brim/raft adhesion structures, (4) adhesive agents (PVA glue, ABS juice), and (5) reducing cooling fan speed for warping-prone materials.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse FDM (Stratasys trademark) with FFF (generic term)** — Examiners specifically check whether you know FDM = FFF. Loss of 1 mark.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse nozzle temperature with bed temperature** — Many students swap these. Remember: **Nozzle = melts polymer; Bed = prevents warping.**
> 3. **Always state units with numerical answers** — A calculation without units is considered incomplete. Loss of 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **For FDM vs. SLA/SLS comparisons, mention BOTH advantages and limitations** — One-sided answers are penalized.
> 5. **Do NOT write generic statements like "FDM is good"** — Always quantify: state temperatures, layer heights, dimensions.
> 6. **Block diagrams MUST have labeled components** — Unlabeled diagrams earn zero marks in KTU.
> 7. **Anisotropy explanation is mandatory for strength-related questions** — FDM parts are inherently anisotropic in Z-direction; missing this loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — FDM (KTU 2024 Module 2)**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- ☐ FDM = Material Extrusion Process (ASTM F2792-12a)
- ☐ FDM ≈ FFF (same process, FFF is the non-proprietary term)
- ☐ Invented by S. Scott Crump (1989), Stratasys Inc.
- ☐ Operates on thermal extrusion of thermoplastic filament
- ☐ Filament diameters: 1.75 mm (common) and 2.85 mm

### 🔧 System Components
- ☐ Extruder (Bowden vs Direct Drive)
- ☐ Hot End (heater block + nozzle, 180°C–320°C)
- ☐ Heated Build Platform (50°C–110°C)
- ☐ X-Y Gantry with stepper motors + timing belts
- ☐ Z-axis lead screw(s)
- ☐ Firmware (Marlin/Klipper)

### ⚙️ Key Process Parameters
- ☐ Nozzle Temperature: 180°C–320°C
- ☐ Bed Temperature: 50°C–110°C
- ☐ Layer Height: 0.05–0.4 mm
- ☐ Print Speed: 30–150 mm/s
- ☐ Nozzle Diameter: 0.2–1.0 mm
- ☐ Infill Density: 0%–100%

### 📐 Critical Formulas (must memorize)
- ☐ $Q = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v_n$ (volumetric flow rate)
- ☐ $v_n = (D/d)^2 \cdot v_f$ (velocity amplification)
- ☐ $t_{total} = \frac{H}{h} \cdot \frac{L_{path}}{v_{print}}$
- ☐ $R_a \approx \frac{h}{2} \cos\theta$ (surface roughness)
- ☐ $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ (mass flow rate)

### 🧪 Common Materials
- ☐ **PLA** — 190–220°C, easy, low warp
- ☐ **ABS** — 230–260°C, strong, warps, needs enclosure
- ☐ **PETG** — 220–250°C, tough, chemical resistant
- ☐ **TPU** — 210–230°C, flexible elastomer
- ☐ **Nylon** — 250–280°C, hygroscopic, needs drying
- ☐ **PC** — 260–300°C, very strong, high temperature needed

### ⚠️ Major Defects
- ☐ Warping (high CTE + low bed temp)
- ☐ Delamination (low nozzle temp + high speed)
- ☐ Stringing (no retraction)
- ☐ Under/Over extrusion (calibration issues)
- ☐ Layer shift (mechanical issues)
- ☐ Z-banding (lead screw wobble)
- ☐ Bubbling (wet filament)

### ✅ Advantages
- ☐ Low cost (machine + material)
- ☐ Material versatility
- ☐ Office-safe (no toxic chemicals)
- ☐ Simple workflow
- ☐ Multi-color/material printing possible

### ❌ Limitations
- ☐ Anisotropic (Z-direction weak)
- ☐ Poor surface finish (visible layer lines)
- ☐ Limited resolution (~0.4 mm)
- ☐ Slow build time for large parts
- ☐ Support structures needed for overhangs

### 🆚 FDM vs SLA vs SLS
- ☐ FDM = cheapest, SLA = best finish, SLS = strongest
- ☐ FDM = filament, SLA = liquid resin, SLS = powder
- ☐ FDM = thermal, SLA = UV laser, SLS = IR laser
- ☐ FDM = no support post-processing, SLA = extensive, SLS = powder removal

### 🎯 KTU-Specific Tips
- ☐ Always cite **ASTM F2792-12a** classification in definitions
- ☐ Quote **temperature ranges** in numerical questions
- ☐ Draw **labeled block diagrams** in process questions
- ☐ Mention **anisotropy** whenever discussing strength
- ☐ Distinguish **FDM (Stratasys)** vs **FFF (generic)**
- ☐ Use **precise SI units** (mm, °C, mm/s, kg/m³)
- ☐ Apply **mass conservation** for flow rate derivations
- ☐ Discuss **warpage remedies** (heated bed, enclosure, brim/raft)

---

> **END OF NOTES — FUSED DEPOSITION MODELLING (FDM)**
> **Module 2 | OEMET832 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
