topics.title: Direct Ink Writing (DIW)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET832 - 3D PRINTING AND TOOLING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Direct Ink Writing (DIW)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: DIRECT INK WRITING (DIW)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Direct Ink Writing (DIW)** is a versatile, extrusion-based additive manufacturing (AM) process in which a liquid-phase ink — typically a highly viscous, shear-thinning fluid or paste — is pneumatically, mechanically, or piston-driven through a small-diameter nozzle and deposited along a digitally defined toolpath to construct three-dimensional objects in a layer-by-layer fashion. Unlike Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) which uses molten thermoplastic filaments, DIW operates at (or near) room temperature and can process a much wider range of material chemistries, including colloidal suspensions, ceramic pastes, metal-filled inks, hydrogels, biomaterials, and even food pastes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** DIW is classified under **Material Extrusion (MEX)** in the ISO/ASTM 52900 taxonomy of additive manufacturing processes. It is also commonly referred to as **Robocasting** in the ceramics literature (Cesarano et al., 1998) and **3D Bioprinting** in biomedical applications when the ink is a bio-ink.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cake Icing Perspective

Imagine you are decorating a birthday cake with a piping bag filled with thick icing. When you squeeze the bag, the icing flows smoothly out of the tiny tip. When you stop squeezing, the icing holds its shape and does not collapse under its own weight, allowing you to build a tall, intricate 3D design on the cake.

**DIW works on exactly this principle:**
- The **piping bag** = the syringe / extruder
- The **icing** = the viscoelastic ink
- The **tiny tip** = the deposition nozzle (typically **100 µm – 1 mm** in diameter)
- The **cake** = the build platform, onto which the structure is built layer by layer

The icing must be *flowable* enough to be squeezed out, but *stiff* enough to retain its shape once deposited. This dual behavior is the fundamental design constraint of every DIW ink.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Engineering Trade-off:** The ink must exhibit a **low viscosity under high shear** (inside the nozzle, so it flows) but a **high viscosity (or yield stress) at rest** (after deposition, so it holds shape). This is the hallmark of a **shear-thinning, thixotropic fluid**.

### 1.3 Key Process Metrics (Bolded Standards)

| Parameter | Typical Range / Standard |
|---|---|
| Nozzle diameter | **100 µm – 1.0 mm** |
| Layer height | **50 µm – 500 µm** |
| Print speed (traverse) | **1 – 50 mm/s** |
| Ink viscosity range | **10⁰ – 10⁶ Pa·s** |
| Build envelope | **~ 1 mm³ – 1 m³** (lab to architectural scale) |
| Typical layer resolution | **± 50 µm** (lateral), **± 25 µm** (vertical) |
| Operating temperature | **Room temperature to ~ 80 °C** (no thermal melting) |

### 1.4 Classification Within Material Extrusion Family

> [!NOTE]
> DIW is one of three major sub-classes of Material Extrusion. Understanding the distinction is a **favourite KTU question**:

1. **FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling)** — Uses *molten thermoplastics*; requires heat; uses solid filament feed.
2. **DIW (Direct Ink Writing)** — Uses *viscous liquid inks* (pastes, gels, suspensions); room temperature; uses syringe/piston feed.
3. **Robocasting** — A *sub-class of DIW* specifically for high-solids-loading ceramic colloidal inks (typically > 40 vol% solids).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Principle — How DIW Works

The DIW process can be decomposed into **four sequential physical stages**:

1. **Ink Formulation & Loading** — A shear-thinning, yield-stress fluid (ink) is loaded into a syringe/barrel. The formulation typically contains a binder, solvent, active material (ceramic/metal/polymer particles, cells, etc.), and rheology modifiers.
2. **Pressure-Driven Extrusion** — A controlled pressure (pneumatic, mechanical piston, or screw-driven) is applied, forcing the ink through a micro-nozzle. The ink experiences **high shear rates** ($\dot{\gamma} \approx 10^2 - 10^3 \text{ s}^{-1}$) inside the nozzle.
3. **Filament Deposition** — The extruded filament is deposited onto a moving X-Y stage (or the nozzle moves). The shear rate drops by orders of magnitude ($\dot{\gamma} \approx 10^0 - 10^1 \text{ s}^{-1}$), and the ink's viscosity recovers, "freezing" the filament in place.
4. **Layer Stacking & Curing** — Successive layers are deposited on top of previous ones. Each layer must support the weight of subsequent layers. Solidification occurs via solvent evaporation, UV curing, temperature-induced gelation, or chemical crosslinking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why DIW works at all:** The **rapid recovery of viscosity** after extrusion (a property called **thixotropy**) is the single most important rheological requirement. If the ink takes too long to "set", the structure will collapse.

### 2.2 The Rheology of DIW Inks — A Conceptual Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Behaviour

A **Newtonian fluid** (like water) has a viscosity that is independent of shear rate:

$$\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)

A **non-Newtonian fluid** (like DIW ink) has a viscosity that changes with shear rate. DIW inks are typically **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** — they get *less viscous* as shear rate increases.

#### 2.2.2 The Herschel-Bulkley Model — The DIW Workhorse Equation

Most DIW inks are best described by the **Herschel-Bulkley model**, which combines a yield stress with power-law shear-thinning:

$$\tau = \tau_y + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^n$$

where:
- $\tau_y$ = **yield stress** (Pa) — the minimum stress required to initiate flow
- $K$ = **consistency index** (Pa·sⁿ)
- $n$ = **flow behaviour index** (dimensionless); $n < 1$ for shear-thinning
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** If $\tau_y = 0$, the Herschel-Bulkley model reduces to the **Power Law**. If additionally $n = 1$, it reduces to the **Newtonian model**. This is a frequent 2-mark conceptual question.

#### 2.2.3 Bingham Plastic Model

A simpler alternative, used for many colloidal inks:

$$\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

where $\mu_p$ is the plastic viscosity. This is a special case of Herschel-Bulkley with $n = 1$.

#### 2.2.4 Thixotropy vs. Shear-Thinning

These are often confused in exam answers — **know the difference**:

| Property | Shear-Thinning | Thixotropy |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate (instantaneous) | Viscosity decreases *over time* under constant shear, and recovers *over time* when shear is removed |
| Time dependence | No | Yes |
| DIW relevance | Allows extrusion through the nozzle | Allows shape retention after deposition |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students answer "shear-thinning" when the correct answer is "thixotropy" (or vice versa). The **time-dependence** is the key discriminator.

### 2.3 The Two Design Constraints — A Logical Framework

For a successful DIW print, two competing mechanical requirements must be satisfied:

**Constraint 1: Extrudability** — The ink must flow through the nozzle.

$$\tau_{\text{wall}} = \Delta P \cdot \frac{D}{4L} \geq \tau_y$$

where:
- $\tau_{\text{wall}}$ = shear stress at the nozzle wall
- $\Delta P$ = applied pressure (Pa)
- $D$ = nozzle diameter (m)
- $L$ = nozzle length (m)
- $\tau_y$ = yield stress (Pa)

**Constraint 2: Shape Retention** — The deposited filament must not collapse.

$$\tau_y \geq \rho g h$$

where:
- $\rho$ = ink density (kg/m³)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
- $h$ = height of the printed structure (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Goldilocks Principle:** The yield stress $\tau_y$ must be **high enough** to support the structure (Constraint 2) but **low enough** to allow extrusion at reasonable pressures (Constraint 1). Typical DIW inks have $\tau_y$ in the range of **100 – 1000 Pa**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Typical Values / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newtonian fluid | $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ | $\mu$ in Pa·s |
| 2 | Power-law fluid | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | $n < 1$ shear-thinning |
| 3 | Bingham plastic | $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau_y$ in Pa |
| 4 | **Herschel-Bulkley (most used)** | $\tau = \tau_y + K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | $n < 1$, $K$ in Pa·sⁿ |
| 5 | Extrudability | $\tau_{\text{wall}} \geq \tau_y$ | $\tau_{\text{wall}} = \Delta P \cdot D/4L$ |
| 6 | Shape retention (gravitational) | $\tau_y \geq \rho g h$ | $h$ in metres |
| 7 | Apparent viscosity | $\mu_{app} = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | Pa·s |
| 8 | Shear rate in cylindrical nozzle | $\dot{\gamma}_w = 8 v / D$ | $v$ = avg velocity (m/s) |
| 9 | Volumetric flow rate (Hagen-Poiseuille analogue) | $Q = \frac{\pi D^3}{32 \mu} \left( \Delta P - \frac{4 \tau_y L}{D} \right)$ | m³/s |
| 10 | Print speed – flow rate relation | $v_{print} = Q / (w \cdot h)$ | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Formula #9 is the *Bingham-plastic flow in a pipe* equation. It is the direct analogue of the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** (which assumes Newtonian flow). The $\frac{4 \tau_y L}{D}$ term is the **pressure loss due to the yield stress** — a "plug-flow" effect. The flow only occurs when $\Delta P > \frac{4 \tau_y L}{D}$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | DIW Application | Ink Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Biomedical** | Tissue engineering scaffolds, organ-on-chip | Hydrogels (alginate, gelatin methacryloyl, collagen) |
| **Ceramics** | Functional lattice ceramics, SOFCs | Alumina, zirconia, barium titanate pastes |
| **Electronics** | Printed sensors, antennas, soft robotics | Silver nanoparticle inks, carbon-black/polymer composites |
| **Energy** | Solid-state batteries, supercapacitor electrodes | LiFePO₄, Li₄Ti₅O₁₂, graphene oxide inks |
| **Construction** | Architectural-scale 3D printing | Cementitious mortars, geopolymer pastes |
| **Food** | Custom-shaped confections, personalized nutrition | Chocolate, dough, surimi pastes |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Shear Rate in a Cylindrical Nozzle

**Starting Premise:** For a fully developed, laminar, steady flow of an incompressible fluid in a cylindrical pipe of diameter $D$ and length $L$, we apply a force balance on a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$.

**Step 1 — Force balance on fluid element:**

The pressure force acting on the cross-section must equal the viscous shear force on the cylindrical surface (this is a momentum balance in the axial direction, $z$):

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau(r) \cdot 2\pi r L$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the shear stress distribution:**

Divide both sides by $\pi r$:

$$\Delta P \cdot r = 2 \tau(r) L$$

Solve for $\tau(r)$:

$$\tau(r) = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the maximum shear stress at the wall:**

Setting $r = D/2$ (the nozzle wall):

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **extrudability criterion** (Formula #5 in the cheat sheet) — the wall shear stress must exceed the yield stress $\tau_y$ for flow to initiate.

**Step 4 — Relate shear stress to shear rate via the constitutive model:**

For a **Bingham plastic**, $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$, so:

$$\dot{\gamma}(r) = \frac{\tau(r) - \tau_y}{\mu_p} = \frac{1}{\mu_p} \left( \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L} - \tau_y \right)$$

**Step 5 — Identify the plug-flow region:**

For a Bingham fluid, flow only occurs where $\tau(r) > \tau_y$. There exists a **critical radius** $r_c$ where $\tau(r_c) = \tau_y$:

$$\tau_y = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r_c}{2L}$$

$$\Rightarrow r_c = \frac{2 \tau_y L}{\Delta P}$$

For $0 \leq r \leq r_c$, the fluid moves as a **rigid plug** (no shear, no internal deformation). For $r_c \leq r \leq D/2$, the fluid is sheared.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **plug-flow region** is a key feature of Bingham plastics. It is precisely what allows a ceramic-loaded DIW ink to extrude without the particles segregating — they all move together as a plug in the central region.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Volumetric Flow Rate (Bingham Fluid in a Pipe)

**Step 1 — Write the velocity gradient:**

From Step 4 above:

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \dot{\gamma}(r) = \frac{1}{\mu_p} \left( \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L} - \tau_y \right)$$

**Step 2 — Integrate to get the velocity profile:**

Integrating with respect to $r$ (noting that $v_z = 0$ at the wall $r = D/2$ due to the no-slip condition):

$$v_z(r) = \int_{D/2}^{r} \dot{\gamma}(r') \, dr' = \int_{D/2}^{r} \frac{1}{\mu_p} \left( \frac{\Delta P \cdot r'}{2L} - \tau_y \right) dr'$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$v_z(r) = \frac{1}{\mu_p} \left[ \frac{\Delta P}{4L} \left( r^2 - \frac{D^2}{4} \right) - \tau_y \left( r - \frac{D}{2} \right) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Compute the volumetric flow rate by integrating over the annular area:**

$$Q = \int_{r_c}^{D/2} v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

For the **plug region** ($0 \leq r \leq r_c$), the fluid moves at the constant plug velocity $v_{plug} = v_z(r_c)$:

$$v_{plug} = \frac{1}{\mu_p} \left[ \frac{\Delta P}{4L} \left( r_c^2 - \frac{D^2}{4} \right) - \tau_y \left( r_c - \frac{D}{2} \right) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Final expression for Q (Hagen-Poiseuille analogue for Bingham fluid):**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi D^3}{32 \mu_p} \left( \Delta P - \frac{4 \tau_y L}{D} \right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The term $\frac{4 \tau_y L}{D}$ inside the parentheses is the **yield pressure** — the minimum pressure required to initiate flow. If $\Delta P \leq \frac{4 \tau_y L}{D}$, the fluid will **not** flow. This is the physical manifestation of the **extrudability criterion**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Shape Retention — Maximum Printable Height

**Problem:** A DIW ink has a yield stress $\tau_y$, density $\rho$. We wish to find the maximum height $h_{max}$ of a self-supporting vertical column.

**Step 1 — Consider the stress at the base of the column:**

The bottom layer experiences a **compressive hydrostatic stress** from the weight of all layers above it. For a column of height $h$ and cross-sectional area $A$:

$$\sigma_{base} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\rho g h A}{A} = \rho g h$$

**Step 2 — Apply the failure criterion (Mohr-Coulomb type, in simplest form):**

The structure will not yield (collapse) as long as the gravitational stress does not exceed the yield stress:

$$\rho g h \leq \tau_y$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the maximum height:**

$$\boxed{h_{max} = \frac{\tau_y}{\rho g}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:**

For a typical DIW ceramic ink with $\tau_y = 200 \text{ Pa}$ and $\rho = 2500 \text{ kg/m}^3$:

$$h_{max} = \frac{200}{2500 \times 9.81} = \frac{200}{24525} \approx 0.0082 \text{ m} = 8.2 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** A 200 Pa yield stress can only self-support a column of **~8 mm height**. To print taller structures, you must either **increase $\tau_y$** (by adding more binder or rheology modifier), **decrease $\rho$** (use a lighter filler), or **introduce a fast-setting cure mechanism** (UV, ionic crosslink, solvent flash-off). This is the central reason why multi-material DIW with in-situ UV curing is preferred for tall structures.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (For KTU Exam Practice)

**Problem:** A DIW system uses a ceramic ink with a Bingham-plastic rheology. The yield stress is $\tau_y = 350$ Pa and the plastic viscosity is $\mu_p = 50$ Pa·s. The nozzle has a diameter $D = 0.5$ mm and a length $L = 5$ mm. The applied pressure is $\Delta P = 80$ kPa. The ink density is $\rho = 2200$ kg/m³.

**Find:**
(a) The wall shear stress and the maximum printable height.
(b) The volumetric flow rate.
(c) The required print speed if the line width is $w = 0.6$ mm and the layer height is $h = 0.3$ mm.

**Solution:**

**(a) Wall shear stress and $h_{max}$:**

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4L} = \frac{80{,}000 \times 0.0005}{4 \times 0.005} = \frac{40}{0.02} = 2000 \text{ Pa}$$

Since $\tau_w = 2000 \text{ Pa} > \tau_y = 350 \text{ Pa}$, the ink **will flow**. ✓

$$h_{max} = \frac{\tau_y}{\rho g} = \frac{350}{2200 \times 9.81} = \frac{350}{21582} \approx 0.0162 \text{ m} = 16.2 \text{ mm}$$

**(b) Volumetric flow rate:**

First, compute the yield pressure:

$$\frac{4 \tau_y L}{D} = \frac{4 \times 350 \times 0.005}{0.0005} = \frac{7}{0.0005} = 14{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$

Then:

$$Q = \frac{\pi D^3}{32 \mu_p} \left( \Delta P - \frac{4 \tau_y L}{D} \right) = \frac{\pi \times (0.0005)^3}{32 \times 50} \times (80{,}000 - 14{,}000)$$

$$Q = \frac{3.1416 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-10}}{1600} \times 66{,}000$$

$$Q = 2.454 \times 10^{-13} \times 66{,}000 = 1.62 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 16.2 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**(c) Print speed:**

$$v_{print} = \frac{Q}{w \cdot h} = \frac{1.62 \times 10^{-8}}{0.0006 \times 0.0003} = \frac{1.62 \times 10^{-8}}{1.8 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.09 \text{ m/s} = 90 \text{ mm/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Strategy:** Notice how all three sub-parts use the **same physical parameters** but apply them to different physical sub-problems (extrusion, gravity, kinematics). KTU examiners love to chain related questions this way. Master the underlying physics, not just the formulas.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DIW System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CAD Model STL File] --> B[Slicer Software G-code Generation]
    B --> C[Toolpath Interpreter]
    C --> D[Pressure Controller Pneumatic or Piston]
    D --> E[Ink Reservoir Syringe Barrel]
    E --> F[Micro Nozzle 100 um to 1 mm]
    F --> G[Build Platform XY Stage]
    G --> H[Deposition Layer n]
    H --> I[Curing Module UV Heat Ionic]
    I --> J[Next Layer n+1]
    J --> K[Completed 3D Part]
    K --> L[Post-Processing Sintering Washing]

    subgraph Feedback_Loop
        M[Pressure Sensor] --> N[Closed Loop Controller]
        N --> D
    end

    subgraph Material_Flow
        O[Ink Formulation Particles Binder Solvent] --> E
    end
```

### 4.2 Shear Rate vs. Viscosity — The Shear-Thinning Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shear Rate LOW 0.1 s-1] -->|High Viscosity 10^5 Pa s| B[Resting State After Deposition]
    C[Shear Rate HIGH 1000 s-1] -->|Low Viscosity 1 to 10 Pa s| D[Inside Nozzle During Extrusion]
    B -->|Thixotropic Recovery Over Seconds| E[Shape Retention Holds Form]
    D -->|Continuous Shear Thinning| F[Smooth Extrusion Through Nozzle]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The two states (low shear / high viscosity at rest, and high shear / low viscosity in the nozzle) are the **same ink** under different mechanical conditions. The arrow between them represents the *reversible* rheological transition that makes DIW possible.

### 4.3 Process Workflow — From CAD to Finished Part

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: 3D CAD Model Creation] --> P2[Step 2: Slicing Layer Thickness 50 to 500 um]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Ink Rheology Tuning Yield Stress Viscosity]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Nozzle and Pressure Calibration]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Layer by Layer Deposition]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: In Process Curing UV Heat Solvent Drying]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Part Removal and Drying]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Post Processing Sintering for Ceramics]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Final Densified Part]
```

### 4.4 Material Classification Tree for DIW Inks

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DIW[Direct Ink Writing Inks] --> P[Polymer Based]
    DIW --> C[Ceramic Based]
    DIW --> M[Metal Based]
    DIW --> B[Bio Based]

    P --> P1[Silicone Elastomers]
    P --> P2[UV Curable Acrylates]
    P --> P3[Epoxy Resins]

    C --> C1[Alumina Al2O3]
    C --> C2[Zirconia ZrO2]
    C --> C3[Calcium Phosphate Bioceramics]

    M --> M1[Silver Nanoparticle Inks]
    M --> M2[Copper Inks]
    M --> M3[Tungsten Heavy Alloys]

    B --> B1[Alginate Hydrogels]
    B --> B2[Gelatin Methacryloyl GelMA]
    B --> B3[Collagen and Decellularized ECM]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Examinations:** The Material Classification Tree is a frequent 5-mark question. Be prepared to give at least **two examples per category** with their **typical applications**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
*Define Direct Ink Writing (DIW). How is it fundamentally different from Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Direct Ink Writing (DIW) is an extrusion-based additive manufacturing process in which a viscous, shear-thinning ink is deposited through a micro-nozzle under applied pressure to build 3D parts layer by layer. **(1 mark)**

**Key differences from FDM: (2 marks)**

| Aspect | DIW | FDM |
|---|---|---|
| Material state | Viscous liquid/paste | Molten thermoplastic |
| Operating temperature | Room temperature (or slightly above) | 180 – 250 °C |
| Feedstock | Liquid ink in syringe | Solid filament spool |
| Materials compatible | Ceramics, metals, bio-inks, food, polymers | Mostly thermoplastics (ABS, PLA, PETG) |
| Post-processing | Often required (sintering, curing) | Usually minimal |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
*Explain the role of yield stress in DIW inks. Why is the Herschel-Bulkley model widely used to describe these inks?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Role of yield stress (2 marks):** The yield stress $\tau_y$ is the minimum shear stress required to initiate flow in the ink. In DIW, it serves two competing purposes:
- It must be **low enough** that the applied pressure can push the ink through the nozzle (extrudability).
- It must be **high enough** that the deposited filament does not collapse under its own weight or under the weight of subsequent layers (shape retention).

**Why Herschel-Bulkley (1 mark):** Most DIW inks are **shear-thinning** (viscosity drops with shear rate) AND possess a **yield stress**. The Herschel-Bulkley model $\tau = \tau_y + K\dot{\gamma}^n$ is the only widely used rheological model that captures **both** features simultaneously, making it the most accurate and versatile choice for DIW.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the working principle of a Direct Ink Writing (DIW) system. List the major process parameters that govern print quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DIW ink exhibits Bingham plastic behaviour with $\tau_y = 500$ Pa and $\mu_p = 80$ Pa·s. The nozzle diameter is 0.6 mm, nozzle length is 8 mm, and applied pressure is 100 kPa. Compute: (i) the wall shear stress, (ii) the volumetric flow rate, and (iii) the maximum height of a self-supporting column if the ink density is 2400 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Working Principle (5 marks):** A DIW system consists of **(1)** a syringe/barrel reservoir holding the ink, **(2)** a pressure-generating actuator (pneumatic, piston, or screw), **(3)** a micro-nozzle of diameter 100 µm – 1 mm, **(4)** a computer-controlled X-Y-Z motion stage, and **(5)** a curing module (UV lamp, heater, or mist chamber). The ink is extruded under controlled pressure as a continuous filament, which is deposited on the build platform following a sliced CAD toolpath. The ink's shear-thinning rheology enables smooth extrusion at high shear (inside the nozzle) and rapid shape retention at low shear (after deposition). Layers are stacked sequentially and cured (partially or fully) before the next layer is added. For ceramic and metal inks, the green (as-printed) part is then subjected to post-processing such as sintering to achieve full densification.

**Key Process Parameters (2 marks):**
1. **Nozzle diameter** — determines line width and resolution.
2. **Layer height** — affects adhesion between layers and surface finish.
3. **Print speed** — must be matched to the extrusion flow rate.
4. **Applied pressure** — controls the volumetric flow rate.
5. **Ink rheology** — yield stress, viscosity, thixotropy.
6. **Cure/solidification rate** — must be fast enough for shape retention.
7. **Nozzle standoff distance** — gap between nozzle tip and previous layer.

> [!NOTE]
> The 5-mark working principle section is **expected to include a labelled diagram**. In the KTU exam, draw the schematic: syringe → actuator → nozzle → deposited filament → build platform. Label each component.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Given:** $\tau_y = 500$ Pa, $\mu_p = 80$ Pa·s, $D = 0.6 \text{ mm} = 6 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $L = 8 \text{ mm} = 8 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\Delta P = 100 \text{ kPa} = 10^5$ Pa, $\rho = 2400 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**(i) Wall shear stress [2 marks]:**

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4L} = \frac{10^5 \times 6 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 8 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{60}{0.032} = 1875 \text{ Pa}$$

Since $\tau_w = 1875 \text{ Pa} > \tau_y = 500 \text{ Pa}$, the ink **will extrude**. ✓

**(ii) Volumetric flow rate [3 marks]:**

First, compute the yield pressure:

$$\frac{4 \tau_y L}{D} = \frac{4 \times 500 \times 8 \times 10^{-3}}{6 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{16}{6 \times 10^{-4}} = 26{,}667 \text{ Pa}$$

Then:

$$Q = \frac{\pi D^3}{32 \mu_p} \left( \Delta P - \frac{4 \tau_y L}{D} \right) = \frac{\pi \times (6 \times 10^{-4})^3}{32 \times 80} \times (10^5 - 26{,}667)$$

$$Q = \frac{3.1416 \times 2.16 \times 10^{-10}}{2560} \times 73{,}333$$

$$Q = 2.651 \times 10^{-13} \times 73{,}333 = 1.944 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 19.44 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**(iii) Maximum self-supporting height [2 marks]:**

$$h_{max} = \frac{\tau_y}{\rho g} = \frac{500}{2400 \times 9.81} = \frac{500}{23{,}544} = 0.0212 \text{ m} = 21.2 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Unit conversion** is the #1 source of mark loss. Many students forget to convert mm → m. Always state units explicitly.
> 2. Students often write $\Delta P$ directly in the flow equation **without subtracting the yield pressure** $\frac{4\tau_y L}{D}$. This gives a wildly incorrect Q. Always check: if your answer is "too large", you probably forgot the yield correction.
> 3. Don't confuse $g = 9.81$ m/s² (gravitational) with $g = 9.81$ cm/s². Use SI units **consistently**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the rheological requirements of a DIW ink. Discuss why shear-thinning and thixotropy are essential, with a clear distinction between the two. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare DIW with other material extrusion processes (FDM, inkjet-based bioprinting, and screw extrusion). Provide two distinct advantages and two limitations of DIW relative to FDM. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

A successful DIW ink must simultaneously satisfy **three rheological requirements (6 marks):**

**1. Shear-thinning (Pseudoplastic behaviour) — [2 marks]**
Shear-thinning means the ink's viscosity decreases as the shear rate increases. Inside the nozzle, shear rates are extremely high ($\sim 10^2 - 10^3$ s⁻¹), and the ink must become fluid enough to flow easily. Once deposited, the shear rate drops to near-zero, and the viscosity must increase to support the structure. The flow behaviour index $n$ in the Herschel-Bulkley model should be **less than 1** (typically 0.2 – 0.5) for good DIW inks.

**2. Yield stress — [2 marks]**
A finite yield stress $\tau_y > 0$ is required to prevent the deposited filament from flowing under its own weight. The yield stress must be high enough to satisfy $\tau_y \geq \rho g h$ for the desired print height, but low enough to allow extrusion at practical pressures.

**3. Thixotropy — [2 marks]**
Thixotropy is the **time-dependent** recovery of viscosity after shear is removed. Even if the ink has the right yield stress and shear-thinning index, it may still slump if the recovery is too slow. A fast thixotropic recovery (typically within 0.1 – 1 second) ensures that the deposited filament "freezes" almost immediately, allowing the next layer to be built on top.

**Distinction between shear-thinning and thixotropy [1 mark]:**
Shear-thinning is an **instantaneous** property — viscosity depends only on the *current* shear rate. Thixotropy is a **time-dependent** property — viscosity depends on the *history* of shear. An ink can be shear-thinning without being thixotropic, but for high-quality DIW, **both** are required.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | DIW | FDM | Inkjet Bioprinting | Screw Extrusion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Material state | Viscous paste/ink | Molten thermoplastic | Low-viscosity bio-ink | High-viscosity paste |
| Temperature | Room temp | 180 – 250 °C | Room temp | Room temp |
| Resolution | 100 µm – 1 mm | 100 – 300 µm | 10 – 50 µm | 500 µm – 2 mm |
| Cell-friendly? | Moderate | No (high T) | Yes (low shear) | Limited (high shear) |
| Viscosity range | 10⁰ – 10⁶ Pa·s | N/A (molten) | 0.001 – 0.1 Pa·s | 10² – 10⁵ Pa·s |
| Multi-material | Easy (syringe swap) | Hard | Easy (multi-nozzle) | Moderate |

**Two Advantages of DIW over FDM (2 marks):**
1. **Material versatility** — DIW can process ceramics, metals, hydrogels, food, and live cells, whereas FDM is essentially limited to thermoplastics.
2. **Room-temperature operation** — DIW does not require heating the material to its melting point, making it suitable for temperature-sensitive biomolecules, drugs, and living cells.

**Two Limitations of DIW relative to FDM (1 mark):**
1. **Lower part density / strength** — As-printed DIW parts often have higher porosity and require post-processing (e.g., sintering), whereas FDM parts are typically near-full density as printed.
2. **Smaller build volumes and slower speeds** — DIW is generally slower and limited to smaller build envelopes compared to industrial FDM systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. The "advantages vs. limitations" sub-question expects a **balanced argument**. Do not list 5 advantages and 0 limitations — that signals lack of depth.
> 2. When comparing DIW with FDM, **always mention temperature** as the differentiator. It is the most defensible exam answer.
> 3. For the thixotropy vs. shear-thinning question, the keyword "**time**" is the single most important differentiator. Use it explicitly in your answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DIW** is a **material extrusion** AM process that uses **viscous, shear-thinning inks** deposited at (or near) **room temperature**.
- The **Herschel-Bulkley model** $\tau = \tau_y + K\dot{\gamma}^n$ is the **primary rheological descriptor** for DIW inks. It captures both the yield stress and shear-thinning behaviour.
- **Two design constraints** must be satisfied: (1) **Extrudability** — $\tau_w \geq \tau_y$ at the nozzle wall, and (2) **Shape retention** — $\tau_y \geq \rho g h$ for the printed structure.
- The **plug-flow region** in Bingham-plastic pipe flow is a unique feature: the ink core moves as a rigid plug, which prevents particle segregation in highly filled ceramic inks.
- **Thixotropy** (time-dependent recovery) is **distinct** from **shear-thinning** (instantaneous). DIW needs both. **Thixotropy** is what gives DIW its "self-healing" or "self-supporting" property after deposition.
- Typical DIW inks have $\tau_y$ in the range of **100 – 1000 Pa**, viscosities from **1 to 10⁶ Pa·s**, and are processed through nozzles of **100 µm – 1 mm** diameter.
- DIW is **NOT** limited to polymers: it processes **ceramics, metals, hydrogels, food, and live cells** — a versatility unmatched by FDM.
- **Nozzle diameter $D$** is the single most influential geometric parameter — it controls the **shear rate** ($\dot{\gamma} \sim 8v/D$), the **line width**, and the **resolution**.
- For **ceramic DIW** (robocasting), the as-printed "green" part is **sintered** at high temperature to achieve full density. The green density and sintering shrinkage are critical design considerations.
- The **volumetric flow rate** for a Bingham fluid is $Q = \frac{\pi D^3}{32 \mu_p} \left( \Delta P - \frac{4\tau_y L}{D} \right)$ — do not forget to **subtract the yield-pressure term**.
- The **maximum printable height** is $h_{max} = \tau_y / (\rho g)$. This is why tall DIW structures need either a high $\tau_y$ or in-situ curing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra for DIW:**
> 1. **Rheology is everything** — if you understand the Herschel-Bulkley model, you understand DIW.
> 2. **Two competing constraints** — extrudability vs. shape retention. The yield stress is the dial that must be tuned between them.
> 3. **Material versatility** is DIW's biggest strength — remember at least 4 application domains.
> 4. **Thixotropy ≠ Shear-thinning** — this distinction is tested in nearly every KTU exam on DIW.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle, Materials, Process Parameters, Advantages and Applications of: Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)
Selective Laser Melting (SLM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET832 - 3D PRINTING AND TOOLING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selective Laser Melting (SLM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** is a Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) based Additive Manufacturing (AM) process in which a high-energy-density **fiber laser** is used to **fully melt and fuse** metallic powder particles layer-by-layer inside a controlled, **inert atmosphere chamber** (typically argon or nitrogen), producing near-fully-dense (theoretical density > 99%) functional metal parts directly from a 3D CAD model.

SLM is governed by **ISO/ASTM 52900** standard under the classification **"Powder Bed Fusion – Laser Metal Fusion (PBF-LB/M)"**. It belongs to the broader family of metal additive manufacturing technologies and is distinct from DMLS (Direct Metal Laser Sintering) due to its ability to achieve complete melting rather than solid-state sintering.

### Key Terminology
- **PBF (Powder Bed Fusion):** A category of AM where a thin layer of powder is spread and selectively fused.
- **Build Chamber:** The sealed, gas-purged environment where melting occurs.
- **Build Platform:** The substrate on which the part is built, which descends after each layer.
- **Recoater Blade / Roller:** Mechanism that spreads a fresh, uniform layer of powder.
- **Full Melting:** Complete phase transition of metal powder from solid to liquid, eliminating porosity.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "Microscopic Welding by a Tractor-Mounted Laser"**
>
> Imagine a **highly precise, automated welder** moving over a sandbox of metal dust. Instead of using a traditional welding torch, it uses a **concentrated beam of laser light** so intense that it instantly turns the metal dust into a tiny puddle of molten liquid. Once that puddle solidifies, it becomes a solid layer of metal. Then, a flat blade pushes a fresh layer of metal dust on top, and the laser welds this new layer to the previous one. Repeat this **thousands of times**, and you get a fully formed, dense metal part — almost as if it was carved from a solid block of steel, but with intricate internal geometries impossible to achieve by traditional machining.

### Why SLM Matters in Engineering
- **Design Freedom:** Enables lattice structures, internal cooling channels, and topology-optimized parts.
- **Material Efficiency:** Buy-to-fly ratio improves drastically (often < 2:1 vs. 20:1 in machining).
- **Functional Prototypes:** Parts are not just prototypes — they are end-use production parts.
- **Lead Time Reduction:** Complex assemblies consolidated into single printed parts.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Metrics in SLM:**
> - **Laser Power (P):** Typically **200 W – 1000 W** (Yb-fiber lasers, wavelength ~**1070 nm**)
> - **Spot Size:** **50 µm – 200 µm**
> - **Layer Thickness (t):** **20 µm – 100 µm**
> - **Chamber Oxygen Level:** **< 50 ppm (often < 10 ppm)** to prevent oxidation
> - **Build Envelope:** Up to **800 mm × 400 mm × 500 mm** (industrial systems)
> - **Build Plate Pre-heat:** **80 °C – 200 °C**
> - **Achievable Density:** **> 99.5%** of theoretical (wrought-equivalent mechanical properties)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle – Step-by-Step Logic

The SLM process operates on a **recurrent, cyclic layer-addition principle**. Each cycle consists of the following sequenced operations:

### Step 1: 3D Model Preparation
- A CAD model is created in software (SolidWorks, CATIA, Fusion 360).
- The model is exported as an **STL file** (Standard Tessellation Language).
- STL is imported into a **slicer software** (e.g., Magics, Materialise, EOSPRINT, PreForm).
- Slicer slices the model into 2D horizontal layers of defined thickness **t** (typically 30–40 µm).

### Step 2: Build Chamber Preparation
- The build platform is mounted and **levelled**.
- Powder reservoirs are filled with **gas-atomized, spherical metal powder** (particle size **15–45 µm**).
- The chamber is **sealed and purged with inert gas** (Argon preferred) until O₂ concentration is below threshold.
- Pre-heating of the build platform begins.

### Step 3: Powder Deposition
- The **recoater blade/roller** moves across the build platform, depositing a uniform powder layer of thickness **t** from the supply cylinder to the build cylinder.
- A counter-roller system ensures a smooth, dense, and uniform powder bed.

### Step 4: Laser Melting (Core Operation)
- A **fiber laser** (wavelength **~1070 nm**, well-absorbed by metals) is steered by **galvanometer scanners** and focused through **F-theta lenses** onto the powder bed.
- The laser selectively scans the cross-section of the part, following the **scan strategy** (zig-zag, chessboard, contour-hatch, island scanning).
- The high energy density causes **melting, wetting, and metallurgical bonding** with the previously solidified layer.
- Surrounding powder remains unmelted and acts as a **support medium**.

### Step 5: Layer Solidification
- The molten pool **solidifies rapidly** (cooling rates ~10³–10⁶ K/s), forming a fine, directional **microstructure**.
- Heat dissipates through the substrate and surrounding powder via conduction.

### Step 6: Platform Lowering & Recoating
- The build platform **descends by exactly one layer thickness (t)**.
- A new powder layer is spread.
- The process repeats until the entire part is built.

### Step 7: Post-Processing
- The build chamber cools down (often under protective gas).
- **Powder removal:** Unmelted powder is removed via brushing, vibrating, or sandblasting.
- **Part detachment:** Parts are cut from the build plate using **Wire EDM, bandsaw, or CNC milling**.
- Optional: **Heat treatment** (stress relief, HIP — Hot Isostatic Pressing), **surface finishing** (polishing, bead blasting, machining).

---

## 2.2 Critical Process Parameters (The "Big Four")

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect |
|-----------|--------|---------------|--------|
| **Laser Power** | $P$ | 200–1000 W | Higher → deeper melt pool, more energy |
| **Scan Speed** | $v$ | 200–2000 mm/s | Higher → less energy, possible lack of fusion |
| **Hatch Spacing** | $h$ | 50–200 µm | Distance between adjacent scan tracks |
| **Layer Thickness** | $t$ | 20–100 µm | Vertical resolution |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for SLM Examinations**

| # | Formula | Description | Variables | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | $\displaystyle E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** — most important SLM parameter | $P$ = Power, $v$ = Scan speed, $h$ = Hatch spacing, $t$ = Layer thickness | $\text{J/mm}^3$ |
| 2 | $\displaystyle E_a = \frac{P}{v \cdot h}$ | **Areal Energy Density** | Energy per unit area | $\text{J/mm}^2$ |
| 3 | $\displaystyle E_l = \frac{P}{v}$ | **Linear Energy Density** | Energy per unit length | $\text{J/mm}$ |
| 4 | $\displaystyle \text{Density}_{\%} = \frac{\rho_{\text{SLM}}}{\rho_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | **Relative density** of printed part | $\rho$ = Density | % |
| 5 | $\displaystyle t_{\text{build}} = \frac{n \cdot t}{\eta_{\text{avg}}}$ | Approximate **build time per layer** | $n$ = No. of layers, $\eta$ = Process efficiency factor | hours |
| 6 | $\displaystyle R_{\text{build}} = v \cdot h \cdot t \cdot \rho$ | **Volumetric build rate** | Material deposition rate | $\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** is the single most important quality-controlling parameter. If $E_v < 40 \text{ J/mm}^3$, expect **lack of fusion porosity**; if $E_v > 120 \text{ J/mm}^3$, expect **keyhole porosity** and **vaporization**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** GE Aviation prints fuel nozzle tips for LEAP engines (consolidates 18 parts into 1).
- **Medical:** Patient-specific **titanium cranial implants** and **orthopedic acetabular cups**.
- **Automotive:** Bugatti's **8-blade titanium brake caliper** for Chiron (functional part).
- **Tooling:** Conformal cooling channels in **injection mold inserts**.
- **Energy:** Gas turbine blades with internal lattice cooling (Siemens, GE).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Volumetric Energy Density (VED)

The Volumetric Energy Density quantifies the laser energy delivered per unit volume of powder. It is derived by considering the **melt pool volume** swept by the laser.

### Step 1: Energy Delivered Along a Scan Track
When the laser moves along a single scan track of length $L$ at scan speed $v$, the time of interaction is:

$$
t_{\text{interaction}} = \frac{L}{v}
$$

The total energy delivered over this length at laser power $P$ is:

$$
E_{\text{delivered}} = P \cdot t_{\text{interaction}} = P \cdot \frac{L}{v}
$$

### Step 2: Volume of the Melted Region Per Track
Each scan track melts a volume defined by:
- Length: $L$
- Width: equal to hatch spacing $h$ (since adjacent tracks overlap to fill the cross-section)
- Depth: equal to layer thickness $t$

Therefore, the volume of material melted per track is:

$$
V_{\text{melted}} = L \cdot h \cdot t
$$

### Step 3: Energy Per Unit Volume
Dividing total delivered energy by the volume melted:

$$
E_v = \frac{E_{\text{delivered}}}{V_{\text{melted}}} = \frac{P \cdot \dfrac{L}{v}}{L \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

The variable $L$ cancels out (the result is independent of scan track length):

$$
E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

### Step 4: Units Verification
$$
\frac{\text{[W]}}{\text{[mm/s]} \cdot \text{[mm]} \cdot \text{[mm]}} = \frac{\text{J/s}}{\text{mm}^3/\text{s}} = \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3}
$$

The unit $\text{J/mm}^3$ is confirmed. This is the **standard KTU board-exam derivation** expected in 14-mark questions.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An SLM machine is processing **316L stainless steel powder** with the following parameters:
- Laser Power: $P = 400 \text{ W}$
- Scan Speed: $v = 1000 \text{ mm/s}$
- Hatch Spacing: $h = 0.1 \text{ mm}$
- Layer Thickness: $t = 0.04 \text{ mm}$

**Find:** (a) Volumetric Energy Density (b) Comment on expected porosity type.

### Solution:

**(a) Computing VED:**

$$
E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
E_v = \frac{400 \text{ W}}{1000 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.1 \text{ mm} \times 0.04 \text{ mm}}
$$

$$
E_v = \frac{400}{1000 \times 0.004}
$$

$$
E_v = \frac{400}{4.0}
$$

$$
E_v = 100 \text{ J/mm}^3
$$

**(b) Comment on Porosity:**

> [!NOTE]
> For **316L stainless steel**, the optimum VED is **60–100 J/mm}^3$. A value of 100 J/mm}^3$ lies at the **upper threshold**, indicating the part will have:
> - **Low lack-of-fusion porosity** (sufficient melting).
> - **Possible keyhole porosity** (over-melting).
> - **Optimal density achievable** with scan strategy optimization (e.g., rotating island pattern).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Process Parameters vs. Defects

| Defect Type | Cause | VED Trend | Visual Signature |
|-------------|-------|-----------|------------------|
| **Lack of Fusion** | Insufficient energy / high scan speed | $E_v < 40$ J/mm³ | Irregular, large pores (10–100 µm) |
| **Keyhole Porosity** | Excessive energy / low scan speed | $E_v > 120$ J/mm³ | Spherical gas pockets (deep melt pool collapses) |
| **Ball Formation** | Scan speed too high → melt pool breaks | High $v$ | Rough, discontinuous tracks |
| **Delamination** | Poor inter-layer bonding | Low $E_v$ or contamination | Layer separation |
| **Cracking** | High thermal gradient | Depends on material | Hot cracks along grain boundaries |
| **Oxidation** | Inadequate inert gas | Independent | Discoloration, brittle oxide layer |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of SLM System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CAD Model] --> B[STL Export]
    B --> C[Slicing Software]
    C --> D[2D Layer Cross Sections]
    D --> E[SLM Control Software]
    E --> F[Inert Gas Purging Unit]
    F --> G[Build Chamber Sealed]
    G --> H[Powder Recoater Blade]
    H --> I[Powder Bed Layer Deposited]
    I --> J[Galvanometer Scanner Steers Laser]
    J --> K[Fiber Laser 1070nm Activated]
    K --> L[Selective Melting of Cross Section]
    L --> M[Melt Pool Solidification]
    M --> N{All Layers Done?}
    N -->|No| O[Build Platform Lowers by Layer Thickness t]
    O --> H
    N -->|Yes| P[Cool Down Phase]
    P --> Q[Powder Removal Brushing]
    Q --> R[Part Detachment Wire EDM]
    R --> S[Post Processing Heat Treatment HIP]
    S --> T[Finished Metal Part]
```

## 4.2 Subsystem Block Diagram of SLM Machine

```mermaid

        L1[Yb Fiber Laser Source]
        L2[Beam Expander]

    end

    C2 --> C3

```

```mermaid

    S5 --> S6[Melt Pool Solidifies]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Selective Laser Melting (SLM). Mention any two metals commonly processed by SLM.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 Marks):** Selective Laser Melting (SLM) is an additive manufacturing process under Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) in which a high-power fiber laser is used to **fully melt** metallic powder particles layer-by-layer inside an **inert atmosphere**, producing near-fully-dense functional metal parts directly from a 3D CAD model.
>
> **Two Metals (1 Mark):** (i) Stainless Steel 316L, (ii) Titanium Ti-6Al-4V, (iii) Inconel 718, (iv) AlSi10Mg.
>
> *(Any two valid metals = 1 mark)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**List any four process parameters of SLM that affect the part density.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. Laser Power ($P$)
2. Scan Speed ($v$)
3. Hatch Spacing ($h$)
4. Layer Thickness ($t$)
5. *Bonus:* Scan Strategy (Chessboard, Island, Contour)

*(Any four with brief explanation = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **working principle of Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the inert atmosphere. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** and explain how it affects the **density and defects** of SLM-processed parts. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Working Principle of SLM:**

1. **(1 Mark) CAD to STL:** A 3D CAD model is converted to STL format and sliced into 2D horizontal layers (thickness $t = 20$–$100 \text{ µm}$).

2. **(1 Mark) Chamber Preparation:** The build chamber is sealed and purged with **Argon gas** to bring oxygen concentration below **50 ppm** (often < 10 ppm). Build platform is pre-heated to **80–200 °C** to reduce thermal gradients.

3. **(1 Mark) Powder Deposition:** A **recoater blade/roller** spreads a thin, uniform layer of gas-atomized spherical metal powder (particle size **15–45 µm**) onto the build platform.

4. **(1.5 Marks) Laser Melting:** A **Yb-fiber laser** (wavelength **~1070 nm**) is steered by **galvanometer scanners** and focused via **F-theta lens** onto the powder bed. The laser energy fully melts the powder particles, creating a small **melt pool** that fuses with the previously solidified layer.

5. **(1 Mark) Solidification & Layering:** The melt pool rapidly solidifies (cooling rates $10^3$–$10^6 \text{ K/s}$), forming a fine, directional microstructure. The build platform descends by $t$, a new powder layer is spread, and the process repeats.

6. **(1.5 Marks) Role of Inert Atmosphere:** Inert gas (Argon preferred over Nitrogen for reactive metals like Ti) serves three critical functions:
   - Prevents **oxidation** of molten metal.
   - Removes **metal vapor plume** from the laser interaction zone.
   - Maintains **thermal stability** and uniform cooling.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to SECTION 4.2)*

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation of VED:**

For a laser of power $P$ scanning a track of length $L$ at speed $v$:

$$
t_{\text{interaction}} = \frac{L}{v}
$$

$$
E_{\text{delivered}} = P \cdot t_{\text{interaction}} = \frac{P \cdot L}{v}
$$

The volume melted per track is:

$$
V = L \cdot h \cdot t
$$

Therefore, the Volumetric Energy Density is:

$$
E_v = \frac{E_{\text{delivered}}}{V} = \frac{\dfrac{P \cdot L}{v}}{L \cdot h \cdot t} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

Units: $\text{J/mm}^3$.

**Effect on Density and Defects (4 Marks):**

- **$E_v < 40 \text{ J/mm}^3$** (Low Energy): Insufficient melting → **Lack of Fusion porosity** → Density drops below 95%.
- **$E_v = 60$–$100 \text{ J/mm}^3$** (Optimum, e.g., 316L steel): Adequate melting → Density > 99.5%, minimal defects.
- **$E_v > 120 \text{ J/mm}^3$** (Excess Energy): Vaporization of metal → **Keyhole porosity**, balling, surface roughness.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare **SLM with DMLS** (Direct Metal Laser Sintering) and **EBM** (Electron Beam Melting) based on energy source, atmosphere, materials, and accuracy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **applications of SLM** in aerospace, medical, and tooling industries with specific part examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

| Feature | SLM | DMLS | EBM |
|---------|-----|------|-----|
| **Energy Source** | Fiber Laser (1070 nm) | Fiber Laser | Electron Beam (vacuum) |
| **Atmosphere** | Inert gas (Ar) | Inert gas (N₂ or Ar) | Vacuum |
| **Melting Type** | Full melting | Solid-state sintering + partial melting | Full melting |
| **Materials** | All metals (Ti, Al, SS, Inconel) | Limited alloys (originally steel, Co-Cr) | Ti alloys, conductive metals |
| **Density** | > 99.5% | 90–95% (porous in early versions) | > 99% |
| **Surface Finish** | Good (Ra ~10–20 µm) | Moderate | Rougher (powder sintering effect) |
| **Residual Stress** | High (requires HIP) | Lower | Lower (high pre-heat ~700°C) |
| **Build Speed** | Moderate | Moderate | Fast (high power EB) |
| **Cooling Rate** | $10^3$–$10^6$ K/s | Slower | Slower (high chamber temp) |

*(Full table with brief justification = 7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Aerospace (2.5 Marks):**
- GE LEAP engine fuel nozzle: **18 parts consolidated into 1**, 25% weight reduction.
- Airbus A350 cabin bracket pylon: Topology-optimized titanium part, **30% lighter**.
- Rocket engine injectors (SpaceX): Internal cooling channels impossible by traditional methods.

**2. Medical (2.5 Marks):**
- **Cranial implants:** Patient-specific Ti-6Al-4V skull plates from CT scan data.
- **Dental crowns and bridges:** Co-Cr frameworks.
- **Orthopedic acetabular cups:** Porous lattice surfaces for bone ingrowth.

**3. Tooling (2 Marks):**
- **Injection mold inserts** with **conformal cooling channels** (follows part contour) → 40% reduction in cycle time, uniform cooling.
- **Die-casting tooling** with internal cooling networks.
- **Hot stamping dies** with optimized cooling for high-strength steel parts.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SLM with DMLS (–2 marks):** SLM achieves *full melting*; DMLS is *sintering-assisted*. This is a frequently tested distinction.
> 2. **Wrong Units in VED Formula (–1 mark):** Writing $P/(v \cdot h)$ instead of $P/(v \cdot h \cdot t)$ loses the unit consistency ($\text{J/mm}^3$).
> 3. **Forgetting Inert Gas Justification (–1 mark):** Merely stating "argon gas is used" without explaining *why* (oxidation prevention, plume removal) costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Missing Cooling Rate in Microstructure Discussion (–1 mark):** $10^3$–$10^6$ K/s cooling produces fine dendritic microstructures — examiners expect this number.
> 5. **Insufficient Block Diagram (–1 to –2 marks):** A vague box-and-line diagram is insufficient. Label **laser, scanner, recoater, build platform, powder bed, and gas flow**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **SLM** = Powder Bed Fusion + **Full Melting** + **Fiber Laser** + **Inert Atmosphere** (Ar)
> - **Standard:** ISO/ASTM 52900 — PBF-LB/M
> - **Wavelength:** **1070 nm** (Yb-fiber laser, well-absorbed by metals)
> - **Oxygen Limit:** < 50 ppm (often < 10 ppm)
> - **Layer Thickness:** 20–100 µm
> - **Hatch Spacing:** 50–200 µm
> - **Powder Size:** 15–45 µm (gas-atomized, spherical)
> - **VED Formula:** $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ in $\text{J/mm}^3$
> - **Optimum VED for 316L:** 60–100 J/mm³
> - **Low VED → Lack of Fusion; High VED → Keyhole Porosity**
> - **Cooling Rate:** $10^3$–$10^6$ K/s → fine dendritic microstructure
> - **Key Difference vs. DMLS:** SLM = full melt; DMLS = sinter-assisted
> - **Key Difference vs. EBM:** SLM uses laser + Ar; EBM uses electron beam + vacuum
> - **Famous Parts:** GE LEAP fuel nozzle, Bugatti Ti brake caliper, conformal cooling molds
> - **Pre-heat Plate:** 80–200°C (SLM) vs. ~700°C (EBM)
> - **Post-Processing:** Powder removal → Wire EDM detachment → HIP → Surface finishing
> - **Density Achieved:** > 99.5% (near-wrought properties)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET832 - 3D PRINTING AND TOOLING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electron Beam Melting (EBM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ MODULE 3: ELECTRON BEAM MELTING (EBM)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electron Beam Melting (EBM)** is a high-vacuum, powder-bed fusion (PBF) additive manufacturing process in which a focused, high-velocity **electron beam** is used as the thermal energy source to selectively melt and fuse successive layers of metallic powder, typically in a **vacuum environment of 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ mbar** at an **accelerating voltage of 60 kV**, producing near-net-shape, fully dense metal parts with superior mechanical properties, particularly for reactive metals such as titanium.

EBM belongs to the **ASTM F2792-12a** classification category of **"Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)"** processes, where the energy source is a **kinetic-energy–to-thermal-energy conversion** mechanism rather than photons (laser).

**Standard Key Process Metrics (Industry Norms):**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Accelerating Voltage | **60 kV** |
| Beam Current | 1 – 50 mA (variable) |
| Beam Power (P) | **0.5 – 5 kW** |
| Vacuum Level | **10⁻⁴ – 10⁻⁵ mbar** |
| Build Chamber Pre-heat | **670 – 720 °C** (for Ti-6Al-4V) |
| Spot Diameter | **~100 – 200 μm** |
| Layer Thickness (t) | 50 – 100 μm |
| Scan Speed (v) | up to **10⁴ mm/s** |
| Hatch Spacing (h) | 100 – 200 μm |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: "A Lightning-Controlled Welding Pen in a Sealed Spaceship"** ⚡
>
> Imagine a **high-altitude welding torch** operating inside a **sealed, airless spaceship**. Instead of a flame, an invisible beam of *electrons* (negatively charged particles) is shot from an **electron gun** at speeds approaching **half the speed of light**. Because air would deflect the electrons, the entire chamber is evacuated to a high vacuum — just like outer space. Two invisible **electromagnetic "magnifying glasses"** (magnetic deflection coils) steer the beam thousands of times per second across a thin bed of metal powder, melting it precisely. The platform lowers, a fresh layer of powder is spread, and the process repeats — building the part layer by layer, almost like **drawing a sculpture in 3D with controlled lightning**.

### 1.3 Why EBM Exists — The Driving Problem

Conventional laser-based PBF (SLM/DMLS) struggles with:
- **Reactive metals** (Titanium, Niobium, Tantalum) that absorb oxygen/nitrogen in the molten state.
- **Residual stresses** due to high thermal gradients.
- **Slow build rates** for large aerospace components.

EBM directly addresses these by using a **vacuum + high-temperature preheat + high-power beam**, making it the **gold standard for aerospace Ti-6Al-4V orthopaedic and structural components**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** *EBM is the only commercial metal AM process that operates under high vacuum, distinguishing it fundamentally from Selective Laser Melting (SLM), which operates under inert gas (Argon/Nitrogen) shielding.*

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Electron Beam Generation

The electron beam is generated by the **thermionic emission** principle:

1. A **tungsten filament cathode** is heated to ~2400 °C by passing a current through it.
2. Electrons at the surface gain enough thermal energy to overcome the **work function (φ ≈ 4.5 eV for W)**.
3. A strong electric field (accelerating voltage, V) extracts and accelerates these electrons toward the workpiece (anode).
4. The kinetic energy acquired by each electron is:

$$E_{k} = e \cdot V$$

where *e* is the electronic charge (1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C) and *V* is the accelerating potential in volts.

**Power delivered by the beam:**

$$P_{beam} = V \cdot I_{beam}$$

where $I_{beam}$ is the beam current (typically 1 – 50 mA).

---

## 2.2 Electron–Matter Interaction Mechanism

When high-energy electrons strike the powder bed:

- They penetrate the surface to a depth of a few micrometers.
- Kinetic energy is dissipated via **electron–phonon collisions**, generating intense local heat.
- This raises local temperature above the melting point almost instantaneously.

**The "Why" of Vacuum — Three Reasons:**

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Beam Propagation** | Electrons would scatter off air molecules, losing focus and energy over a few cm. |
| **Oxidation Prevention** | Ti, Al, Nb oxidize at >400 °C; vacuum eliminates O₂ and N₂. |
| **Cathode Protection** | The hot tungsten filament would burn out instantly in air. |

---

## 2.3 The Magnetic Deflection System

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** EBM uses **no moving mirrors or galvanometers** to steer the beam. Instead, two pairs of **electromagnetic deflection coils** (X and Y axes) act like invisible lenses, bending the electron path via the **Lorentz force** (F = qv × B).

This enables **scan speeds of up to 10,000 mm/s**, which is roughly **10–100× faster than laser-based systems**, making EBM the fastest PBF process per unit volume.

---

## 2.4 Multi-Melt Pool Strategy

A signature feature of EBM is the use of **multiple preheat + melt passes**:

1. **Pre-heat Pass** — Low power (~10–20% of nominal) to gently sinter the powder layer.
2. **Contour Pass** — Traces the outer boundary of the part cross-section.
3. **Fill/Melt Pass** — High-power passes that fully melt the interior.

This staged approach **reduces residual stress** and **prevents powder "smoke" effects** (electrostatic repulsion of powder particles by the charged beam).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all **exam-solvable equations** for EBM. Master these — they are the highest-yield formulas in Module 3.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electron Kinetic Energy | $E_k = e \cdot V$ | eV (or J) | *e* = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C |
| 2 | Beam Power | $P = V \cdot I_{beam}$ | Watts (W) | *V* in volts, *I* in amps |
| 3 | Power Density | $\phi = \dfrac{P}{A_{spot}}$ | W/mm² | $A_{spot} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ |
| 4 | Volumetric Energy Density | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | J/mm³ | **Most critical KTU formula** |
| 5 | Specific Energy | $E_s = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | J/mm³ | Equivalent form of (4) |
| 6 | Linear Energy Density | $E_L = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | J/mm | Energy per unit scan length |
| 7 | Mass Energy Input | $E_m = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t \cdot \rho}$ | J/kg | $\rho$ = material density |
| 8 | Beam Spot Area | $A_{spot} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | mm² | *d* = spot diameter |
| 9 | Build Rate (Volume) | $\dot{V} = v \cdot h \cdot t$ | mm³/s | Product of scan kinematics |
| 10 | Lorentz Deflection Force | $F = q (v \times B)$ | N | Magnetic steering principle |

**Critical Boundary Conditions for Ti-6Al-4V (KTU must-know):**

| Parameter | Symbol | Critical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Optimal Energy Density | $E_v$ | **40 – 80 J/mm³** |
| Melting Point | $T_m$ | **1668 °C** |
| Preheat Temperature | $T_{pre}$ | **670 °C** (≈ 0.4 T_m) |
| Density (fully dense) | $\rho$ | **4.43 g/cm³** |
| Layer Thickness (typical) | $t$ | **70 μm** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Preheat Matters:** $T_{pre}/T_m \approx 0.4$ is the *Marangoni-stress-relief regime*. Keeping the bed hot at ~0.4 × melting point allows the material to remain ductile during cooling, drastically reducing residual stress and part distortion — a major advantage of EBM over SLM.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Why EBM? |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Ti-6Al-4V brackets, turbine blades | Vacuum prevents O-embrittlement |
| **Medical Implants** | Spinal cages, hip stems (Ti-6Al-4V ELI) | High build rate, low residual stress |
| **Automotive (Motorsport)** | Lightweight structural nodes | Topology-optimized lattice structures |
| **Defence** | Reactive metal (Nb, Ta) prototypes | Only AM process capable of handling these |
| **Energy** | Ni-superalloy combustion components | High-temperature capability |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Cathode Emission to Beam Power

**Problem Statement:**  
An EBM system operates with an accelerating voltage of **60 kV** and a beam current of **15 mA**. Calculate the (a) kinetic energy of a single electron, (b) the total beam power, and (c) the power density if the focused spot diameter is **0.2 mm**.

---

### Step 1: Identify the Governing Equation

The kinetic energy of a charged particle accelerated through a potential difference *V* is equal to the work done on the charge:

$$E_k = q \cdot V$$

where *q* = *e* = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C (electronic charge).

---

### Step 2: Calculate the Single-Electron Kinetic Energy

Given V = 60 kV = 60,000 V:

$$E_k = (1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,C) \times (60{,}000\,V)$$

$$E_k = 9.612 \times 10^{-15}\,J$$

In electron-volts (a more natural unit for this scale):

$$E_k = 60{,}000\,eV = 60\,keV$$

---

### Step 3: Calculate the Total Beam Power

Using the electrical power equation, with $I_{beam}$ = 15 mA = 0.015 A:

$$P_{beam} = V \cdot I_{beam}$$

$$P_{beam} = (60{,}000\,V) \times (0.015\,A) = 900\,W$$

---

### Step 4: Calculate the Spot Area and Power Density

Spot diameter *d* = 0.2 mm, so radius *r* = 0.1 mm:

$$A_{spot} = \pi r^2 = \pi \times (0.1)^2 = 0.0314\,mm^2$$

Power density:

$$\phi = \frac{P_{beam}}{A_{spot}} = \frac{900\,W}{0.0314\,mm^2}$$

$$\boxed{\phi \approx 28{,}662\,W/mm^2 \approx 2.87 \times 10^{4}\,W/mm^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A typical EBM delivers a power density of **~10⁴ W/mm²** — roughly **100× higher than a household laser pointer** and comparable to a focused solar furnace, but on a microscopic spot. This is why even refractory metals melt almost instantly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Volumetric Energy Density for EBM Process Parameter Design

**Problem Statement:**  
For an EBM process on Ti-6Al-4V, the following parameters are set: Beam power *P* = 1500 W, scan speed *v* = 4000 mm/s, hatch spacing *h* = 0.15 mm, layer thickness *t* = 0.07 mm. Determine the volumetric energy density and comment on whether it falls in the optimal processing window.

---

### Step 1: Write the Energy Density Expression

The volumetric energy density is the energy delivered per unit volume of powder:

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

---

### Step 2: Compute the Denominator (Effective Build Volume Rate)

$$\dot{V}_{build} = v \cdot h \cdot t$$

$$\dot{V}_{build} = (4000\,mm/s) \times (0.15\,mm) \times (0.07\,mm)$$

$$\dot{V}_{build} = 42\,mm^3/s$$

---

### Step 3: Compute the Volumetric Energy Density

$$E_v = \frac{1500\,W}{42\,mm^3/s} = 35.71\,J/mm^3$$

---

### Step 4: Compare to the Optimal Window

| Range | Value (J/mm³) | Result |
|---|---|---|
| **Under-melted (Lack of Fusion)** | $E_v < 30$ | Insufficient melting → porosity |
| **Optimal for Ti-6Al-4V EBM** | $40 \leq E_v \leq 80$ | Full density, low defects |
| **Over-melted (Keyholing / Evaporation)** | $E_v > 100$ | Vaporization, balling, residual stress |

Our value of **35.71 J/mm³ is borderline low** — at the edge of the lack-of-fusion regime. To bring it into the optimal window, the engineer should:

- **Reduce scan speed** (e.g., from 4000 → 2800 mm/s), or
- **Increase beam power** (e.g., from 1500 W → 2000 W), or
- **Reduce hatch spacing** to 0.1 mm.

A corrected optimal setting would be:

$$E_v^{opt} = \frac{2000\,W}{(2800\,mm/s)(0.15\,mm)(0.07\,mm)} = 67.99\,J/mm^3 \;\; \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — EBM Process Parameter Influence Map

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **high-yield KTU exam matrix**. Memorize the qualitative trends.

| Process Parameter ↑ (Increase) | Effect on Energy Density $E_v$ | Effect on Part Quality |
|---|---|---|
| Beam Power *P* | $E_v$ ↑ proportionally | Deeper melt; risk of keyholing if too high |
| Scan Speed *v* | $E_v$ ↓ (inverse) | Faster build, but risk of lack-of-fusion |
| Hatch Spacing *h* | $E_v$ ↓ (inverse) | Coarser build; porosity between hatches |
| Layer Thickness *t* | $E_v$ ↓ (inverse) | Faster build, but coarser Z-resolution |
| Spot Diameter *d* | $E_v$ unchanged, but $\phi$ ↓ | Wider, shallower melt pool |
| Preheat $T_{pre}$ | $E_v$ effectively ↑ (less energy needed) | Reduces residual stress dramatically |

**Key Trade-off Equation (Engineer's Design Triangle):**

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \dot{V}_{build} = v \cdot h \cdot t \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{Quality} \propto f(E_v, T_{pre})$$

A high **build rate** requires large $v \cdot h \cdot t$, but **quality** requires $E_v$ in the optimal band. The engineer must therefore raise *P* to compensate, leading to the practical EBM "rule of thumb":

> *To double build rate, double beam power, then re-validate $E_v$ window.*

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Electron Penetration Depth in Ti-6Al-4V (Kanaya-Okayama Model)

**Objective:** Estimate the electron beam penetration depth in titanium at 60 keV.

The **Kanaya-Okayama equation** (1972) gives the maximum penetration range $R$ of electrons in a solid:

$$R = \frac{0.0276 \cdot A \cdot E^{1.67}}{Z^{0.89} \cdot \rho}$$

where:
- *A* = atomic weight (g/mol)
- *E* = beam energy (keV)
- *Z* = atomic number
- *ρ* = density (g/cm³)

**For Titanium (Ti, Z = 22, A = 47.87, ρ = 4.43 g/cm³, E = 60 keV):**

$$R = \frac{0.0276 \times 47.87 \times (60)^{1.67}}{(22)^{0.89} \times 4.43}$$

Computing each piece:

- $60^{1.67} = 60^{1} \cdot 60^{0.67}$. $\log_{10}(60) = 1.778$, $1.67 \times 1.778 = 2.969$, so $60^{1.67} \approx 10^{2.969} \approx 931$.
- $22^{0.89}$: $\log_{10}(22) = 1.342$, $0.89 \times 1.342 = 1.195$, so $22^{0.89} \approx 15.7$.

Substituting:

$$R = \frac{0.0276 \times 47.87 \times 931}{15.7 \times 4.43}$$

$$R = \frac{1230.5}{69.55} \approx 17.7\,\mu m$$

**Conclusion:** At 60 keV, electrons penetrate roughly **15–20 μm into titanium**, justifying the standard 50–70 μm layer thickness (the beam easily fuses the full layer with a small margin).

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EBM System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below captures the principal subsystems of a commercial EBM machine (e.g., Arcam Q20plus) and their functional flow. It is rendered in Mermaid syntax for clean block-level representation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[High Voltage Power Supply 60 kV DC] --> B1[Electron Gun Filament Cathode]
    B1 -->|Thermionic Emission| C1[Accelerated Electron Beam]
    C1 --> D1[Electromagnetic Deflection Coils XY]
    D1 -->|Lorentz Steering| E1[Focused Beam Spot Powder Bed]
    E1 -->|Kinetic to Thermal Conversion| F1[Local Melt Pool]
    F1 --> G1[Pre-heated Build Platform 670 C]
    G1 --> H1[Solidified Cross Section Layer]
    H1 --> I1[Platform Lower Z axis]
    I1 --> J1[Powose Recoater Applies New Layer]
    J1 --> E1
    K1[Vacuum Pump System 10 to power 5 mbar] --> A1
    K1 --> B1
    K1 --> E1
    L1[Control PC Scan Strategy and Process Parameters] --> D1
    L1 --> A1
    M1[Cooling System Heat Exchanger] --> B1
    F1 --> N1[Process Monitoring Pyrometer and CCD]
    N1 --> L1
    H1 --> O1[Final 3D Metal Part]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left column** = electrical/vacuum input systems.
- The **middle column** = beam generation and steering.
- The **right column** = the part-building loop (melt → lower → recoat → repeat).
- **Closed feedback loop:** The pyrometer (N1) measures real-time melt-pool temperature and feeds data to the control PC (L1), which modulates the deflection coil signal — this is the **closed-loop thermal control** unique to EBM.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Process-Flowchart: Layer-by-Layer EBM Build Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Build Setup] --> S1[Evacuate Chamber to 10 power minus 4 mbar]
    S1 --> S2[Pre heat Build Platform to 670 C]
    S2 --> S3[Spread First Powder Layer 70 um Recoater]
    S3 --> S4[Pre heat Pass Low Power 300 W]
    S4 --> S5[Contour Pass Traces Outer Boundary]
    S5 --> S6[Fill Pass High Power 1500 to 3000 W]
    S6 --> S7{Is Layer Complete}
    S7 -->|No| S8[Recoat Next Layer]
    S8 --> S4
    S7 -->|Yes| S9[Lower Platform by 70 um]
    S9 --> S10{Are All Layers Done}
    S10 -->|No| S3
    S10 -->|Yes| S11[Cool Down in Vacuum to Below 100 C]
    S11 --> S12[Remove Powder via Powder Recovery System]
    S12 --> S13[Final Part Extracted and Blasted]
    S13 --> S14[End Build]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When drawing this flowchart in an exam, **always label temperatures, pressures, and layer thicknesses** on the relevant arrows. A flowchart without numbers is considered incomplete for full marks.

---

## 4.3 Comparison Mermaid — EBM vs SLM (DMLS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[EBM Electron Beam Melting] --> A1[Vacuum Environment]
    A --> A2[Beam Source 60 kV Electrons]
    A --> A3[Steering Magnetic Coils]
    A --> A4[Typical Material Ti 6Al 4V]
    A --> A5[Pre-heat 670 C]
    A --> A6[Residual Stress LOW]
    A --> A7[Build Rate HIGH]

    B[SLM Selective Laser Melting] --> B1[Inert Gas Argon]
    B --> B2[Beam Source Fiber Laser 200 W 1 kW]
    B --> B3[Steering Galvo Mirrors]
    B --> B4[Typical Material 316L Steel]
    B --> B5[Pre-heat 100 to 200 C]
    B --> B6[Residual Stress HIGH]
    B --> B7[Build Rate MODERATE]
```
