- **Porosity** is the enemy of ceramic strength: $\sigma_f = \sigma_0 \exp(-nP)$ — even 3% porosity reduction can yield ~16% strength gain.
- **Sintering follows Arrhenius-type diffusion kinetics**: $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$.
- **Binder Jetting** = best for large sand molds; **SLA** = best for high-precision investment casting patterns.
- **Industrial applications:** Foundry molds, investment casting patterns, injection mold inserts, extrusion dies, high-temp jigs, dental/bioceramic tools.
- **Always state material + process + application** in a 14-mark question to score full marks.
- **Sintering temperature of SiC (1900–2200 °C)** is the highest among common ceramics — remember this for comparison questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cast Metal Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET832 - 3D PRINTING AND TOOLING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cast Metal Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cast Metal Tools (CMT)** in the context of 3D Printing and Tooling refer to metal tools, dies, and molds that are produced by **pouring molten metal into a preformed cavity (mold)**, where the mold itself is fabricated — wholly or partially — using an **Additive Manufacturing (AM) process**. This bridges the gap between rapid prototyping and rapid manufacturing by leveraging AM to produce functional metallic tooling without conventional CNC machining of die steels.

As per the KTU 2024 syllabus nomenclature, Cast Metal Tools are classified under **Indirect Rapid Tooling (IRT)** and **Direct Rapid Tooling (DRT)** routes, primarily utilizing:

- **3D Printed Master Patterns** (used in sand casting and investment casting)
- **Direct 3D Printed Sand Molds** (Binder Jetting of foundry sand)
- **3D Printed Ceramic Shells** (SLA-based investment casting shells)
- **3D Printed Metallic Patterns** (for plaster mold casting, epoxy tooling)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Cast Metal Tools are the *fastest* route to functional metallic tooling when production volumes are in the range of **100 – 10,000 parts**, bridging prototype tooling and hard production tooling.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Conceptual Analogy)

Imagine you want to bake a chocolate cake in the shape of a star:
1. First, you sculpt a star-shaped master from clay (the **3D printed pattern**).
2. Then you press the clay star into wet sand to create a cavity (the **mold**).
3. You remove the clay and pour molten chocolate (the **molten metal**) into the sand cavity.
4. Once it cools, you break the sand and retrieve a solid chocolate star (the **cast metal tool**).

In Cast Metal Tools, **Additive Manufacturing replaces the time-consuming hand sculpting** of the master pattern. Instead of weeks of pattern-making, an engineer can 3D print a wax-like or plastic pattern in a few hours, then use traditional foundry processes to cast it in aluminum, bronze, or even tool steel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** AM is used for *pattern or mold making*; the actual metal tool is still produced via *conventional casting metallurgy*. This is what makes cast metal tools both **fast** (pattern is digital) and **durable** (cast metal behaves like conventionally machined tool steel).

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Shrinkage Allowance:** Typically **0.5% – 2.5%** depending on cast alloy (Al ≈ 1.3%, Steel ≈ 2.0%, Cast Iron ≈ 1.0%).
- **Dimensional Tolerance:** Sand cast tools achieve **±0.3 mm to ±0.5 mm**; investment cast tools achieve **±0.05 mm to ±0.15 mm**.
- **Lead Time Reduction:** From 6–12 weeks (conventional) to **1–3 weeks** (rapid cast tooling).
- **Hardness Range:** Cast aluminum tools: **80–150 HB**; Cast iron tools: **180–250 HB**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cast Metal Tooling Routes

Cast Metal Tools via AM are produced through two principal technological pathways:

### A. Indirect Cast Metal Tooling (Pattern-Based)
The AM process builds a *positive pattern*, which is then used in conventional foundry processes.

| Sub-Process | AM Pattern Technology | Casting Method | Typical Tool Material |
|-------------|----------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| 3D Printed Wax/Plastic Pattern | SLA, FDM, PolyJet | Investment Casting | Tool Steel, Bronze, Aluminum |
| 3D Printed Sand Master | Binder Jetting (indirect) | Sand Casting | Cast Iron, Aluminum |
| 3D Printed Polystyrene Pattern | FDM (with foam filament) | Lost Foam Casting | Aluminum alloys |

### B. Direct Cast Metal Tooling (Mold-Based)
The AM process builds the *mold itself* directly, eliminating the pattern step.

| Sub-Process | AM Mold Technology | Casting Method | Typical Tool Material |
|-------------|--------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| 3D Printed Sand Mold | Binder Jetting of silica sand | Sand Casting | Cast Iron, Aluminum |
| 3D Printed Ceramic Shell | SLA with ceramic-filled resin | Investment Casting | Superalloys, Tool Steel |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Process Logic (Indirect Route via SLA Pattern)

1. **CAD Model Generation** — Create the tool geometry in CAD, with **draft angles of 1°–3°** and **shrinkage compensation** pre-built into the model.
2. **Slicing & Build Setup** — Orient the pattern in the SLA/FDM build chamber; add **support structures** for overhangs.
3. **Additive Manufacturing of Pattern** — Build in photo-curable resin (SLA) or thermoplastic (FDM). Layer thickness: **0.05 mm – 0.15 mm**.
4. **Pattern Post-Processing** — Remove supports, UV-cure (for SLA), lightly sand parting lines.
5. **Investment Shell Building** — Dip pattern in ceramic slurry, stucco with refractory grains, repeat 6–8 times to build the shell.
6. **Pattern Burnout (Dewaxing)** — Flash-fire furnace at **1000°C – 1100°C** to eliminate pattern residue and sinter the ceramic shell.
7. **Metal Pouring** — Pour molten tool steel or bronze at **1500°C – 1600°C** into the preheated shell.
8. **Cooling, Shell Removal & Finishing** — Knock off ceramic, cutoff gates, machine critical interfaces, polish cavities.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table** — it covers every numerical question KTU examiners can ask on this topic. Note that vertical bars are rendered using `\vert` to preserve table formatting.

| Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pattern Shrinkage Compensation** | $D_p = \dfrac{D_f}{1 - S}$ | $D_p$ = Pattern dimension (mm), $D_f$ = Final tool dimension (mm), $S$ = Shrinkage fraction (e.g., 0.02) | Inverse compensation for casting shrinkage |
| **Linear Shrinkage Percentage** | $S = \dfrac{D_p - D_f}{D_p} \times 100$ | $S$ in %, lengths in mm | Computes alloy shrinkage |
| **Volume-to-Surface Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{V}{A}$ | $V$ = Volume (m³), $A$ = Surface area (m²) | Used in Chvorinov's cooling rule |
| **Chvorinov's Cooling Time** | $t = B \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{2}$ | $t$ = Solidification time (s), $B$ = Mold constant (s/m²) | Predicts solidification time for riser design |
| **Draft Angle Compensation** | $h_{draft} = L \times \tan(\theta)$ | $h_{draft}$ = Extra height (mm), $L$ = Draw length (mm), $\theta$ = Draft angle (deg) | Pattern withdrawal clearance |
| **Riser Modulus (Sphere)** | $M_{riser} = \dfrac{D_{riser}}{6}$ | $M$ = Modulus (m), $D$ = Diameter (m) | Riser must have higher modulus than casting |
| **Pattern Material Cost** | $C = V \times \rho \times u$ | $V$ = Volume (m³), $\rho$ = Density (kg/m³), $u$ = Unit cost (₹/kg) | AM pattern economics |
| **Casting Yield** | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{casting}}{W_{poured}} \times 100$ | $W$ = Weight (kg) | Metallurgical efficiency check |
| **Machining Allowance** | $A_m = t_m$ (per face) | $A_m$ = Stock to remove (mm), $t_m$ = Machining tolerance | Tool steel post-machining |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Quick-turn die-casting dies for **cylinder heads, gearbox housings, and brake calipers** where 3D printed sand cores allow complex internal cooling channels impossible with conventional sand cores.
- **Medical Implants:** Investment cast titanium implants (knee, hip) using SLA patterns for patient-specific geometry.
- **Aerospace:** Cast nickel-superalloy turbine blades using 3D printed ceramic shell cores with internal lattice structures for cooling.
- **Plastic Injection Molding:** Cast aluminum (Al-Si) prototype injection molds for **bridge production** of 5,000–20,000 plastic parts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Shrinkage Compensation for a Cast Steel Tool

**Problem Statement:** A plastic injection mold cavity of nominal dimension **200 mm × 100 mm × 50 mm** is to be produced via the investment casting route using **3D printed SLA pattern**. The cast alloy is **tool steel with 2.0% solidification shrinkage**. Compute the dimensions that must be built into the 3D printed pattern.

### Step 1: Identify the Governing Equation
The pattern must be oversized to compensate for the shrinkage that occurs as molten metal solidifies. From the KTU formula sheet:

$$D_p = \frac{D_f}{1 - S}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Shrinkage Fraction
For a 2.0% shrinkage, the fraction $S = 0.02$ (dimensionless). Substituting:

$$D_p = \frac{D_f}{1 - 0.02} = \frac{D_f}{0.98}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Length-wise Dimension
For $D_f = 200$ mm:

$$D_{p,1} = \frac{200}{0.98} = 204.0816 \text{ mm} \approx 204.08 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Width-wise Dimension
For $D_f = 100$ mm:

$$D_{p,2} = \frac{100}{0.98} = 102.0408 \text{ mm} \approx 102.04 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Height-wise Dimension
For $D_f = 50$ mm:

$$D_{p,3} = \frac{50}{0.98} = 51.0204 \text{ mm} \approx 51.02 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 6: Verify the Inverse Operation
Substitute the pattern dimension back into the shrinkage equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{check} &= \frac{D_p - D_f}{D_p} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{204.08 - 200}{204.08} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{4.08}{204.08} \times 100 \\
&= 1.999\% \approx 2.0\% \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7: Summary of Pattern Dimensions

| Final Tool Dimension (mm) | Shrinkage Fraction | Pattern Dimension (mm) | Extra Material (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 200 | 0.02 | 204.08 | +4.08 |
| 100 | 0.02 | 102.04 | +2.04 |
| 50 | 0.02 | 51.02 | +1.02 |

## 3.2 Derivation: Solidification Time via Chvorinov's Rule

**Problem Statement:** A cast iron tool of volume $V = 0.001 \, m^3$ and surface area $A = 0.45 \, m^2$ is poured into a 3D printed sand mold. The mold constant for the binder-jetted silica sand is $B = 540 \, s/m^2$. Calculate the solidification time.

### Step 1: Recall the Chvorinov's Equation

$$t = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Volume-to-Surface Ratio

$$\frac{V}{A} = \frac{0.001}{0.45} = 0.002222 \, m$$

### Step 3: Square the Modulus

$$\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2} = (0.002222)^{2} = 4.938 \times 10^{-6} \, m^{2}$$

### Step 4: Multiply by the Mold Constant

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= 540 \times 4.938 \times 10^{-6} \\
t &= 2.667 \times 10^{-3} \, s
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Convert to Practical Units (Minutes)

$$t = \frac{2.667 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \approx 4.44 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{minutes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The V/A ratio (Chvorinov's modulus) governs the *position of the last point to solidify*. The riser must always have a **larger modulus** than the casting itself; otherwise, shrinkage porosity forms in the casting body.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Cast Metal Tooling Routes

| Parameter | SLA Pattern + Investment | FDM Pattern + Sand | Binder-Jetted Sand Mold | SLA Ceramic Shell |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pattern/Mold Material** | Photopolymer resin | ABS / PLA | Silica sand + binder | Ceramic-filled resin |
| **Tool Material Cast** | Tool steel, Bronze | Cast iron, Al | Cast iron, Al | Superalloys |
| **Dimensional Tolerance** | $\pm 0.05$ mm | $\pm 0.3$ mm | $\pm 0.4$ mm | $\pm 0.1$ mm |
| **Surface Finish (Ra)** | $0.8 - 3.2 \, \mu m$ | $3.2 - 12.5 \, \mu m$ | $6.3 - 25 \, \mu m$ | $1.6 - 6.3 \, \mu m$ |
| **Lead Time** | 2 – 3 weeks | 1 – 2 weeks | 1 – 2 weeks | 2 – 4 weeks |
| **Tool Life (parts)** | 10,000 – 100,000 | 500 – 5,000 | 500 – 5,000 | 50,000 – 500,000 |
| **Best For** | Plastic injection molds | Low-volume castings | Large cast iron dies | Turbine blades |
| **Cost Driver** | Resin + Shell build | Pattern destruction | Sand + binder | Ceramic resin |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Indirect Cast Metal Tooling (SLA Pattern + Investment Casting)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CAD Model with Shrinkage Compensation] --> A2[SLA 3D Printing of Master Pattern]
    A2 --> A3[Pattern Post-Curing and Support Removal]
    A3 --> A4[Investment Shell Dipping and Stuccoing 6 to 8 times]
    A4 --> A5[Pattern Burnout in Flash Furnace 1000 degrees C]
    A5 --> A6[Preheating of Ceramic Shell]
    A6 --> A7[Pouring of Molten Tool Steel 1550 degrees C]
    A7 --> A8[Solidification and Cooling]
    A8 --> A9[Shell Knockoff and Gate Cutoff]
    A9 --> A10[Machining of Backing and Cooling Lines]
    A10 --> A11[Final Cast Metal Tool]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Direct Cast Metal Tooling (Binder-Jetted Sand Mold)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Sand Hopper Feeds Abrasive Silica Sand] --> B2[Recoater Spreads Uniform Sand Layer 0.2 to 0.5 mm]
    B2 --> B3[Inkjet Printhead Deposits Furan or Phenolic Binder on Sand Grains]
    B3 --> B4[Build Platform Lowers by One Layer Thickness]
    B4 --> B5{Reached Required Height}
    B5 -- No --> B2
    B5 -- Yes --> B6[Depowdering of Unbound Sand]
    B6 --> B7[Pouring of Molten Metal into 3D Printed Mold]
    B7 --> B8[Solidification and Shakeout]
    B8 --> B9[Final Cast Metal Tool]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Decision Matrix for Selecting a Cast Metal Tooling Route

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Tooling Requirement Identified] --> C2{Production Volume}
    C2 -- Less than 500 parts --> C3[3D Printed Polystyrene Pattern plus Lost Foam Casting]
    C2 -- 500 to 5000 parts --> C4[FDM Pattern plus Sand Casting]
    C2 -- 5000 to 50000 parts --> C5[SLA Pattern plus Investment Casting]
    C2 -- More than 50000 parts --> C6[Conventional CNC Machined Tool Steel]

    C5 --> C7{Tolerance Requirement}
    C7 -- Less than 0.1 mm --> C8[SLA Ceramic Shell Direct]
    C7 -- More than 0.1 mm --> C9[SLA Wax Pattern plus Investment]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Hybrid Cast Metal Tooling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Digital CAD Model of Tool] --> D2[Topology Optimization for Conformal Cooling]
    D2 --> D3[AM of Pattern or Mold with Embedded Cooling Channels]
    D3 --> D4[Conventional Foundry Process Casting]
    D4 --> D5[Post Processing CNC Machining of Reference Faces]
    D5 --> D6[Heat Treatment and Surface Polishing]
    D6 --> D7[Inspection using CMM and 3D Scanner]
    D7 --> D8{Meets Tolerance}
    D8 -- No --> D5
    D8 -- Yes --> D9[Approved Cast Metal Tool]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each flowchart represents the *block-level functional architecture* of the process. The actual physical tooling (pattern, mold, shell) is built through a series of additive and subtractive steps coordinated digitally.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define "Cast Metal Tools" in the context of 3D printing. List any two indirect rapid tooling methods used to produce them.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Cast Metal Tools are metal tools, dies, or molds produced by **pouring molten metal into a cavity**, where the cavity itself is fabricated using **Additive Manufacturing** — either as a master pattern (indirect route) or as the mold itself (direct route). This technique merges the geometric freedom of AM with the metallurgical strength of conventional casting.

**Two Indirect Rapid Tooling Methods (1 mark):**
1. **SLA Pattern + Investment Casting** — A photopolymer master pattern is 3D printed, invested in ceramic slurry, and burnt out before pouring tool steel or bronze.
2. **FDM Pattern + Sand Casting** — A thermoplastic (ABS/PLA) pattern is FDM-printed and used to create a sand mold; the pattern is destroyed during mold formation or burnout.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the role of shrinkage compensation in cast metal tools. What is the typical shrinkage percentage for (i) aluminum alloys, and (ii) tool steels?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Role of Shrinkage Compensation (2 marks):**
When molten metal solidifies, it undergoes volumetric and linear shrinkage. If the 3D printed pattern is made to the *final* tool size, the cast tool will be **undersized**. Therefore, the pattern must be **pre-compensated (enlarged)** by the inverse of the shrinkage fraction using:

$$D_p = \frac{D_f}{1 - S}$$

This ensures that, after shrinkage, the cast tool matches the intended nominal dimension.

**Typical Shrinkage Values (1 mark):**
- (i) **Aluminum alloys:** approximately **1.0% – 1.3%**
- (ii) **Tool steels:** approximately **2.0% – 2.5%**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **process of rapid casting using 3D printed SLA patterns** for producing a tool steel injection mold. Include a process flow and discuss material selection, build parameters, and post-processing steps. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **applications of cast metal tools in plastic injection molding and die casting industries**, with specific examples of components and the production volumes suited for each route. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The SLA-pattern-based investment casting route is the most common indirect method for producing tool steel injection molds. The SLA-built photopolymer master is invested in a ceramic shell, dewaxed, and filled with molten tool steel.

**Step-by-Step Process (5 marks):**
1. **CAD Design:** The mold cavity is modeled with **draft angles of 1°–2°**, parting lines, and **shrinkage compensation of 2.0%** pre-applied to the pattern.
2. **SLA Build:** A photopolymer resin (e.g., acrylate or epoxy-based) is cured layer-by-layer with a UV laser. Layer thickness: **0.05 mm – 0.1 mm**. Build orientation is selected to minimize supports on critical cavity surfaces.
3. **Post-Curing:** The pattern is UV-post-cured for **30–60 minutes** to achieve full polymerization and dimensional stability.
4. **Shell Investment:** The pattern is repeatedly dipped in **ceramic slurry (silica or zircon)** and stuccoed with **refractory grains (fused silica, molochite)**. Typically **6–8 layers** are built over 24–48 hours.
5. **Burnout (Dewaxing):** The shell is heated rapidly (flash dewaxing) in a furnace at **1000°C – 1100°C**, which vaporizes the pattern and pre-sinters the shell.
6. **Metal Pouring:** Molten **H13 tool steel or P20** is poured at **1500°C – 1600°C** under vacuum or centrifugal force.
7. **Cooling & Finishing:** The shell is knocked off, gates cut, and the **backing plates and cooling channels** are CNC-machined to specifications.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The SLA + investment route delivers **±0.05 mm tolerance**, mirror-like surface finish, and tool life up to **100,000 plastic parts**, making it ideal for medium-volume production.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Plastic Injection Molding (3.5 marks):**
Cast aluminum (Al-Si alloy) and cast tool steel molds are widely used as **bridge tooling** to bridge the gap between prototype and mass production.
- **Components:** Glove box lids, dashboard inserts, mobile phone housings, custom automotive brackets.
- **Production Volume:** **5,000 – 50,000 parts** per mold.
- **Advantages:** Faster lead time (2–3 weeks), 60% lower cost than CNC-machined steel molds, and the ability to **conformally cool** the mold by 3D printing the cooling channels in the cast pattern.

**Die Casting (3.5 marks):**
Cast iron and cast steel dies are used in **aluminum and magnesium die casting** of high-pressure die cast components.
- **Components:** Engine blocks, gearbox housings, structural automotive parts, heat sinks.
- **Production Volume:** **10,000 – 200,000 parts** per die.
- **Advantages:** Cast metal dies withstand the high thermal cycling (250°C – 700°C) of die casting, and the 3D printed sand cores enable **complex internal geometries** (e.g., water jackets in engine blocks) that are difficult with conventional sand cores.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **binder jetting process for direct 3D printing of sand molds** for cast metal tooling. Explain the working principle, materials, and advantages over conventional sand mold making. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable numerical example, explain the **shrinkage compensation calculations** required when designing a 3D printed pattern for a cast aluminum injection mold. Also explain the role of **draft angle and machining allowance**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
Binder Jetting is a powder-bed additive process where a liquid binder is selectively jetted onto layers of foundry sand to bond the grains into a solid mold.
- A thin layer of **silica sand (AFS 50–70 grain size)** is spread by a recoater to a thickness of **0.2 mm – 0.5 mm**.
- An **inkjet printhead** deposits droplets of **furan, phenolic, or inorganic binder** only in the regions corresponding to the mold cavity and core geometry.
- The build platform lowers, a new sand layer is spread, and the process repeats.
- Once complete, the **unbound sand is removed by vacuum or brushing** (depowdering), leaving a hollow, ready-to-pour sand mold.

**Materials (2 marks):**
- **Sand:** Silica (SiO₂), olivine, or chromite; grain size 100–200 μm.
- **Binders:** Furan resin (acid-cured), phenolic resin (heat-cured), or colloidal silica (for non-ferrous metals).
- **Curing:** Some systems use ambient curing; others post-cure the mold in an oven at **180°C – 200°C** to improve strength.

**Advantages over Conventional Sand Molds (2 marks):**
1. **No Pattern Required:** The mold is the pattern — eliminates pattern-making cost and lead time.
2. **Complex Internal Geometries:** Internal cooling channels, conformal passages, and lattice structures are easily printed, which are impossible with traditional wood/metal patterns.
3. **Fast Iteration:** Design changes are made digitally, with new molds printed in **hours** rather than days.
4. **No Draft Angle Required (optional):** Since the unbound sand is loose, the mold can be designed with **zero draft** if needed, simplifying cavity design.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Numerical Example on Shrinkage Compensation (4 marks):**

A cast aluminum (Al-Si) injection mold has a rectangular cavity of nominal size **150 mm × 80 mm × 40 mm**. The solidification shrinkage of the alloy is **1.3%**. Calculate the pattern dimensions.

Using the governing equation:

$$D_p = \frac{D_f}{1 - S} = \frac{D_f}{1 - 0.013} = \frac{D_f}{0.987}$$

| Final Cavity Dimension $D_f$ (mm) | Pattern Dimension $D_p = D_f \vert 0.987$ (mm) |
|---|---|
| 150 | $150 \div 0.987 = 151.97$ |
| 80 | $80 \div 0.987 = 81.05$ |
| 40 | $40 \div 0.987 = 40.53$ |

**Verification (1 mark):**

$$S_{check} = \frac{151.97 - 150}{151.97} \times 100 = 1.30\% \quad \checkmark$$

The pattern must be oversized by approximately **2 mm along the 150 mm direction** to account for shrinkage.

**Role of Draft Angle (1 mark):**
Draft angle ($\theta = 1°$–$3°$) is the **tapered relief** provided on the vertical walls of the pattern so that it can be **withdrawn from the sand mold without damaging the mold cavity**. The pattern height is extended to accommodate this taper using:

$$h_{draft} = L \times \tan(\theta)$$

**Role of Machining Allowance (1 mark):**
Machining allowance (typically **0.5 mm – 1.5 mm per face**) is the **extra stock** deliberately added to critical dimensions of the cast tool so that **finish CNC machining** can correct surface roughness, tool offset, and any minor casting distortion — bringing the tool to the final engineering tolerance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Pattern with Mold:** Students often interchange "pattern" and "mold." Remember: the **pattern is the positive replica** (consumed in casting), while the **mold is the negative cavity** (into which metal is poured).
> 2. **Forgetting the Inverse:** Shrinkage compensation is **NOT** $D_p = D_f \times (1 + S)$. The correct inverse form is $D_p = D_f \div (1 - S)$. Using the wrong formula leads to oversize errors up to 4%!
> 3. **Ignoring Draft Angle:** In KTU answers, simply mentioning "add 1.5% to pattern" without stating **draft and machining allowances** will cost at least 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up Process Names:** "Binder jetting" and "SLA" are **completely different AM processes** — do not interchange them when explaining sand-mold printing.
> 5. **Missing Material Examples:** Always cite **specific alloys** (e.g., H13 tool steel, Al-Si 356, cast iron GG25) rather than generic "metal" — examiners reward metallurgical specificity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Cast Metal Tools**

- **Definition:** Cast Metal Tools = molten metal poured into an AM-made cavity (pattern or mold) to form a functional metallic tool.
- **Two main routes:** **Indirect** (AM pattern + conventional casting) and **Direct** (AM mold, no separate pattern).
- **Most common AM pattern processes:** **SLA** (investment casting), **FDM** (sand casting), **Binder Jetting** (direct sand mold).
- **Master Shrinkage Equation to memorize:** $D_p = D_f \div (1 - S)$ — the **inverse** form is non-negotiable.
- **Typical Shrinkage Values:** Aluminum ≈ 1.3%, Steel ≈ 2.0%, Cast Iron ≈ 1.0%, Bronze ≈ 1.5%.
- **Chvorinov's Cooling Rule:** $t = B \cdot (V \div A)^{2}$ — risers must have higher V/A than the casting to feed shrinkage.
- **Chvorinov's Mold Constant $B$** for binder-jetted silica sand: roughly **500–600 s/m²**; for investment ceramic shells: **200–400 s/m²**.
- **Dimensional Tolerance Hierarchy:** Investment cast ≤ SLA ceramic shell < SLA pattern + investment < Binder-jetted sand < FDM pattern + sand.
- **Production Volume Sweet Spot:** Cast metal tools are ideal for **100 – 100,000 parts**; below this use polymer AM tools, above this use CNC-machined steel.
- **Key Industrial Applications:** Injection mold cavities (plastic parts), die casting dies (engine blocks, housings), turbine blades (investment cast superalloys), investment cast medical implants.
- **Three Pattern Allowances to always state:** **Shrinkage**, **Draft** (1°–3°), and **Machining** (0.5–1.5 mm/face).
- **Conformal Cooling:** 3D printed patterns/molds allow cooling channels that follow cavity contour — drastically reducing cycle time in cast injection molds.
- **Chvorinov's Modulus is the key riser design parameter** — it is the V/A ratio that determines solidification order.
- **Lost Foam vs Investment:** Lost foam uses a **polystyrene (foam) pattern** vaporized during metal pour; investment uses a **wax/resin pattern** melted out before pouring.
- **Material Specifications to Quote in Answers:** SLA resin (DSM Somos, Accura), Sand (AFS 50–70), Tool Steel (H13, P20), Aluminum (Al-Si 356, A360).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Investment Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET832 - 3D PRINTING AND TOOLING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Investment Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Investment Casting (Lost-Wax Casting / Precision Casting):** Investment casting is a metal-forming manufacturing process in which a disposable **wax pattern** (replica of the final part) is coated with a refractory ceramic slurry to form a one-piece disposable mold (the "investment"), after which the wax is melted out, the ceramic shell is fired to high temperature, and molten metal is poured into the cavity to produce a near-net-shape component with excellent surface finish and dimensional accuracy.

The process is governed by the standard **IC process chain**: *Pattern → Tree Assembly → Slurry Dipping & Stuccoing → Dewaxing → Burnout → Pouring → Knockout → Finishing*. It is a **disposable-mold**, **expendable-pattern**, **near-net-shape** casting process with typical achievable tolerances of **± 0.1 mm to ± 0.4 mm** and surface roughness of **Ra 1.6 – Ra 6.3 μm** (as-cast).

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Tolerance (as-cast) | **± 0.1 mm – ± 0.4 mm** |
| Surface Roughness (Ra) | **1.6 – 6.3 μm** |
| Minimum Wall Thickness | **0.5 – 1.5 mm** |
| Weight Range | **few grams – 80+ kg** |
| Pouring Temperature | **650 °C – 1750 °C** (alloy dependent) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The "Chocolate Lollipop Trick":** Imagine you have a small wax unicorn. You dip a string into it, dip the whole unicorn in white liquid ceramic, then sprinkle dry sand on it. Let it harden into a shell. Now imagine pouring hot water inside — the wax unicorn melts and runs out the bottom, leaving behind a perfect hollow ceramic unicorn-shaped cup. If you then pour molten chocolate (or in our case, molten metal) into that hollow ceramic shell, let it cool, and crack the ceramic away, you have a perfect solid chocolate unicorn. That, in one sentence, is **Investment Casting** — replacing the wax with metal, and the chocolate with the real engineering part.

The word **"Investment"** comes from the older English meaning: *to surround or clothe* (as in "invested in a cloak"). The ceramic literally *clothes* the wax pattern. Because the wax is "lost" in the process, it is also called the **Lost-Wax Process** — a method dating back ~5,000 years to the Indus Valley and ancient Egypt.

## 1.3 Why Investment Casting Matters (Engineering Motivation)

- It is the **only economical method for casting complex internal cavities, thin walls, and intricate 3D geometry** without parting lines.
- Critical for **aerospace super-alloy blades (single-crystal turbine blades)**, medical implants, jewelry, and firearm components.
- Produces **near-net-shape** parts, drastically reducing post-machining and material waste — directly supporting **sustainable / green manufacturing** objectives under NEP 2020.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Sequential Steps of Investment Casting

### **Step 1 — Pattern Production (Wax / Master Pattern)**
- A **master die** (typically aluminum or steel) is machined to the negative geometry of the final part.
- Molten **pattern wax** (a blend of paraffin, microcrystalline wax, and natural/synthetic resins) is injected into the die cavity.
- The wax pattern is ejected — it is an **exact positive replica** of the desired metal part, including part features.
- Typical wax injection parameters: wax temperature **65 – 75 °C**, die temperature **25 – 30 °C**, injection pressure **0.5 – 1.5 MPa**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Material Property — Pattern Wax:** Must have low shrinkage (≤ 0.5 %), high tensile strength, low ash content (< 0.05 %), and good flow. Common grades: *Freemelt 155*, *Blue Wax*, *Plasticine-based filler waxes*.

### **Step 2 — Tree Assembly (Wax Tree / Spruing)**
- Multiple wax patterns are **welded** (using a heated spatula) onto a central **wax sprue** to form a "tree."
- This allows several parts to be cast in one pour, improving economy.
- The sprue acts as the **down-runner and riser** pathway for molten metal.

### **Step 3 — Primary Coating (Face Coat / Investment Slurry)**
- The wax tree is **dipped** into a **ceramic slurry** — a mixture of:
  - **Refractory filler**: fused silica (SiO₂), zircon (ZrSiO₄), or alumina (Al₂O₃).
  - **Binder**: colloidal silica (SiO₂ sol) or ethyl silicate hydrolyzate.
- This first coat (the *face coat*) is critical — it reproduces the **surface finish** of the final casting.

### **Step 4 — Stuccoing**
- The wet slurry-coated tree is immediately **rain-sprinkled** with coarser dry refractory grains (stucco), e.g., 30–60 mesh fused silica.
- This builds shell thickness and provides mechanical strength for subsequent handling.
- The process of **dipping + stuccoing** is repeated **5–9 times**, with each layer dried (air-dried at controlled humidity, 22 °C, 40–60 % RH, for 6–24 h per coat).

### **Step 5 — Dewaxing (De-Investment)**
- The built-up shell is placed in a **flash dewaxing autoclave** at **0.6 – 0.7 MPa** steam pressure and **150 – 180 °C** for **6 – 15 minutes**.
- The wax expands and flows out rapidly — it is **recovered, filtered, and reused** (typical wax recovery: 80–95 %).
- Alternative methods: *furnace dewaxing* (slower), *microwave dewaxing*.

### **Step 6 — Burnout (Firing / Sintering)**
- The dewaxed shell is fired in a **gas or electric furnace** at **900 – 1100 °C** for **1 – 4 hours**.
- This:
  1. Removes residual wax and organic matter.
  2. **Sinters** the ceramic — giving the shell high strength and **zero moisture**.
  3. Preheats the mold so that molten metal does not suffer a thermal shock on pouring.

### **Step 7 — Pouring**
- The preheated shell is removed from the furnace and **molten metal is poured** immediately.
- Pouring methods: *gravity pour, vacuum-assisted pour, centrifugal casting, counter-gravity pour (vacuum induction).*
- Pour temperature is typically **50 – 150 °C above the liquidus** of the alloy (superheat).

### **Step 8 — Knockout, Cut-off, and Finishing**
- After solidification, the ceramic shell is **knocked off** (vibratory or by hammer).
- Individual castings are **cut from the tree** using abrasive cut-off wheels or band saws.
- Final finishing: *grinding, sandblasting, machining critical features, heat treatment, inspection.*

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are exam-grade, board-valuator friendly. Memorize the units!**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_{shell} = \dfrac{m_{dry\,shell}}{V_{shell}}$ | Bulk density of ceramic shell | $\text{g/cm}^3$ |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_{wax} = \dfrac{L_{die} - L_{pattern}}{L_{die}} \times 100\%$ | Wax pattern shrinkage (typically 0.3 – 0.8 %) | % |
| 3 | $\sigma_{shell} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_{min}}$ | Shell green-strength / fired-strength | MPa |
| 4 | $\Delta T_{superheat} = T_{pour} - T_{liquidus}$ | Superheat above liquidus | °C or K |
| 5 | $t_{solid} = k \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{2}$ | Chvorinov's rule (solidification time) | s |
| 6 | $h_{f} = h_{r} + \dfrac{\sigma}{g \cdot \rho_{m}}$ | Bernoulli: feeder head height (mod) | m |
| 7 | $V_{shrink} = f \cdot V_{pattern}$ | Total volumetric shrinkage compensation (1.5 – 3 % for steels) | cm³ |
| 8 | $Ra_{casting} \approx (1.5 - 2.0) \times Ra_{pattern}$ | Surface finish transfer factor | μm |
| 9 | $t_{autoclave} \approx \dfrac{V_{wax\,content}}{P_{steam}}$ | Dewaxing time scaling | min |
| 10 | $n_{coats} = \lceil \dfrac{t_{req}}{t_{per\,coat}} \rceil$ | Number of ceramic coats required | integer |

**Where:**
- $F_{max}$ = maximum crushing load on shell, $A_{min}$ = cross-sectional area
- $V, A$ = casting volume and surface area (Chvorinov)
- $k$ = mold constant, $f$ = volumetric shrinkage fraction
- $T_{pour}, T_{liquidus}$ = pouring and alloy liquidus temperatures
- $\sigma$ = surface tension, $g$ = gravity, $\rho_m$ = metal density

## 2.3 Critical Process Parameters and Why They Matter

- **Slurry Viscosity:** Controls shell thickness. Lower viscosity = thinner face coat = smoother surface but weaker shell. Typical: **30 – 50 seconds Zahn cup #5**.
- **Stucco Grain Size:** Face coat uses 100–200 mesh (fine); back coats use 12–30 mesh (coarse) for strength.
- **Drying Cycle:** Insufficient drying causes **lamination cracks** during dewaxing — a common KTU exam and industrial defect.
- **Burnout Temperature:** Too low → residual moisture → steam blow during pour. Too high → silica inversion at **573 °C** (α→β quartz) → shell cracking.
- **Pour Temperature Window:** Superheat too high → metal-mold reaction, grain coarsening. Too low → misrun, cold shuts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 573 °C Matters:** Quartz has a polymorphic transformation at 573 °C (α-quartz → β-quartz) with a volume change of ~0.86 %. Heating past this too quickly during burnout causes **spalling** of the ceramic shell. This is a favorite KTU 2-mark and 14-mark question topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pattern Shrinkage Compensation

When a wax pattern is molded, the wax **shrinks upon cooling** from injection temperature to room temperature. To obtain a final metal casting of correct dimension, three sequential compensations must be applied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shrinkage Compensations in IC (applied multiplicatively):**
> 1. **Wax shrinkage** (wax cooling, die to room): $S_w \approx 0.3 - 0.8 \,\%$
> 2. **Ceramic shell / investment expansion** (mold firing): $S_{ce} \approx 0.5 - 1.0 \,\%$
> 3. **Metal solidification shrinkage** (liquid → solid): $S_m \approx 1.5 - 6.0 \,\%$ (alloy dependent)

The **die cavity dimension** $L_{die}$ required to produce a final metal part of nominal dimension $L_{nom}$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{die} &= L_{nom} \cdot (1 + S_w) \cdot (1 + S_{ce}) \cdot (1 + S_m) \\
&= L_{nom} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{3} (1 + S_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step expansion (linear approximation for small $S$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{die} &\approx L_{nom} \cdot [\,1 + (S_w + S_{ce} + S_m)\,] \\
L_{die} &\approx L_{nom} \cdot [\,1 + (0.005 + 0.0075 + 0.03)\,] \quad \text{(using mid-range values)} \\
L_{die} &\approx L_{nom} \cdot [\,1 + 0.0425\,] \\
L_{die} &\approx 1.0425 \cdot L_{nom}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, for a **50 mm** nominal steel casting, the die cavity should be machined to:

$$
L_{die} = 50 \times 1.0425 = 52.125 \text{ mm}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Solidification Time via Chvorinov's Rule (Riser / Sprue Sizing)

To design a riser that solidifies **after** the casting body, Chvorinov's rule is applied:

$$
t_s = k \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{2}
$$

where $V$ = volume, $A$ = surface area, $k$ = mold constant.

**Condition for a sound riser:**

$$
t_{riser} \geq t_{casting} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; k \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_r}{A_r}\right)^{2} \geq k \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_c}{A_c}\right)^{2}
$$

Since $k$ cancels:

$$
\dfrac{V_r}{A_r} \geq \dfrac{V_c}{A_c}
$$

**Engineering safety rule (riser modulus 1.2× to 1.5× casting modulus):**

$$
M_r = \dfrac{V_r}{A_r} \geq (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) \cdot M_c
$$

**Worked example (sphere-casting case):**

For a sphere of diameter $D_c$:

$$
V_c = \dfrac{\pi D_c^{3}}{6}, \quad A_c = \pi D_c^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_c = \dfrac{D_c}{6}
$$

For a hemispherical top-riser (feed metal) of diameter $D_r$:

$$
M_r = \dfrac{D_r}{6}
$$

Equating with safety factor 1.25:

$$
\dfrac{D_r}{6} = 1.25 \cdot \dfrac{D_c}{6} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; D_r = 1.25 \cdot D_c
$$

So for a 60 mm diameter spherical steel casting, the **riser diameter** should be **at least 75 mm**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters vs. Defect Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favorite Topic: Cause-Effect Table of Investment Casting Defects**

| Defect | Probable Cause | Process Stage | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Misrun / Cold Shut** | Low pour temperature, low superheat | Pouring | Raise pour temp 30 – 50 °C |
| **Porosity (gas)** | Moisture in shell, dirty melt | Burnout / Pouring | Pre-bake shell at 1100 °C, degas metal |
| **Inclusion (ceramic)** | Weak shell, slag in pour | Pouring | Improve shell strength, use ceramic filters |
| **Surface roughness** | Coarse stucco, low slurry viscosity | Investing | Use 100+ mesh face-coat stucco |
| **Cracks in shell** | Rapid heating past 573 °C | Burnout | Slower ramp (≤ 100 °C/h) |
| **Wax deformation** | Hot storage (> 30 °C) | Pattern storage | Air-conditioned pattern room (20 – 25 °C) |
| **Lamination** | Insufficient drying between coats | Investing | Increase humidity-controlled drying time |
| **Metal-mold reaction** | High pour temp, reactive alloy (Ti, Mg) | Pouring | Use face-coat of yttria (Y₂O₃) or zircon |

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Surface Finish Transfer

The as-cast surface roughness is related to the wax pattern surface by an empirical transfer coefficient:

$$
Ra_{casting} = K_t \cdot Ra_{pattern}
$$

with $K_t \approx 1.5 - 2.0$ for investment casting. For a typical polished wax pattern with $Ra_{wax} = 0.8 \,\mu m$:

$$
Ra_{casting} = 1.75 \times 0.8 = 1.4 \,\mu m \;\;\approx\;\; Ra\,1.6
$$

This is **why investment castings are renowned for their mirror-like surface finish** — the smooth wax and the fine face-coat together yield finishes comparable to machined surfaces.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Investment Casting End-to-End

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Strategy:** Because Investment Casting is fundamentally a sequential flow process, a **flowchart** is the most pedagogically clear schematic representation. The following Mermaid block renders the complete 8-stage process chain with parallel inspection feedback loops.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Master Die Machining - Aluminum Steel] --> B[Step 2: Wax Injection into Die Cavity]
    B --> C[Step 3: Eject Wax Pattern - Replica of Part]
    C --> D[Step 4: Wax Tree Assembly - Weld to Central Sprue]
    D --> E[Step 5: Primary Slurry Dip - Refractory Filler + Binder]
    E --> F[Step 6: Stuccoing - Dry Ceramic Grains]
    F --> G[Step 7: Repeat Dip-Stucco 5 to 9 Times - Drying Between Coats]
    G --> H[Step 8: Dewaxing in Autoclave - 0.6 MPa Steam]
    H --> I[Step 9: Burnout Furnace 900 to 1100 C]
    I --> J[Step 10: Preheated Shell Ready for Pouring]
    J --> K[Step 11: Pour Molten Metal - Gravity or Vacuum]
    K --> L[Step 12: Solidification and Cooling]
    L --> M[Step 13: Knockout - Break Ceramic Shell]
    M --> N[Step 14: Cut-off from Tree]
    N --> O[Step 15: Grinding Sandblasting Machining]
    O --> P[Step 16: Final Inspection and Heat Treatment]
    P --> Q{Final Casting Acceptable}
    Q -- No --> R[Scrap or Remelt]
    Q -- Yes --> S[Finished Investment Casting Part]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Investment Casting Foundry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MFC[Material Feedstock and Chemistry]
        M1[Wax Blending Unit]
        M2[Ceramic Slurry Mixer]
        M3[Alloy Melting Furnace]
    end
    subgraph PT[Pattern Tree Section]
        P1[Master Die]
        P2[Wax Injector]
        P3[Welding and Tree Assembly Booth]
    end
    subgraph IS[Investment Shell Shop]
        I1[Dip Tank Slurry]
        I2[Stucco Rain Booth]
        I3[Climate Controlled Drying Room]
        I4[Flash Autoclave Dewaxing]
        I5[High Temperature Burnout Furnace]
    end
    subgraph PC[Pouring and Casting Cell]
        C1[Vacuum or Gravity Pour Station]
        C2[Solidification Bay]
        C3[Knockout and Cut-off Station]
    end
    subgraph FT[Finishing and QC]
        F1[Grinding and Blasting]
        F2[Heat Treatment Furnace]
        F3[NDT and Dimensional Inspection]
    end
    M1 --> P2
    M2 --> I1
    M3 --> C1
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4 --> I5 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> F1 --> F2 --> F3
```

## 4.3 Schematic of the Ceramic Shell Cross-Section

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Concept Description (text-rendered, since Mermaid cannot depict physical sections):** A ceramic shell after 7 coats has three functional zones:
> - **Face Coat (innermost, ~0.3 mm):** Fine 200-mesh fused silica + colloidal silica binder → gives surface finish.
> - **Intermediate Coats (2–5, ~0.5–0.8 mm each):** Progressively coarser stucco (60 → 30 mesh) → gives strength.
> - **Backup Coat (outermost, ~1.5 mm):** Coarse 12–20 mesh + cement-bonded plaster → gives rigidity for handling.
> - **Total shell thickness:** typically **6 – 15 mm**, depending on casting size and alloy.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define investment casting and name any two materials used for pattern making.  [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

Investment casting, also known as **lost-wax casting**, is a precision metal-forming process in which a disposable wax pattern is invested (surrounded) with a refractory ceramic slurry, dewaxed to leave a ceramic shell, fired at high temperature, and filled with molten metal to produce a near-net-shape component.

Two materials used for pattern making are:
1. **Pattern waxes** (e.g., paraffin–microcrystalline wax blends with synthetic resins).
2. **Polystyrene / polyurethane foam** (used in the "lost-foam" variant for larger patterns).

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for materials.)**

### **Q2. What is the function of stuccoing in investment casting?  [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

Stuccoing is the process of **rain-sprinkling dry refractory ceramic grains** onto the wet slurry-coated wax pattern immediately after dipping. Its functions are:
1. To **build up the shell thickness** rapidly, providing mechanical strength for subsequent handling.
2. To create a **rough surface** that helps the next slurry coat to adhere firmly (improving inter-layer bonding).
3. To give the shell **permeability** for gas and steam to escape during dewaxing and pouring.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for functions.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, every Part-B question carries **14 marks**, and within it the student is given an internal choice (sub-part OR alternative full question). Below are **TWO alternative full questions (A and B)**, each with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Students answer **ONE of the two full-question options**.

---

### **🔹 Question A (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### **A(a). With a neat flowchart, explain the step-by-step process of investment casting. List any four advantages of the process. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Flowchart** (refer to Section 4.1 above for Mermaid flow diagram — student may draw a labeled block diagram).

**Step-by-step explanation (6 × ~1 mark each):**

1. **Pattern production:** A metal die (Al / steel) is used to injection-mold a wax replica of the part. Waxes used are paraffin / microcrystalline / natural-resin blends. The wax pattern must be dimensionally stable, have low ash content, and exhibit low thermal shrinkage.

2. **Wax tree assembly:** Multiple wax patterns are welded onto a central wax sprue using a heated spatula. The sprue forms the running and feeding system. Several parts are cast in one shell to improve economy.

3. **Investment (shell-making):** The tree is dipped into a ceramic slurry (refractory filler + binder), then stuccoed with dry ceramic grains. The dip–stucco–dry cycle is repeated 5–9 times to build a shell of 6–15 mm thickness. Coats are dried under controlled humidity (40–60 % RH, 22 °C, 6–24 h per coat).

4. **Dewaxing:** The shell is heated rapidly in a steam autoclave at 0.6–0.7 MPa, 150–180 °C, for 6–15 minutes. Wax expands and flows out; it is recovered for reuse. Rapid steam heating minimizes shell cracking.

5. **Burnout / firing:** The dewaxed shell is fired at 900–1100 °C for 1–4 hours. This removes residual wax/organic matter, sinters the ceramic for strength, and preheats the mold.

6. **Pouring:** Molten metal is poured (gravity / vacuum / centrifugal) into the hot shell. Superheat is typically 50–150 °C above the alloy liquidus.

7. **Knockout, cut-off, finishing:** After cooling, the ceramic shell is broken off, the casting is cut from the tree, ground, blasted, and machined as required.

**Four advantages (0.5 mark each):**
- Excellent surface finish (Ra 1.6 – 6.3 μm) — minimal machining.
- High dimensional accuracy (± 0.1 – 0.4 mm).
- Complex geometry possible — thin walls, internal cavities, no parting lines.
- Wide alloy range: ferrous (steel, stainless), non-ferrous (Al, Cu, Ti, Ni super-alloys), and precious metals.
- Near-net-shape → low material waste, energy efficient.

**(Total: 7 marks — 1 flowchart + 4 process steps × 1 mark + 2 marks for advantages.)**

---

#### **A(b). Discuss the role of dewaxing in investment casting. Explain flash dewaxing with its process parameters. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of dewaxing (3 marks):**
Dewaxing is the step in which the wax pattern is removed from the built-up ceramic shell, leaving a clean, dimensionally accurate ceramic cavity ready for burnout and metal pouring. Its purposes are:
- **Cavity formation:** Provides the negative shape for the molten metal.
- **Wax recovery:** The wax is collected, filtered, and recycled (recovery 80–95 %), reducing cost.
- **Shell integrity:** Proper dewaxing avoids shell cracking — the shell must withstand the rapid removal of wax without rupture.

**Flash Dewaxing (4 marks):**
Flash dewaxing is the most common industrial dewaxing technique. The invested shell is placed in a **high-pressure steam autoclave**.

**Process parameters:**
- **Steam pressure:** 0.6 – 0.7 MPa (≈ 6 – 7 bar gauge)
- **Steam temperature:** 150 – 180 °C
- **Cycle time:** 6 – 15 minutes
- **Heating rate:** very rapid (hence "flash")

**Mechanism:** The high-pressure steam heats the wax surface very rapidly. The wax near the shell surface melts first and forms a thin liquid film; the remaining interior wax expands due to thermal expansion and hydraulic pressure, and is expelled as a slug through the sprue opening. The shell interior is left clean.

**Advantages over furnace dewaxing:** Faster cycle, cleaner wax recovery, less shell cracking. **Caveat:** Requires careful autoclave pressure control to prevent shell rupture from sudden steam hammer.

**(Total: 7 marks — 3 for role + 4 for flash dewaxing.)**

---

### **🔹 Question B (14 Marks — Alternative to Question A)**  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### **B(a). Explain the burnout operation in investment casting. Why is the temperature range 900 – 1100 °C preferred? What happens if the shell is heated past 573 °C too rapidly? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Burnout operation (3 marks):**
After dewaxing, the ceramic shell is fired in a **gas or electric burnout furnace**. It serves three functions:
1. **Removes residual wax and organic binder** that may still be present in trace amounts.
2. **Sinters the refractory particles**, giving the shell high mechanical strength and rigidity.
3. **Preheats the mold** to a temperature close to (or slightly below) the alloy pouring temperature, minimizing thermal shock when metal is poured.

**Why 900 – 1100 °C is preferred (2 marks):**
- Above ~900 °C, the colloidal silica binder fully densifies and the ceramic shell achieves its **maximum fired strength** (often 10–20 MPa MOR).
- Below 900 °C → insufficient sintering → weak, porous shell.
- Above 1100 °C → risk of (a) silica inversion issues, (b) excessive shrinkage / distortion, and (c) for steel castings, **metal-mold reaction** is intensified on pouring.

**Significance of 573 °C and rapid heating (2 marks):**
At **573 °C**, quartz (SiO₂) undergoes a polymorphic transformation:

$$
\alpha\text{-quartz} \xrightarrow{573\,°C} \beta\text{-quartz} \quad \Delta V \approx +0.86\,\%
$$

This is accompanied by a sudden **volume expansion** of ~0.86 %. Heating the shell **too rapidly past this temperature** induces **thermal stress + volume jump simultaneously**, causing:
- **Spalling and cracking** of the shell.
- Loss of dimensional accuracy of the cavity.
- Possible catastrophic shell failure on pouring.

**Best practice:** Limit heating rate to **≤ 100 °C/h through the 500 – 600 °C range**, then resume faster ramp above 600 °C.

**(Total: 7 marks — 3 + 2 + 2.)**

---

#### **B(b). Compare investment casting with sand casting and die casting on the following criteria: (i) Pattern type, (ii) Tolerance, (iii) Surface finish, (iv) Production rate, (v) Cost, (vi) Typical alloys, (vii) Application areas. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Investment Casting | Sand Casting | Die Casting |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Pattern type | **Wax (disposable)** | Wood / metal (reusable for some) | Permanent steel die |
| (ii) Tolerance | **± 0.1 – 0.4 mm** (excellent) | ± 0.5 – 2 mm (poor) | ± 0.05 – 0.25 mm (excellent) |
| (iii) Surface finish (Ra) | **1.6 – 6.3 μm** (very good) | 12 – 25 μm (rough) | 0.8 – 3.2 μm (very good) |
| (iv) Production rate | **Low – Medium** (one part per shell) | Low – Medium | Very High (1000s/day) |
| (v) Cost per part | **High** (ceramic shell + wax) | Low | High tooling, low per-piece |
| (vi) Typical alloys | All metals: steels, Al, Ti, Ni, super-alloys, Au/Ag | Steels, cast iron, Al, Cu | Low-melt: Zn, Al, Mg (≤ 700 °C) |
| (vii) Applications | Turbine blades, jewelry, implants, firearms, surgical tools | Engine blocks, machine bases, large housings | Automotive parts, electronics housings, toys |

**Inference (1 mark):**
Investment casting occupies the **niche of high-precision, complex-geometry parts in any alloy (especially high-melting-point super-alloys)** — a capability that neither sand casting (rough) nor die casting (limited to low-melting alloys) can match. This is why investment casting is **the only choice for aerospace single-crystal Ni-super-alloy turbine blades**.

**(Total: 7 marks — 1 per row × 6 rows + 1 for inference = 7.)**

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in IC Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "dewaxing" with "burnout."** Dewaxing = wax removal (150 – 180 °C steam). Burnout = ceramic firing (900 – 1100 °C). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this swap.
> 2. **Forgetting the 573 °C silica inversion point** when asked about burnout — this is a guaranteed 2-mark question in every KTU paper.
> 3. **Stating surface finish as Ra 0.8 μm** (this is for die casting or machined surfaces). IC finishes are typically **Ra 1.6 – 6.3 μm as-cast**.
> 4. **Omitting wax recovery** in the dewaxing step — examiners expect the 80 – 95 % recovery statistic.
> 5. **Drawing a vague flowchart** — must contain at least 6 distinct blocks; a one-line "die → cast → part" sketch will fetch 0 of 2 marks.
> 6. **Mixing up "investment" (ceramic) with "investing" (the act of dipping).** Use "investment" for the ceramic shell, "investing" for the process.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 60-Second Rapid Revision Checklist for Investment Casting**

- ✅ **Other names:** Lost-wax casting, precision casting.
- ✅ **Pattern material:** Wax (paraffin-based) — *must* have < 0.05 % ash, < 0.8 % shrinkage.
- ✅ **Shell build-up:** 5 – 9 coats of slurry + stucco; 6 – 15 mm final thickness.
- ✅ **Slurry:** Refractory (fused silica / zircon / alumina) + binder (colloidal silica).
- ✅ **Stucco:** 100–200 mesh (face) → 12–30 mesh (backup).
- ✅ **Dewaxing:** Flash autoclave, 0.6 – 0.7 MPa steam, 150 – 180 °C, 6 – 15 min; wax recovery 80 – 95 %.
- ✅ **Burnout:** 900 – 1100 °C, 1 – 4 h. **Watch the 573 °C silica inversion!**
- ✅ **Pouring superheat:** 50 – 150 °C above liquidus.
- ✅ **Tolerance:** ± 0.1 – 0.4 mm. **Surface finish:** Ra 1.6 – 6.3 μm.
- ✅ **Min wall thickness:** 0.5 – 1.5 mm.
- ✅ **Chvorinov's rule:** $t_s = k \cdot (V/A)^2$ — use to design risers (riser modulus 1.2 – 1.5 × casting modulus).
- ✅ **Shrinkage compensations (multiplicative):** Wax (~0.5 %) + Shell (~0.75 %) + Metal (~3 %) → die ~1.04 × nominal.
- ✅ **Defect → Cause mapping** is high-yield: misrun = low superheat; porosity = moist shell; inclusion = weak shell; lamination = insufficient drying.
- ✅ **Applications:** Aerospace turbine blades (single-crystal Ni super-alloys), jewelry, medical implants, dental crowns, firearm components, art sculptures.
- ✅ **Unique advantage:** The **only** casting process for high-melting super-alloys with intricate geometry and no parting line.
- ✅ **Comparison anchor:** IC = precision + any alloy; Sand = rough + cheap; Die = precision + low-melt only.
- ✅ **NEP 2020 / Green Mfg link:** Near-net-shape → less machining → less material waste → sustainable.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fusible Metallic Core and Sand Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET832 - 3D Printing and Tooling
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fusible Metallic Core and Sand Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fusible Metallic Core (FMC)

**Academic Definition:**
A *Fusible Metallic Core* (also called a **Lost Core**, **Sacrificial Core**, or **Low-Melting-Point Alloy Core**) is a precision-engineered metallic insert manufactured from eutectic or near-eutectic bismuth-based alloys that can be liquefied and extracted from a finished casting or molded component by simple heating. The core is used to generate **complex internal cavities, undercuts, and convoluted fluid passages** that cannot be produced by collapsible sand cores or conventional two-part molds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Fusible metallic cores bridge the gap between **traditional foundry tooling** and **additive manufacturing**. In a 3D-printing context, these alloys are deposited via Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF), Bound Metal Deposition (BMD), or Direct Metal Deposition (DMD) to form sacrificial tooling that melts at **< 300 °C** — well below the parent metal's solidus.

**Standard Physical Constants & Composition Benchmarks (KTU Reference):**

| Alloy Designation | Composition (wt %) | Melting Point (°C) | Density (g/cm³) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Field's Metal | Bi 32.4 / In 51 / Sn 16.5 | **62** | 7.9 | Polymer injection cores |
| Wood's Metal | Bi 50 / Pb 26.7 / Sn 13.3 / Cd 10 | **70** | 9.4 | Plastics, prototype castings |
| Bi–Sn Eutectic | Bi 58 / Sn 42 | **138** | 8.6 | Aluminum alloy cores |
| Bi–Pb–Sn | Bi 52.5 / Pb 31.5 / Sn 16 | **95** | 9.7 | Zinc die-casting cores |

---

### 1.2 Sand Casting

**Academic Definition:**
*Sand Casting* is a **gravity-fed, expendable-mold** metal forming process in which a shaped cavity — produced by compacting sand bonded with clay, resin, or silicate — receives molten metal, allowing it to solidify before being shaken out and reclaimed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Modern **3D sand printing** (Binder Jetting of silica/foundry sand) has eliminated the wooden pattern in sand casting. The CAD geometry is sliced into 2D layers and selectively jetted with phenolic binder, producing a "pattern-less" mold in **< 24 hours**.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Jello Mold" Mental Model:**

Imagine making a shaped cake (sand casting) versus filling a water balloon inside a box (fusible core):

* **Sand Casting Analogy → Jello Mold:** You push a shaped object (pattern) into gelatin (sand mold), remove the pattern, and pour in colored juice (molten metal). When cold, you crack the gelatin away (shakeout).
* **Fusible Metallic Core Analogy → Ice Cube in a Cup:** You place a precisely shaped ice block (Bi-Sn core) inside a cup, pour hot wax (molten Al) around it. When wax solidifies, you warm the assembly; the ice melts and flows out, leaving a perfect internal cavity — just like how a Bi–Sn core melts out of an aluminum inlet manifold.

> [!TIP]
> **Why two technologies in one Module?**
> Both are **expendable tooling systems** central to *Rapid Tooling* — Fusible Cores deliver complex **internal** geometry, while Sand Casting (via 3D-printed molds) delivers complex **external** geometry. Together they enable near-net-shape components in a single rapid manufacturing cycle.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fusible Metallic Core — Operational Logic

**Stage-wise breakdown:**

1. **Core Fabrication Stage:** Low-melting alloy cast, machined, or 3D-printed (FFF of filled Bi-polymer composite filaments) to the negative of the desired internal cavity.
2. **Placement & Encapsulation:** Core is inserted into the die/mold cavity (fixed by core prints or pins). Molten parent alloy (commonly **Al-Si, Mg, or Zn**) is injected at high pressure.
3. **Solidification:** The parent alloy freezes around the core; thermal contraction is designed so the **parent shrinks onto the core**, locking it firmly.
4. **Core Extraction:** The entire assembly is heated in **molten salt bath / hot oil / circulating air oven** at **T_core_melt + 30 °C** for **5–20 minutes**. Liquid alloy drains into a collection tray and is **recycled (>95% recovery is typical)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Criterion for Core Alloy:**
> $$ T_{melt,parent} - T_{melt,core} \;\geq\; 150\ ^\circ C $$
> This ensures the parent alloy has fully solidified (with adequate safety margin) before the core is melted out, preventing any remelting or dimensional distortion of the finished casting.

---

### 2.2 Sand Casting — Operational Logic

**Stage-wise breakdown:**

1. **Mold Preparation:** Silica sand (SiO₂ ≥ 95%) is mixed with **binder** — clay + water (green sand), furan resin + catalyst (no-bake), or phenolic resin (shell molding).
2. **Pattern Withdrawal:** The pattern is rapped (vibrated) and lifted, leaving a negative cavity. *Draft angles* of **1°–2°** ensure clean separation.
3. **Core Setting:** Internal sand cores are placed in the drag half before closing.
4. **Pouring:** Molten metal at the **pouring temperature** (typically 100–200 °C above liquidus) fills the sprue → runner → gate → cavity.
5. **Solidification & Cooling:** Controlled by **chills**, **risers**, and **insulating pads**.
6. **Shakeout:** Sand is mechanically or hydraulically removed; the casting is cut off (sprue removed), cleaned, and inspected.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **Board-favourite** and directly testable in KTU ESE. Note that vertical bars use `\vert` to preserve table integrity.

| # | Formula | LaTeX Expression | Application | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chvorinov's Rule (Solidification Time) | $t \;=\; B \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{2}$ | Predict solidification time | t (s), V (m³), A (m²) |
| 2 | Riser Design Criterion (Modulus Rule) | $M_{riser} \;\geq\; 1.2 \cdot M_{casting}$ | Riser must solidify **last** | Dimensionless (m) |
| 3 | Modulus of Casting | $M \;=\; \dfrac{V}{A}$ | Volume-to-Surface ratio | m |
| 4 | Shrinkage Allowance (Linear) | $L_{pattern} \;=\; \dfrac{L_{casting}}{1 - S}$ | Pattern dimension compensation | mm |
| 5 | Mold Permeability (AFS Standard) | $P \;=\; \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{A \cdot \Delta P \cdot t}$ | Sand mold gas venting | cm⁴/(g·min) |
| 6 | Volumetric Pouring Weight | $W_{metal} \;=\; \rho \cdot V_{cavity} \cdot (1 + k_{loss})$ | Charge calculation | kg |
| 7 | Sprue Exit Velocity (Bernoulli) | $v \;=\; \sqrt{2 g h_{eff}}$ | Gating design | m/s |
| 8 | Reynolds Number for Gating | $Re \;=\; \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Flow regime (Laminar if Re < 2000) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Heat Flux at Mold Interface | $q \;=\; h \, (T_{metal} - T_{mold})$ | Transient heat transfer | W/m² |
| 10 | Core Thermal Safety Margin | $\Delta T \;=\; T_{melt,parent} - T_{melt,core}$ | FMC selection | °C |
| 11 | Sand Grain Fineness (AFS) | $AFS \;=\; \dfrac{\sum (\text{Residue \%} \times \text{Sieve No.})}{\sum \text{Residue \%}}$ | Sand quality grading | Number |
| 12 | Bi-Sn Alloy Density (Approx.) | $\rho_{BiSn} \approx 8.6 \; g/cm^3$ | Core weight estimation | g/cm³ |

> **KTU Examiner's Tip:**
> Memorise **Chvorinov's Rule** and the **Modulus Rule (M_riser ≥ 1.2 M_casting)** — these two formulas appear in **almost every KTU foundry question** since 2018.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Automotive:** Aluminium inlet manifolds with helical water jackets — only possible with FMC.
* **Aerospace:** Hollow turbine blades with convoluted cooling passages (Investment + FMC hybrid).
* **Heavy Machinery:** 3D sand-printed prototype engine blocks (BMW, Mahindra) — **80% lead-time reduction** vs. conventional pattern-making.
* **Medical:** Patient-specific titanium implants manufactured via investment casting of 3D-printed wax patterns.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Chvorinov's Rule for Solidification Time

**Physical Premise:** Heat is extracted through the *surface area* (A) and stored in the *volume* (V) of the casting. Time of solidification ∝ Volume/area ratio squared.

**Step 1 — Write heat balance at the solidification interface:**

$$
Q_{released} = Q_{absorbed\;by\;mold}
$$

**Step 2 — Latent heat released by solidifying metal of volume V:**

$$
Q_{released} = \rho \, V \, L
$$

where L = Latent heat of fusion (J/kg).

**Step 3 — Heat absorbed by mold through surface area A in time t:**

$$
Q_{absorbed} = h \, A \, (T_{melt} - T_{mold}) \, t
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and isolate t:**

$$
\rho \, V \, L \;=\; h \, A \, (T_{melt} - T_{mold}) \, t
$$

$$
t \;=\; \frac{\rho \, L}{h \, (T_{melt} - T_{mold})} \cdot \frac{V}{A}
$$

**Step 5 — Recognise that $\rho L / [h(T_m - T_{mold})]$ is a property of the casting-mold combination — call it the *Mold Constant* B:**

$$
B \;=\; \frac{\rho \, L}{h \, (T_{melt} - T_{mold})}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute to recover Chvorinov's Rule:**

$$
\boxed{\; t \;=\; B \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^{2} \;}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Solidification time scales with the **square** of the modulus. Doubling the V/A ratio quadruples the solidification time — a crucial insight for **riser sizing** in sand casting.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Shrinkage Allowance for Pattern Dimension

**Given:** Final casting dimension = 250 mm, alloy = Cast Iron with linear shrinkage S = 1.0 % = 0.010.

**Step 1 — Definition of shrinkage ratio:**

$$
S \;=\; \frac{L_{pattern} - L_{casting}}{L_{pattern}}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for L_pattern:**

$$
L_{pattern} \cdot S \;=\; L_{pattern} - L_{casting}
$$

$$
L_{pattern} \, (1 - S) \;=\; L_{casting}
$$

**Step 3 — Final pattern length:**

$$
L_{pattern} \;=\; \frac{L_{casting}}{1 - S}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$
L_{pattern} \;=\; \frac{250}{1 - 0.010} \;=\; \frac{250}{0.990}
$$

$$
L_{pattern} \;=\; 252.525 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Additional shrinkage allowance (ΔL):**

$$
\Delta L \;=\; L_{pattern} - L_{casting} \;=\; 2.525 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Standard Shrinkage Values (KTU Data Sheet):**

| Metal | Liquid Shrinkage (%) | Solidification Shrinkage (%) | Total (Pattern Allowance) (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cast Iron (Gray) | 0.0 – 1.5 | 0.0 – 2.5 | **1.0** |
| Steel | 1.5 – 2.5 | 3.0 | **2.0** |
| Aluminium Alloys | 1.0 – 1.5 | 3.5 – 4.5 | **1.5** |
| Copper Alloys | 1.5 | 4.0 – 4.5 | **1.5 – 2.0** |

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Riser Sizing by Chvorinov's Rule

**Problem (KTU ESE Style):**
A steel casting has a modulus M_c = 25 mm. The mold constant for sand is B = 4.0 min/cm². Design a cylindrical top riser with **height-to-diameter ratio h = 1.5** such that it solidifies **at least 25 % later** than the casting.

**Step 1 — Riser safety criterion:**

$$
M_{riser} \;\geq\; 1.25 \cdot M_{casting} \;\Rightarrow\; M_{riser} \;\geq\; 31.25 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Modulus of a cylinder (h = 1.5 D):**

$$
V \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} h \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} (1.5 D) \;=\; 0.375 \pi D^{3}
$$

$$
A \;=\; \pi D h + \frac{\pi D^{2}}{2} \;=\; \pi D (1.5 D) + 0.5 \pi D^{2} \;=\; 2.0 \pi D^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute M = V/A:**

$$
M \;=\; \frac{0.375 \pi D^{3}}{2.0 \pi D^{2}} \;=\; 0.1875 \, D
$$

**Step 4 — Equate to required modulus:**

$$
0.1875 \, D \;=\; 31.25 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
D \;=\; 166.67 \text{ mm} \;\approx\; 167 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Riser height:**

$$
h \;=\; 1.5 \cdot 167 \;=\; 250 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification — solidification times:**

$$
t_{casting} \;=\; B \cdot M^{2} \;=\; 4.0 \times (2.5)^{2} \;=\; 25.0 \text{ min}
$$

$$
t_{riser} \;=\; 4.0 \times (3.125)^{2} \;=\; 39.06 \text{ min}
$$

$$
\frac{t_{riser}}{t_{casting}} \;=\; \frac{39.06}{25.0} \;=\; 1.562 \;\;>\;\; 1.25 \;\; \checkmark
$$

> **Conclusion:** The riser solidifies **56 % later** than the casting, providing an adequate safety margin for feeding shrinkage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow: Fusible Metallic Core Manufacturing Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C[Step 2: Cast Core in Low-Temp Alloy Bi-Sn or Field's Metal]

    D --> E{Quality OK?}

    E -- Yes --> F[Step 4: Place Core in Die Insert via Core Prints]
    F --> G[Step 5: Inject Molten Parent Alloy Al or Mg at 650-700C]
    G --> H[Step 6: Solidify under Pressure Lock by Differential Shrinkage]
    H --> I[Step 7: Extract Assembly and Heat to 70-150C in Salt Bath]
    I --> J[Step 8: Liquid Core Drains Out 95 percent Recovery]
    J --> K[Step 9: Final Casting with Internal Cavity Ready for Inspection]
```
