<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`
*Define a Fusible Metallic Core. List any two low-melting alloys commonly used, along with their melting points.*

**Model Answer:**
A Fusible Metallic Core is a sacrificial metallic insert made from low-melting-point alloys (typically **Bi-Sn**, **Bi-Pb-Sn**, or **Field's Metal**) used to produce complex internal cavities in castings or injection-molded parts. After the parent metal solidifies, the core is melted out by heating above its melting point and recovered for reuse.

*Field's Metal* (Bi 32.4 % / In 51 % / Sn 16.5 %) melts at **62 °C**, while the *Bi–Sn eutectic* melts at **138 °C**.

**[3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two alloys]**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`
*Differentiate between green sand molding and no-bake (chemically bonded) sand molding.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Green Sand Molding | No-Bake Sand Molding |
|---|---|---|
| Binder | Bentonite clay + water | Furan / phenolic resin + acid catalyst |
| Curing | Self-set by moisture | Chemically hardened at room temperature |
| Mold Strength | Moderate | High |
| Lead Time | Fast (< 1 h) | Slower (2–8 h for cure) |
| Application | Mass production | Large castings, cores |

**[3 marks — 1 mark per key differentiating parameter × 3]**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Per KTU 2024 ESE Regulations, students must attempt ONE of the following two sub-choices.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure** for manufacturing a hollow aluminium inlet manifold using a **Fusible Metallic Core**. Discuss the **material selection criteria** for the core alloy and the **thermal safety margin** required during the casting process. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **3D sand-printing (binder jetting)** process for producing a complex sand mold. List **four advantages** of 3D-printed molds over conventional wooden patterns. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** A cylindrical steel casting of diameter **100 mm** and height **200 mm** is to be sand cast. Using **Chvorinov's Rule** (B = 4.0 min/cm²), determine:
* (i) The solidification time of the casting.
* (ii) The minimum modulus required for a cylindrical top riser (h = 1.5 D) using a safety factor of **1.25**.
* (iii) The riser diameter and height. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **risers, chills, and gates** in controlling shrinkage and porosity in sand castings. State **Chvorinov's Rule** and explain how it is applied in riser design. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solutions

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) FMC Procedure for Hollow Aluminium Manifold — [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Cavity Design (1 mark):**
The CAD model of the manifold is designed with the internal cooling jacket as a *negative* solid. A Bi-based core alloy is selected with **ΔT = T_melt,Al − T_melt,core ≥ 150 °C** — i.e., Bi-Sn eutectic (138 °C) is appropriate since Al melts at 660 °C (ΔT = 522 °C >> 150 °C).

**Step 2 — Core Fabrication (1 mark):**
The core is either (i) gravity-cast in a steel die, (ii) machined from a Bi-Sn billet, or (iii) 3D-printed (FFF of filled Bi-polymer filament followed by sintering).

**Step 3 — Core Insertion & Casting (2 marks):**
The core is positioned in the die cavity using *core prints* (mechanical supports). Molten aluminium is injected at **680–720 °C** under high pressure. Differential thermal contraction locks the parent Al onto the core on cooling.

**Step 4 — Core Extraction (2 marks):**
The casting is heated in a **molten salt bath** or hot oil at **150–170 °C** for 10–20 minutes. The Bi-Sn melts, drains into a collection tray, and is **recycled (> 95 % recovery)**. The manifold emerges with a clean internal jacket.

**Step 5 — Finishing (1 mark):**
Flash and runner remnants are trimmed; the casting is pressure-tested and inspected.

**(b) 3D Sand Printing Process — [7 marks]:**

**Process (4 marks):**
1. The CAD geometry is exported as STL and sliced into 50–200 µm thick layers.
2. A thin layer of silica sand (SiO₂ > 95 %) is recoated across the build platform.
3. A printhead selectively jets **furan/phenolic binder droplets** onto the sand layer, bonding particles only in the *cross-section of the mold*.
4. The platform lowers by one layer thickness, and the process repeats.
5. After 6–18 hours, the unbound sand is vacuumed away, revealing the cured mold.
6. The mold is oven-cured at **180–200 °C** to remove residual moisture and improve strength.

**Sketch (1 mark):** *[Refer to Figure 4.2 — Sand Printing Workflow]*

**Four Advantages (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **No wooden pattern required** — eliminates pattern storage, modification, and wear costs.
2. **Complex geometries achievable** — internal channels, organic shapes, lattice-reinforced cores.
3. **Lead time reduced** by 70–80 % — mold ready in < 24 h vs. 2–4 weeks.
4. **Easy design iteration** — modify CAD and reprint, no retooling penalty.

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Cylindrical Steel Casting — [7 marks]:**

**Given:** D = 100 mm, H = 200 mm, B = 4.0 min/cm², safety factor = 1.25, h_riser = 1.5 D_r.

**(i) Solidification time of the casting (2 marks):**

$$
V \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} H \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} (10)^{2} (20) \;=\; 1570.8 \text{ cm}^{3}
$$

$$
A \;=\; \pi D H + 2 \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \;=\; \pi (10)(20) + 2 \cdot \frac{\pi (10)^{2}}{4} \;=\; 628.32 + 157.08 \;=\; 785.40 \text{ cm}^{2}
$$

$$
M_{casting} \;=\; \frac{V}{A} \;=\; \frac{1570.8}{785.40} \;=\; 2.0 \text{ cm}
$$

$$
t_{casting} \;=\; B \cdot M^{2} \;=\; 4.0 \times (2.0)^{2} \;=\; 16.0 \text{ min}
$$

**(ii) Required modulus of riser (2 marks):**

$$
M_{riser} \;\geq\; 1.25 \cdot M_{casting} \;=\; 1.25 \times 2.0 \;=\; 2.5 \text{ cm}
$$

**(iii) Riser diameter and height (3 marks):**

For h = 1.5 D:

$$
M_{riser} \;=\; 0.1875 \, D_{r}
$$

$$
D_{r} \;=\; \frac{M_{riser}}{0.1875} \;=\; \frac{2.5}{0.1875} \;=\; 13.33 \text{ cm} \;=\; 133.3 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
h_{r} \;=\; 1.5 \times 133.3 \;=\; 200.0 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Verification (optional):**

$$
t_{riser} \;=\; 4.0 \times (2.5)^{2} \;=\; 25 \text{ min} \;\;>\;\; 16 \text{ min} \;\; \checkmark
$$

---

**(b) Role of Risers, Chills, and Gates — [7 marks]:**

**Risers (2 marks):**
Risers are *reservoirs of molten metal* placed on top of (or beside) the casting cavity. They compensate for **liquid and solidification shrinkage** by feeding liquid metal into the casting as it shrinks. Chvorinov's Rule ensures:

$$
t_{riser} \;\geq\; t_{casting} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; M_{riser} \;\geq\; 1.2 \, M_{casting}
$$

**Chills (2 marks):**
Chills are metallic (or sand) inserts placed in the mold to **accelerate local cooling**. End-chills at the extremities reduce the effective modulus, while side-chills eliminate hot spots, thereby preventing **centreline shrinkage porosity**.

**Gates (2 marks):**
Gates are narrow channels through which metal enters the cavity. The gate dimensions are designed to:
* Control **pouring velocity** ($v = \sqrt{2gh}$),
* Ensure **laminar flow** (Re < 2000) to minimise oxide entrapment, and
* Allow easy separation from the casting without flash.

**Chvorinov's Rule (1 mark):**

$$
t \;=\; B \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^{2}
$$

where *t* is solidification time, *V* is volume, *A* is surface area, and *B* is the mold constant.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> * **Do not confuse "modulus" with "radius"!** Modulus = V/A, not just the geometric radius. Students who confuse these lose **2 marks** instantly.
> * **Always convert units consistently** (mm ↔ cm) before applying B. If B = 4.0 min/cm² and you compute V/A in mm, the answer is **off by 100×**.
> * **Riser safety factor must be ≥ 1.2**, not 1.0. A safety factor of 1.0 leaves no margin for error — KTU expects **1.2 to 1.25**.
> * In FMC questions, **always state the Bi-alloy recycling rate** (> 95 %) — this is a free 1-mark bonus in Part B.
> * For sand casting, mentioning **draft angle (1°–2°)** and **rapping** during pattern withdrawal shows examiner-awareness and fetches an extra mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Fusible Metallic Core:** A **low-melting Bi-based alloy insert** used to create **complex internal cavities**. Melting point is **62 °C – 300 °C**, well below parent alloy melting points.
* **Safety margin** between core and parent melting points: **≥ 150 °C**.
* **Common core alloys:** Field's Metal (62 °C), Wood's Metal (70 °C), Bi-Sn eutectic (138 °C).
* **Sand casting** uses **silica sand** bonded with **clay (green)** or **resin (no-bake/chemically bonded)**.
* **Chvorinov's Rule:** $\;t = B(V/A)^{2}\;$ — Solidification time ∝ (Modulus)².
* **Modulus rule for risers:** $\;M_{riser} \geq 1.2 \cdot M_{casting}\;$.
* **Shrinkage allowance:** $L_{pattern} = L_{casting}/(1 - S)$ — varies from 1 % (cast iron) to 2 % (steel).
* **3D sand printing** (binder jetting) eliminates the **wooden pattern** and reduces lead time by **70–80 %**.
* **3D-printed FMCs** (FFF of Bi-polymer filled filaments) offer rapid iteration of internal cavity designs.
* **Sand grain size** is graded by AFS Fineness Number (typically **50–70 AFS** for ferrous castings).
* **Risers feed shrinkage**; **chills eliminate hot spots**; **gates control fill velocity**.
* **Mold permeability** governs gas venting and directly affects casting soundness.
* **Core recovery** in FMC systems is **> 95 %**, making it economically attractive for series production.
* **KTU 2024 outcome-based emphasis:** Always link your answer to **CO (Course Outcome)** — for this module, CO3/CO4: *Apply rapid tooling techniques for casting and molding processes.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct Methods for Rapid Tool Production: Classification of Direct Rapid Tool Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET832 - 3D PRINTING AND TOOLING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Direct Methods for Rapid Tool Production: Classification of Direct Rapid Tool Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct Rapid Tooling (DRT)** is a category of Rapid Tooling (RT) in which the **production tool itself is fabricated directly** by an additive manufacturing (RP) system in a **single, integrated, layer-by-layer build cycle**, with **no intermediate master pattern, sacrificial model, or soft tooling stage** required. The output of the AM machine is either the final metallic/plastic tool itself, or a near-net-shape preform that requires only **minimal post-processing** such as infiltration, curing, sintering, or surface finishing.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Direct Rapid Tooling is positioned as a **"one-step" tool-making strategy**, where the CAD data flows directly to the additive machine, and the tool emerges from the build platform as a functional component. This contrasts with **Indirect Rapid Tooling (IRT)**, which uses the RP-built part only as a pattern for a secondary process (e.g., silicone rubber mold, investment casting, or sand casting).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Cake Baker vs. The Cake Mould Maker**
> - **Indirect Tooling** is like a baker who first sculpts a clay sculpture, then uses it to make a silicone mould, then pours cake batter into that mould to finally get the cake. *Three steps, three objects.*
> - **Direct Tooling** is like a 3D food printer that **prints the cake directly from the recipe (CAD file)**, layer by layer, in one machine, in one go. *One step, one object — the tool itself.*

For a KTU student, remember this: **In Direct Methods, the AM machine IS the tool-making machine. In Indirect Methods, the AM machine is only the pattern-making machine.**

## 1.3 Defining Metrics & Standard Terms

- **Build Envelope** — Maximum physical size of the tool that can be produced (**in mm³**).
- **Layer Thickness** — Vertical resolution of deposition (**typically 20–100 µm**).
- **Tool Life** — Number of parts that can be produced by the manufactured tool (**cycles**).
- **Post-Processing Time** — Secondary operations required (infiltration, HIP, machining) (**in hours**).
- **Volumetric Accuracy** — Dimensional conformity of the printed tool (**in % or µm**).

## 1.4 Position in the KTU 2024 RT Classification Tree

Rapid Tooling (RT)
├── **Direct Rapid Tooling (DRT)** ← *Module 4 Focus*
│   ├── Powder Bed Fusion family
│   ├── Directed Energy Deposition family
│   ├── Binder Jetting family
│   └── Material Extrusion family
└── Indirect Rapid Tooling (IRT)
    ├── Soft Tooling (silicone, epoxy)
    └── Hard Tooling (investment, sand cast)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Rationale — "The Why"

Direct Rapid Tooling methods are classified based on the **state and form of the feedstock material** and the **energy source used to consolidate each layer**. The KTU 2024 syllabus groups them into four major families:

### A. Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) Family
- The metal powder is pre-spread as a thin layer on a build platform.
- A high-energy beam (laser/electron) selectively fuses each cross-section.
- **Energy Source:** Laser or Electron Beam.
- **Mechanism:** Selective melting or sintering of metal powder.

### B. Directed Energy Deposition (DED) Family
- Powder or wire is **injected coaxially** with the energy source.
- Material is deposited AND fused simultaneously.
- **Mechanism:** No pre-spread bed; material is "blown" into the melt pool.

### C. Binder Jetting Family
- A liquid binder is selectively jetted onto a metal powder bed.
- Green part is formed, then **de-bound and sintered** in a furnace.
- **Mechanism:** Chemical bonding, not melting.

### D. Material Extrusion / Direct Write Family
- Metal-filled paste or filament is extruded through a nozzle.
- Green part is then **sintered or infiltrated** to reach full density.
- **Mechanism:** Extrusion + thermal post-processing.

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown of Each Direct Method

### 2.2.1 Selective Laser Sintering — Direct AIM (AARON / AARON-DS)
- **Process:** A CO₂ laser sinters a mixture of **steel powder (Fe–Ni–C)** pre-coated with a **polymer binder**.
- **Resulting State:** "Green" porous steel preform.
- **Post-Process:** The preform is infiltrated with **molten bronze (Cu)** to fill the porosity, achieving ~70–80% theoretical density.
- **Application:** Plastic injection mould inserts for low-volume production (typically **<10,000 cycles**).
- **Limitation:** Bronze infiltration means the tool is not pure steel — it is a **steel–bronze composite**.

### 2.2.2 Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS)
- **Process:** A high-power **Yb-fibre laser** selectively melts pre-spread metal powder (e.g., **Maraging steel 1.2709, Stainless 316L, Inconel 718, Ti6Al4V**).
- **Resulting State:** **Near-full density (>95%)** part in a single build.
- **Post-Process:** Stress relief, support removal, optional Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP), and CNC machining of critical surfaces (parting line, cavity face).
- **Application:** Hard tooling — injection moulds, die-casting inserts, extrusion dies, conformal cooling channels.
- **Advantage:** No binder, no bronze — true metal tooling.

### 2.2.3 Selective Laser Melting (SLM)
- **Process:** Functionally identical to DMLS but the laser **fully melts** the powder (vs. sintering in DMLS).
- **Distinction:** DMLS often sinters below the melting point, SLM ensures full melt pool formation.
- **Resulting State:** **~99% theoretical density**.
- **Application:** High-performance tool inserts with complex internal cooling.

### 2.2.4 Electron Beam Melting (EBM)
- **Process:** A **high-energy electron beam (60 kV)** melts conductive metal powder under **high vacuum**.
- **Resulting State:** Fully dense metallic tool.
- **Advantage:** Higher build speed than SLM, lower residual stress.
- **Limitation:** Restricted to conductive metals (no polymers/ceramics).
- **Application:** Aerospace tooling, titanium implants, hot-work die sections.

### 2.2.5 Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS)
- **Process:** A **powder nozzle (4-jet coaxial)** delivers metal powder directly into the path of a **high-power Nd:YAG laser** focused on the substrate.
- **Resulting State:** Fully dense deposit built on top of a substrate plate.
- **Distinction:** No powder bed — material is "blown" into the laser focal zone.
- **Application:** **Tool repair, re-coating of worn die surfaces, and adding features to existing tools** (cladding). Also used for hybrid additive-subtractive cells.

### 2.2.6 Electron Beam Additive Manufacturing (EBAM) / EBF³
- **Process:** Electron beam melts **wire feedstock** in a vacuum chamber.
- **Resulting State:** High deposition rate, large build volume.
- **Application:** Large-scale tooling and aerospace structural components.

### 2.2.7 Metal Binder Jetting (3D Printing / Prometal)
- **Process:** An inkjet head selectively deposits a **polymeric binder** onto a metal powder bed (e.g., **420 stainless steel, H13 tool steel**).
- **Resulting State:** "Brown" part held by the binder.
- **Post-Process Chain:** **De-binding → Sintering (in H₂/N₂ atmosphere furnace) → Optional Infiltration with bronze or copper.**
- **Application:** Complex mould inserts, medium-volume runs.

### 2.2.8 Direct Write / Robocasting (e.g., Prometal Paste-Based)
- **Process:** A metal-loaded paste (e.g., **17-4 PH stainless + binder solvent**) is extruded through a fine nozzle (typically **0.2–1.0 mm**) layer by layer.
- **Resulting State:** "Green" part that is dried, de-bound, and sintered.
- **Application:** Smaller mould inserts, conformal cooling inserts.

### 2.2.9 PolyJet / Multi-Jet for Soft Tooling
- **Process:** Photopolymer resin is jetted and UV-cured layer by layer to directly form a tool.
- **Application:** Soft tooling (silicone-like moulds) for very low-volume casting or thermoforming.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Parameter Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **compilation of the engineering relationships** most often used in KTU 14-mark derivations involving volumetric deposition, energy density, and tool-life estimation.

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Laser Energy Density (ED)** | $ED \;=\; \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | J/mm³ | Controls melt-pool integrity in SLM/DMLS |
| **Volumetric Shrinkage (S)** | $S \;=\; \dfrac{V_{green} - V_{sintered}}{V_{green}} \times 100$ | % | Critical for binder-jetted/sintered tools |
| **Density Ratio (ρ_ratio)** | $\rho_{ratio} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_{actual}}{\rho_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | % | Quality indicator for AM tools |
| **Layer Build Time (T_L)** | $T_{L} \;=\; \dfrac{A_{layer}}{v_{scan} \cdot w_{spot}}$ | s/layer | Used to estimate total build time |
| **Deposition Rate (D_R)** | $D_{R} \;=\; \pi \cdot r^{2} \cdot v_{feed}$ | mm³/s | For DED/LENS processes |
| **Tool Life Cycles (N)** | Empirical (Log-linear with hardness) | cycles | Indirect — depends on post-process |

**Where:**
- $P$ = Laser power (**W**)
- $v$ = Scan speed (**mm/s**)
- $h$ = Hatch spacing (**mm**)
- $t$ = Layer thickness (**mm**)
- $r$ = Melt-pool radius (**mm**)
- $v_{feed}$ = Powder feed rate (**mm/s**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always specify the **units of ED as J/mm³**. A value **< 40 J/mm³** typically causes lack-of-fusion porosity, while a value **> 100 J/mm³** causes keyhole porosity — both are 2-mark questions waiting to happen.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Conformal Cooling Channels:** Only direct methods (especially DMLS/SLM) can fabricate **internally curved, topology-optimized cooling lines** that follow the cavity contour — impossible by conventional CNC drilling. This can reduce cycle time by **20–40%** in plastic injection moulding.
- **Tool Repair:** LENS and EBAM are deployed in industries for **reclaiming worn forging/casting dies** by depositing fresh tool-steel material on damaged regions — extending tool life.
- **Bridge Manufacturing:** Direct tooling enables **tool production in 1–7 days** vs. 4–12 weeks for conventional tool-and-die, supporting mass-customization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Generic Process Flow for Powder-Bed-Fusion Direct Tooling (DMLS/SLM)

Below is the exhaustive step-by-step logic for fabricating a metal injection-mould insert via DMLS — the model answer for any 14-mark KTU question on "describe with a neat sketch the DMLS process."

> **Step 1 — CAD Preparation**
> The 3D CAD model of the tool insert is exported as an **STL file**, oriented on the build platform, and sliced into horizontal layers of thickness $t$ (typically **30 µm**).

> **Step 2 — Powder Deposition**
> A recoater blade spreads a uniform layer of metal powder (e.g., **Maraging steel 1.2709**, particle size **15–45 µm**) over the build plate. The layer thickness $t$ is controlled by the recoater mechanism.

> **Step 3 — Selective Melting**
> A **Yb-fibre laser** of power $P$ traces the cross-section at scan speed $v$, with hatch spacing $h$, fusing the powder into a solid cross-section.

The energy delivered per unit volume is given by:

$$ED \;=\; \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

If $ED$ is in the optimal window (typically **60–100 J/mm³** for maraging steel), the melt pool consolidates with minimum porosity.

> **Step 4 — Platform Lowering**
> The build platform descends by one layer thickness $t$, and a fresh powder layer is spread by the recoater.

> **Step 5 — Repetition**
> Steps 2–4 are repeated for all $N$ layers, where:

$$N \;=\; \frac{Z_{total}}{t}$$

with $Z_{total}$ being the total height of the tool insert.

> **Step 6 — Build Completion & Cooling**
> After the last layer, the build chamber is allowed to cool (in SLM under **inert argon**; in EBM under vacuum). Cooling can take **6–12 hours** to avoid thermal distortion.

> **Step 7 — Stress Relief**
> The build is heat-treated (e.g., **490 °C for 6 h** for maraging steel) to relieve residual stresses, followed by ageing for **HRC 50+** hardness.

> **Step 8 — Support Removal & Surface Finishing**
> Support structures are cut off. Critical functional surfaces (cavity face, parting line) are finished via **CNC milling, EDM, or micro-shot peening** to achieve the required surface roughness (typically **Ra 0.2–0.8 µm** for plastic moulds).

> **Step 9 — Tool Qualification**
> The tool is fitted into a mould base, polished, and tested for fitment, draft angles, and ejection clearance.

## 3.2 Energy Density Derivation — Why It Matters for Tool Quality

> **Why ED is the master variable in PBF tool-making:**

We begin with the volume of material consolidated per unit time:

$$V_{cons} \;=\; v \cdot h \cdot t$$

The energy delivered to this volume per second equals the laser power $P$. Therefore, the volumetric energy density is:

$$ED \;=\; \frac{P}{V_{cons}} \;=\; \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

- If $ED < 40 \text{ J/mm}^3$ → **Lack of fusion porosity** (un-melted powder grains).
- If $40 \leq ED \leq 100 \text{ J/mm}^3$ → **Conduction mode** — optimal for tooling.
- If $ED > 100 \text{ J/mm}^3$ → **Keyhole porosity** (vaporization of alloying elements).

This is the **classic KTU 14-mark derivation** for any direct PBF method.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix of Direct Rapid Tooling Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix below is the **single most exam-relevant comparative table** in this module. Memorize it column-by-column.

| Method | Feedstock | Energy Source | Final Density | Post-Process Required | Typical Tool Material | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct AIM (SLS)** | Steel + polymer-coated powder + bronze infiltrant | CO₂ Laser | 70–80% | Bronze infiltration | Steel–Bronze composite | Low-volume plastic injection |
| **DMLS** | Pure metal powder | Yb-fibre Laser | 95–99% | Stress relief, HIP, CNC | Maraging steel, H13, SS 316L | Hard tooling, conformal cooling |
| **SLM** | Pure metal powder | Yb-fibre Laser | ~99% | Stress relief, CNC | Ti6Al4V, Inconel 718 | High-performance inserts |
| **EBM** | Conductive metal powder | Electron Beam | ~99% | Stress relief, finish | Ti6Al4V, CoCr | Aerospace & medical tooling |
| **LENS** | Powder stream | Nd:YAG Laser | ~99% | None (or light machining) | Tool steel, Inconel | Tool repair, cladding |
| **EBAM** | Wire | Electron Beam | ~99% | None | Ti, Steel | Large structural tooling |
| **Metal Binder Jetting** | Metal powder + liquid binder | Print head (chemical) | 60% (green) | De-bind, sinter, infiltrate | SS 420, H13 | Complex mould inserts |
| **Robocasting** | Metal paste | Furnace sintering | 90–95% | Sinter, infiltrate | 17-4 PH, SS | Small insert features |
| **PolyJet (Soft Tool)** | Photopolymer | UV | 100% (polymer) | None | Polymer resin | Silicone-like soft moulds |

## 3.4 Decision Logic — When to Choose Which Direct Method

A KTU exam favourite: "Compare and contrast **Direct AIM** and **DMLS** for injection-mould fabrication."

The **decision rule** is governed by four parameters:

1. **Production Volume Required** → Below 10,000 parts: Direct AIM; above 10,000 parts: DMLS/SLM.
2. **Tool Material Purity Required** → Pure tool steel needed: DMLS/SLM; composite acceptable: Direct AIM.
3. **Geometric Complexity** → Conformal cooling internal channels: DMLS/SLM only.
4. **Lead Time** → Shortest: Direct AIM (no need for high-power laser, single bronze infiltration).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Differentiate between Direct and Indirect Rapid Tooling methods with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Direct Rapid Tooling (DRT):** The tool is fabricated directly by the AM process in a single build cycle with minimal post-processing. *Example:* DMLS of a steel injection-mould insert. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Indirect Rapid Tooling (IRT):** An RP pattern is first built, then used to produce the actual tool via a secondary process. *Example:* SLS master pattern → silicone rubber mould. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**List any four direct rapid tooling methods and state the energy source for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Method | Energy Source |
|---|---|
| DMLS | Yb-fibre laser **(0.75 mark)** |
| EBM | Electron beam **(0.75 mark)** |
| LENS | Nd:YAG laser **(0.75 mark)** |
| Metal Binder Jetting | Print head + furnace sintering **(0.75 mark)** |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing DMLS and SLM** — they are functionally similar but differ in melt-pool physics. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if you use them interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention post-processing steps** (stress relief, HIP, bronze infiltration). Direct ≠ No-post-process. Examiners deduct **3 marks** if you skip this.
> 3. **Omitting material names** — generic "metal powder" is not accepted. Always state *Maraging steel 1.2709, Ti6Al4V, H13, SS 316L, Inconel 718*. Loss = **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Missing the energy-density formula** in PBF answers = **2 marks** lost.
> 5. **Confusing DED with PBF** — DED uses coaxial powder feed, PBF uses pre-spread powder bed. Loss = **3 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a) Classify the various direct rapid tooling methods in detail, with suitable examples.     (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Direct rapid tooling methods are classified into four major families based on feedstock state and energy source.

**1. Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) Family — 2 marks**
- Powder is pre-spread on a build platform; a heat source fuses each layer.
- **Methods:** Direct AIM (SLS-based), DMLS, SLM, EBM.
- *Direct AIM* uses a CO₂ laser to sinter a **steel + polymer-coated powder** mix, later infiltrated with **bronze**. DMLS uses a fibre laser to fully melt **maraging steel or H13** powder, producing >95% dense inserts suitable for injection moulds.

**2. Directed Energy Deposition (DED) Family — 2 marks**
- Powder/wire is fed coaxially with the energy beam; no pre-spread bed.
- **Methods:** LENS (laser + powder), EBAM (electron beam + wire).
- *LENS* uses an **Nd:YAG laser + 4-jet powder nozzle**, mainly for **tool repair and cladding**.

**3. Binder Jetting Family — 1.5 marks**
- A print head deposits a **liquid polymeric binder** onto a metal powder bed to form a "brown" part, which is then **de-bound and sintered** in a furnace.
- *Example:* ExOne / Prometal process using **420 SS or H13 powder**.

**4. Material Extrusion Family — 1.5 marks**
- A **metal-loaded paste** is extruded through a fine nozzle (Robocasting) or **metal-filled filament** is FDM-printed, then sintered.
- *Example:* Direct Write for **17-4 PH stainless steel inserts**.

---

**(b) Explain with a neat block diagram the DMLS process for fabricating an injection-mould insert. List the typical post-processing operations.     (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**DMLS Process — 5 marks (1 mark per step)**

1. **CAD Preparation:** 3D model of the mould insert is exported as STL and sliced into layers of thickness $t$ ≈ 30 µm.
2. **Powder Deposition:** A recoater blade spreads a uniform layer of **maraging steel 1.2709 powder (15–45 µm)** over the build platform under **inert argon atmosphere**.
3. **Selective Melting:** A **Yb-fibre laser** (typically 200–400 W) scans the cross-section at speed $v$ ≈ 800–1500 mm/s, with hatch spacing $h$ ≈ 0.1 mm, fully melting the powder in the part cross-section. The energy density is:

$$ED \;=\; \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

which is maintained in the **60–100 J/mm³** conduction-mode window.

4. **Layer-wise Deposition:** The build platform descends by $t$, a fresh powder layer is spread, and step 3 repeats. For a 100 mm tall insert with $t = 30 \, \mu m$, the number of layers is:

$$N \;=\; \frac{100}{0.030} \;\approx\; 3333 \text{ layers}$$

5. **Cooling & Build Removal:** The build chamber is cooled under argon (6–12 h) to prevent oxidation and distortion.

**Post-Processing Operations — 2 marks (0.5 each)**
- **Stress Relief Heat Treatment** at ~490 °C for 6 h.
- **Age Hardening** to achieve HRC 50+.
- **Support Removal** by EDM/wire-cut.
- **CNC Machining** of parting line, cavity face, and ejector-pin holes to achieve **Ra 0.2–0.8 µm** surface finish.
- **Optional HIP (Hot Isostatic Pressing)** to close residual porosity.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a) Describe the Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS) process. Compare LENS with DMLS.     (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**LENS Process — 4 marks**
LENS is a **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** process where:
1. A **high-power Nd:YAG laser (typically 500 W – 4 kW)** is focused onto a substrate.
2. **Metal powder** (e.g., H13, Inconel, Ti6Al4V) is delivered through a **4-jet coaxial nozzle** into the laser focal zone.
3. The powder is **melted on-the-fly**, forming a solid weld bead that is deposited as a single track.
4. The **CNC gantry or robotic arm** moves the deposition head along the toolpath to build the tool **layer by layer**.
5. The process takes place inside a **sealed chamber flooded with argon** to prevent oxidation.
6. The deposited part achieves **>99% theoretical density** with metallurgical bonding to the substrate.

**Comparison LENS vs. DMLS — 3 marks (1.5 per row)**

| Parameter | LENS | DMLS |
|---|---|---|
| Feedstock Delivery | Powder injected coaxially with beam | Powder pre-spread as bed |
| Energy Source | Nd:YAG laser | Yb-fibre laser |
| Build Envelope | Large (limited by chamber/robot reach) | Smaller (limited by powder bed) |
| Resolution | Lower (≈ 0.5–1 mm bead) | High (≈ 50–100 µm) |
| Internal Channels | Difficult | Easy (unsupported overhangs in powder bed) |
| Best Use | **Tool repair, cladding, large features** | **Complex mould inserts, conformal cooling** |

---

**(b) What is Direct AIM? Explain its process flow, materials, and limitations.     (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition — 1 mark**
**Direct AIM** (originally developed by **DTM Corporation**, later AARON/AceS) is a **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** based direct rapid tooling method in which a **steel + polymer-binder-coated powder** is selectively sintered, and the resulting porous preform is **infiltrated with molten bronze** to produce a near-net-shape metallic tool.

**Process Flow — 3 marks (0.6 each)**
1. **CAD Preparation** and slicing into layers (typically $t = 100 \, \mu m$).
2. **Powder Coating:** Fine steel powder is pre-coated with a **thermoplastic polymer binder** (e.g., polyamide).
3. **Selective Sintering:** A **CO₂ laser** traces the cross-section, melting the polymer binder and fusing adjacent steel particles to form a "green" part.
4. **Powder Removal:** Loose unsintered powder is brushed and vacuumed away.
5. **Bronze Infiltration:** The green preform is placed in a furnace and **infiltrated with molten bronze (Cu–Sn alloy at ~1000 °C)**, which fills the porosity.
6. **Machining & Finishing:** Critical surfaces (cavity face, parting line) are finished by CNC/EDM.

**Materials — 1.5 marks**
- **Matrix:** Pre-alloyed steel powder (Fe–Ni–C), particle size ~30 µm.
- **Infiltrant:** Bronze (Cu 88%, Sn 12%) at infiltration temperature ~1000 °C.

**Limitations — 1.5 marks**
- Tool is a **steel–bronze composite**, not pure tool steel.
- Limited tool life (**<10,000 cycles**).
- **Bronze softening** above 200 °C — unsuitable for high-temperature applications.
- **Dimensional accuracy** is lower than DMLS due to infiltration shrinkage (~0.5–1%).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 10 Must-Remember Points for KTU OEMET832 – Module 4**

1. **Direct Rapid Tooling (DRT) = Tool is built directly by the AM machine, no master pattern required.**
2. **4 Families of DRT:** Powder Bed Fusion (PBF), Directed Energy Deposition (DED), Binder Jetting, Material Extrusion.
3. **Direct AIM** = SLS + bronze infiltration = steel–bronze composite tool for <10,000 cycles.
4. **DMLS / SLM** = Laser-melted metal powder bed = near-full density, true tool steel (e.g., **Maraging 1.2709, H13**).
5. **EBM** = Electron beam in vacuum = Ti6Al4V, high build speed, restricted to conductive metals.
6. **LENS** = DED + coaxial powder feed = best for **tool repair and cladding**, not for fine internal channels.
7. **EBAM** = Electron beam + wire = highest deposition rate, large aerospace tools.
8. **Metal Binder Jetting** = Print head + furnace sinter = complex internal channels, requires de-bind + sinter + infiltrate chain.
9. **Energy Density Formula (Master equation):**

$$ED \;=\; \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

— 40–100 J/mm³ is the safe conduction-mode window for PBF of tooling.

10. **Decision Rule:** **Need conformal cooling + complex internal geometry → DMLS/SLM. Need to repair/recoat a worn die → LENS/EBAM. Need composite tool in <7 days at low cost → Direct AIM. Need large volume + Ti-alloy tooling → EBM.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM) Tools

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM)** is a sheet-based rapid prototyping (RP) process belonging to the **Material Addition Subtraction Hybrid** family, in which successive sheets of material (typically paper, polymer, or metal foils) are bonded together using a thermal adhesive or welding process, and then selectively cut to the required cross-sectional contour using a **Computer-Controlled Laser (CO₂ laser)** or a mechanical knife, to build a 3D physical model or functional tool layer-by-layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> LOM is classified under **ASTM F2792** Standard Terminology for Additive Manufacturing as a *"Sheet Lamination"* process. It is one of the earliest commercial RP technologies, pioneered by **Helisys Inc. (USA)** in 1991.

## 2. Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

Think of LOM like building a **3D jigsaw puzzle using sticky note pads**:
- Imagine you have a stack of **post-it notes** (sheets), each with glue on one side.
- You press one note down onto a platform, then use a **scissors (laser)** to cut a specific shape out of that single layer.
- You then bring in the next note, press it down on top of the previous one, and cut another shape.
- Repeating this hundreds of times for different 2D cross-sections gives you a complete 3D object.
- At the end, you peel away the **uncut (waste) portions** (called the *"skeleton"* or *"cross-hatch grid"*) to reveal your final 3D part.

The "skeleton" waste is what makes LOM unique — it physically supports overhanging features during construction, eliminating the need for separate support structures (unlike SLA or FDM).

## 3. Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| **Layer Thickness** | **0.05 mm to 0.5 mm** (typically 0.1 mm for paper) |
| **Laser Type** | **CO₂ laser, 25 W to 50 W** |
| **Build Volume (Helisys LOM-2030)** | **813 mm × 559 mm × 508 mm** |
| **Positioning Accuracy** | **±0.1 mm (X-Y)**, **±0.15 mm (Z)** |
| **Feed Roller Pressure** | **0.2 MPa to 0.4 MPa** |
| **Adhesive Activation Temperature** | **80 °C to 110 °C** (for paper-based rolls) |
| **Typical Part Tolerance** | **±0.25 mm** |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often test the **layer thickness (0.1 mm for paper, 0.2 mm for plastic)** and **laser type (CO₂)** as direct one-liners. Memorize these values.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis — Working Principle & Sub-Systems

## 1. The LOM Process — Step-by-Step Operating Logic

The Laminated Object Manufacturing process can be decomposed into the following **six sequential stages**:

### Stage 1 — CAD Model Slicing
- The 3D CAD model is imported into the LOM slicing software (e.g., **LOMSlice**, **ArtiosCAD**).
- The software slices the model into horizontal 2D cross-sections corresponding to the **build layer thickness (Δz)**.
- The boundary contour of each slice is converted into a **laser tool path** (vector file).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why slicing?** Because LOM is a layer-by-layer process — it cannot directly fabricate 3D geometry; it must convert 3D into a stack of 2D profiles.

### Stage 2 — Sheet Feed & Positioning
- A new sheet is advanced from a **feed roll (supply spool)** over the **build platform** by a **feed roller assembly**.
- The sheet is held under tension to ensure flatness.
- A **heated roller (lamination roller)** applies heat and pressure to bond the new sheet to the previously laminated stack.

### Stage 3 — Lamination (Bonding)
- The heated roller traverses the build platform, applying:
  - **Heat (T_lam ≈ 80–110 °C)** — to activate the thermal adhesive pre-coated on the back of paper sheets.
  - **Pressure (P_lam ≈ 0.3 MPa)** — to ensure intimate contact and air-bubble-free bonding.
- For plastic/metal foils, an external adhesive is sprayed or pre-applied.

### Stage 4 — Laser Cutting
- The **CO₂ laser (λ = 10.6 μm)** traces the cross-sectional contour of the current layer along the X-Y plane.
- The laser **vaporizes** the material along the cut path, producing a clean kerf.
- The laser also cuts a **cross-hatch pattern** through the entire sheet area to create a **"skeleton"** of small squares (typically 10 mm × 10 mm) that hold the uncut waste material together for easy post-removal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the cross-hatch?** The skeleton is the genius of LOM — it acts as a built-in support structure, holding uncut waste in place so that overhanging, cantilevered, and hollow features remain supported throughout the build without dedicated support generation.

### Stage 5 — Platform Lowering
- The build platform is lowered by **one layer thickness (Δz)** — typically **0.1 mm** for paper-based LOM.
- The next sheet is fed, and the cycle repeats.

### Stage 6 — Depowdering / Skeleton Removal (Post-Processing)
- After the entire part is built, the operator manually removes the **uncut waste cubes** (the skeleton) using a **wire brush, scraper, or sandblasting**.
- For tooling applications, additional steps such as **resin infiltration, sanding, and sealing** are performed.

## 2. Sub-System Architecture

| Sub-System | Function | Critical Component |
|---|---|---|
| **Material Feed System** | Supplies fresh sheets to build area | Feed roller, supply roll, tensioning arms |
| **Lamination System** | Bonds layers thermally | Heated roller (T-controlled), pressure regulator |
| **Cutting System** | Cuts cross-sectional profiles | CO₂ laser, galvanometer scanner, focusing optics |
| **Motion System** | Provides X-Y-Z positioning | Servo motors, ball screws, build platform Z-axis |
| **Control System** | Synchronizes all operations | CNC controller, slicer software, host computer |
| **Exhaust & Filtration** | Removes laser fumes and particulates | HEPA filter, activated carbon scrubber, blower |

## 3. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Build Time | $T_{build} = \dfrac{N_{layers} \cdot t_{layer}}{3600}$ | hours | Where $N_{layers}$ is number of slices, $t_{layer}$ is time per layer (s) |
| Number of Layers | $N_{layers} = \left\lceil \dfrac{H_{part}}{\Delta z} \right\rceil$ | dimensionless | $H_{part}$ = part height, $\Delta z$ = layer thickness |
| Laser Power Required | $P_{laser} = k \cdot v_{cut} \cdot t_{mat}$ | W | $v_{cut}$ = cutting speed, $t_{mat}$ = sheet thickness, $k$ = material constant |
| Lamination Pressure | $P_{lam} = \dfrac{F_{roller}}{A_{contact}}$ | MPa | $F_{roller}$ = roller force, $A_{contact}$ = contact area |
| Material Utilization Efficiency | $\eta_{mat} = \dfrac{V_{part}}{V_{consumed}} \times 100\%$ | % | Accounts for waste skeleton |
| Layer Adhesion Strength | $\sigma_{adh} \propto T_{lam} \cdot t_{hold}$ | MPa | Higher temp & hold time increase bond strength |
| Kerf Width | $w_{kerf} \approx 0.1$ to $0.2$ mm | mm | Width of laser cut (compensated in tool path) |
| Volumetric Build Rate (LOM) | $\approx 5 \text{ to } 30$ in³/hr | in³/hr | Compared to SLA: ~1 in³/hr; FDM: ~2–5 in³/hr |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU numerical problems, the most frequently tested equations are **$N_{layers} = H_{part} / \Delta z$** and **$T_{build} = N_{layers} \times t_{layer}$**. Practice at least 2–3 problems using these.

## 4. Engineering Utility of LOM

- **Sand Casting Patterns**: LOM paper patterns are used to make expendable moulds for short-run sand casting.
- **Investment Casting Wax Patterns**: Replaces traditional injection-moulded wax patterns for low-volume castings.
- **Stretch Forming / Hydroforming Dies**: Short-run sheet metal forming tooling for prototyping car body panels.
- **Master Patterns for Silicone Moulding**: For vacuum casting of polyurethane prototypes.
- **Sand Moulds & Cores**: Direct fabrication of sand moulds for metal casting (using bonded sand sheets).
- **Visual & Functional Models**: Architectural models, medical models, packaging mock-ups.

## 5. Advantages & Limitations — The KTU Comparison Table

| Aspect | LOM (Laminated Object Manufacturing) |
|---|---|
| **Strengths** | Large build volume, no support structures, low material cost (paper), fast for large parts, good for casting patterns |
| **Weaknesses** | Poor surface finish on Z-axis (staircase effect), high waste material, post-processing labour-intensive, limited to sheet materials |
| **Material Range** | Paper (most common), plastic, metal foils, ceramics, composites |
| **Surface Finish** | Ra ≈ 10–25 μm (X-Y), Ra ≈ 100–200 μm (Z-direction) |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.1 mm (best in class for paper-based RP) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 1. Derivation I — Total Build Time for an LOM Part

**Problem Context (KTU Style):** Calculate the total build time for an LOM part of height 100 mm using a layer thickness of 0.1 mm, given that the time per layer (including feed + laminate + cut + lower) is 45 seconds.

### Step 1: Identify the parameters
Given data:
- $H_{part} = 100$ mm (part height)
- $\Delta z = 0.1$ mm (layer thickness)
- $t_{layer} = 45$ s (time per layer)

### Step 2: Calculate the number of layers

$$
N_{layers} = \left\lceil \frac{H_{part}}{\Delta z} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{100 \text{ mm}}{0.1 \text{ mm}} \right\rceil = 1000 \text{ layers}
$$

**Logic:** The part height divided by the layer thickness gives the number of discrete cross-sections. We use the ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ to ensure the last layer fully covers the topmost slice.

### Step 3: Calculate the total build time in seconds

$$
T_{total} = N_{layers} \times t_{layer} = 1000 \times 45 \text{ s} = 45000 \text{ s}
$$

**Logic:** Each layer requires 45 seconds of machine time, so 1000 layers require 1000 × 45 s.

### Step 4: Convert to hours for engineering readability

$$
T_{build} = \frac{T_{total}}{3600} = \frac{45000}{3600} = 12.5 \text{ hours}
$$

**Result:** The build will take **12.5 hours**.

---

## 2. Derivation II — Material Utilization Efficiency

**Problem Context:** An LOM part occupies a bounding box of 200 mm × 200 mm × 100 mm. The waste skeleton (cross-hatch) accounts for 60% of the consumed sheet volume. Find the **material utilization efficiency** $\eta_{mat}$.

### Step 1: Compute bounding box (consumed) volume

$$
V_{bbox} = L \times W \times H = 200 \times 200 \times 100 = 4{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

### Step 2: Compute actual part volume (assume dense cube, worst case)

$$
V_{part} = V_{bbox} \times (1 - f_{waste}) = 4{,}000{,}000 \times 0.40 = 1{,}600{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Logic:** Since 60% of consumed material is waste skeleton, only 40% becomes the actual part.

### Step 3: Calculate material utilization efficiency

$$
\eta_{mat} = \frac{V_{part}}{V_{consumed}} \times 100\% = \frac{1{,}600{,}000}{4{,}000{,}000} \times 100\% = 40\%
$$

**Result:** Material utilization is only **40%** — this is a key LOM disadvantage for expensive sheet materials.

---

## 3. Derivation III — Kerf Compensation for Dimensional Accuracy

**Problem Context:** The CAD model specifies a feature with a circular hole of diameter 25.0 mm. The LOM laser kerf width is 0.15 mm. Calculate the **compensated tool path diameter** to ensure the final physical hole measures 25.0 mm.

### Step 1: Understand the kerf effect

The laser removes material of width $w_{kerf}$ along the cut path. If we cut *along* the CAD boundary, the resulting hole will be **larger by $w_{kerf}$** on each side, totaling an excess of $w_{kerf}$ on the diameter.

### Step 2: Apply inward offset (compensate)

$$
D_{toolpath} = D_{CAD} - w_{kerf} = 25.0 - 0.15 = 24.85 \text{ mm}
$$

**Logic:** The laser cuts *inward* of the desired boundary by the kerf width, so the resulting physical hole matches the CAD dimension.

### Step 3: For external dimensions (bosses, outer walls)

$$
D_{toolpath, ext} = D_{CAD} + w_{kerf} = 25.0 + 0.15 = 25.15 \text{ mm}
$$

**Logic:** For outside dimensions, we cut *outward* of the boundary by the kerf to compensate for material lost during cutting.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love testing this kerf compensation concept. **Internal features = subtract kerf; External features = add kerf.** This is a guaranteed 2–3 mark question.

---

## 4. Analytical Matrix — LOM Process Variants

| Variant | Sheet Material | Bonding Method | Cutting Method | Tooling Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper LOM (Helisys)** | Adhesive-backed kraft paper | Heated roller | CO₂ laser | Sand casting patterns, visual models |
| **Plastic LOM (SLA-style)** | PVC / PVDF sheets | Adhesive spray + roller | CO₂ laser | Investment casting patterns |
| **Metal LOM (MELD-Form)** | Metal foils (Ti, SS) | Diffusion bonding (vacuum) | Laser or CNC mill | Short-run stamping dies, forging dies |
| **Composite LOM** | Ceramic or fibre-reinforced sheets | Brazing/sintering | Laser | Tooling inserts |
| **Sand LOM** | Bonded sand sheets | Adhesive + thermal cure | Laser | Direct sand moulds & cores |

## 5. Comparative Analysis Matrix — LOM vs Other RP Processes

| Parameter | LOM (Sheet Lamination) | SLA (Vat Photopolymerization) | FDM (Material Extrusion) | SLS (Powder Bed Fusion) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Raw Material** | Paper / polymer / metal sheets | Photopolymer resin | Thermoplastic filament | Polymer / metal powder |
| **Layer Thickness** | 0.05–0.5 mm | 0.05–0.15 mm | 0.1–0.3 mm | 0.06–0.3 mm |
| **Support Structure** | Not required (skeleton) | Required | Required | Not required (powder bed) |
| **Surface Finish** | Poor (Z-direction) | Excellent | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Build Speed (large parts)** | High | Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Material Cost** | Very Low | High | Low | Moderate |
| **Tooling Suitability** | Excellent (casting patterns) | Limited (resin) | Limited (FDM tooling) | Excellent (SLS patterns) |
| **Tolerance** | ±0.1 mm | ±0.1 mm | ±0.2 mm | ±0.2 mm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 1. Mermaid Block Diagram — LOM Machine Architecture

    D --> G[Laser Cutting System]

    E --> E2[Feed Roller Assembly]

    F --> F2[Pressure Regulator 0.3 MPa]

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM) and state two advantages over SLA.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM) is a **sheet-based additive manufacturing** process in which layers of adhesive-backed material (paper, plastic, or metal) are successively bonded together using a heated roller and cut to the required cross-sectional profile using a **CO₂ laser**, to build a 3D part.

**Two advantages over SLA:**
1. **No support structures are required** — the uncut cross-hatched "skeleton" supports overhanging features during the build, unlike SLA which needs delicate support generation and removal.
2. **Larger build volume and lower material cost** — paper-based LOM is significantly cheaper than photopolymer resin and can build large industrial parts (e.g., sand casting patterns) at much lower cost per part.

---

### Question 2: List four materials commonly used in Laminated Object Manufacturing and state the typical layer thickness range.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
**Four materials used in LOM:**
1. **Adhesive-backed kraft paper** (most common, used in Helisys systems)
2. **Plastic sheets (PVC, PVDF, polyester)**
3. **Metal foils (stainless steel, titanium, aluminium)**
4. **Ceramic / composite pre-preg sheets**

**Typical layer thickness range:** **0.05 mm to 0.5 mm** (most commonly **0.1 mm** for paper-based LOM).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step working principle of the Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM) process** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **cross-hatch skeleton** in supporting overhanging features. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A sand casting pattern of dimensions 300 mm × 200 mm × 150 mm is to be built using LOM. The layer thickness is 0.2 mm and the time per layer cycle is 60 seconds. Calculate: (i) the total number of layers, (ii) the total build time in hours, and (iii) the volumetric build rate in cm³/hr. **(7 Marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**(a)** Compare and contrast **Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM)** with **Stereolithography (SLA)** and **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)** under the following heads: material, support structure, surface finish, build speed, and tooling suitability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the application of LOM in **(i) sand casting patterns** and **(ii) short-run sheet metal forming dies**, with neat flow diagrams. Discuss two limitations of LOM for tooling applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

## Model Solutions — Part B

### Question A — Model Solution

#### (a) Working Principle of LOM Process — Step-by-Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Expected Answer Length:** 1.5 to 2 pages with a labelled block diagram.

**Step 1 — CAD Slicing:** The 3D CAD model is imported into LOM slicing software (LOMSlice), which slices the model into horizontal 2D cross-sections corresponding to the build layer thickness (Δz = 0.1 to 0.2 mm). The slice boundaries are converted into a laser tool path.

**Step 2 — Sheet Feed:** A new sheet of adhesive-backed material (typically kraft paper) is advanced from the supply roll over the build platform by a feed roller.

**Step 3 — Lamination:** A heated roller (80–110 °C) applies pressure (~0.3 MPa) to bond the new sheet to the top of the existing laminated stack. The thermal adhesive on the back of the sheet is activated, forming a strong inter-layer bond.

**Step 4 — Laser Cutting:** A computer-controlled **CO₂ laser (25–50 W)** traces the cross-sectional contour of the current layer, vaporizing the material along the cut path. The same laser also cuts a **cross-hatch grid (10 mm × 10 mm squares)** through the unused portion of the sheet.

**Step 5 — Platform Lowering:** The Z-axis platform descends by one layer thickness Δz, ready for the next sheet feed.

**Step 6 — Repetition:** Steps 2–5 repeat for each layer until the entire part is built.

**Step 7 — Post-Processing:** The part is removed from the platform, and the cross-hatched waste skeleton is broken away using a scraper or wire brush. The part is then sanded and optionally resin-coated.

**Role of the Cross-Hatch Skeleton:**
- The cross-hatch grid is cut into the unused area of every sheet.
- It holds uncut waste material in place around the part, acting as a **built-in support structure**.
- This **eliminates the need for dedicated supports**, allowing easy fabrication of overhanging, cantilevered, and hollow features.
- After the build, the operator simply breaks off the waste cubes by hand.

#### (b) Numerical Solution

**Given Data:**
- Part dimensions: L = 300 mm, W = 200 mm, H = 150 mm
- Layer thickness: Δz = 0.2 mm
- Time per layer cycle: $t_{layer}$ = 60 seconds

**(i) Total Number of Layers:**

$$
N_{layers} = \frac{H_{part}}{\Delta z} = \frac{150 \text{ mm}}{0.2 \text{ mm}} = 750 \text{ layers}
$$

**Logic:** Divide the part height by layer thickness to get the number of discrete cross-sections.

**(ii) Total Build Time:**

$$
T_{total} = N_{layers} \times t_{layer} = 750 \times 60 \text{ s} = 45000 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{build} = \frac{T_{total}}{3600} = \frac{45000}{3600} = 12.5 \text{ hours}
$$

**Result:** Total build time is **12.5 hours**.

**(iii) Volumetric Build Rate:**

$$
V_{part} = L \times W \times H = 300 \times 200 \times 150 = 9{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 9000 \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
\text{Build Rate} = \frac{V_{part}}{T_{build}} = \frac{9000 \text{ cm}^3}{12.5 \text{ hr}} = 720 \text{ cm}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**Result:** Volumetric build rate is **720 cm³/hr** (≈ 44 in³/hr).

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### (a) Comparative Study — LOM vs SLA vs FDM

> [!NOTE]
> **Expected Answer Length:** 1.5 pages in tabular form. KTU examiners prefer a clean comparison table.

| Parameter | LOM (Sheet Lamination) | SLA (Vat Photopolymerization) | FDM (Material Extrusion) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Material** | Paper / plastic / metal sheets | Photopolymer liquid resin | Thermoplastic filament (ABS, PLA) |
| **Support Structure** | Not required (built-in skeleton) | Required (delicate, manual removal) | Required (manual removal) |
| **Surface Finish** | Moderate in X-Y, poor in Z | Excellent (best in class) | Moderate (visible layer lines) |
| **Build Speed** | High for large parts | Slow (laser dot-by-dot) | Moderate (nozzle speed limited) |
| **Tooling Suitability** | Excellent (casting patterns, dies) | Limited (brittle resin) | Limited (low strength) |
| **Material Cost** | Very Low | High | Low |
| **Tolerance** | ±0.1 mm | ±0.1 mm | ±0.2 mm |

**Conclusion:** LOM is the most suitable sheet-based RP process for **large tooling patterns** and short-run functional tooling, while SLA excels in **fine-detail prototypes** and FDM in **low-cost concept models**.

#### (b) Applications of LOM in Tooling

**(i) Sand Casting Patterns:**
- LOM paper patterns are used as expendable patterns for sand casting.
- The LOM paper pattern is placed in a flask, and foundry sand mixed with binder is compacted around it.
- After curing, the pattern is withdrawn (or burned out for thin-wall patterns), leaving a sand mould cavity.
- Molten metal is poured, cooled, and shakeout is performed.
- **Advantages:** Fast pattern turnaround, no need for expensive wooden or metal patterns, ideal for prototype castings and short production runs (typically < 50 parts).

**(ii) Short-Run Sheet Metal Forming Dies:**
- LOM is used to build resin-coated paper or composite die blocks for stretch forming, hydroforming, and short-run stamping.
- The LOM die is infiltrated with epoxy or phenolic resin to enhance hardness and wear resistance.
- The die is mounted in a hydraulic press, and a metal sheet (e.g., aluminium) is placed over the die.
- Press cycle stretches the metal to conform to the die surface.
- **Advantages:** Lead time reduction from weeks (conventional tooling) to days; ideal for prototype car body panels and aerospace skin panels in quantities of 5–50.

**Two Limitations of LOM for Tooling:**
1. **Poor Z-axis surface finish and staircase effect** — LOM parts have visible lamination lines in the build direction, requiring extensive post-processing (sanding, filling) before being used as a tooling surface.
2. **Low material strength in Z-direction (delamination risk)** — The inter-layer adhesive bond is the weakest link; under high forming pressures, LOM tools may delaminate or fail, limiting them to low-pressure forming applications and small production runs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Don't confuse the laser type** — LOM uses **CO₂ laser (10.6 μm wavelength)**, NOT Nd:YAG or fibre laser. Mark deduction of 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Always mention "cross-hatch skeleton" or "waste grid"** when describing LOM's support-free feature — this is the most-missed scoring keyword.
> 3. **For numerical problems, ALWAYS include the unit conversion step** (e.g., seconds to hours) — many students correctly compute 45,000 s but forget to write **12.5 hours**, losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Don't write "support structures are not needed" without qualifying** that the *cross-hatched skeleton acts as a built-in support* — examiners want the technical justification, not just the statement.
> 5. **Mistake to avoid in kerf compensation:** Internal hole = **subtract** kerf from CAD diameter; External boss = **add** kerf. Reversed logic is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM) Tools**

- ✅ **LOM = Sheet Lamination Process** (ASTM F2792) — uses **CO₂ laser** to cut cross-sectional profiles on **adhesive-backed paper/foil sheets**.
- ✅ **Pioneered by Helisys Inc. (USA), 1991** — one of the earliest commercial RP systems.
- ✅ **Key components:** Supply roll, feed roller, heated laminating roller, CO₂ laser, galvanometer scanner, Z-axis build platform, exhaust system.
- ✅ **Cross-hatch skeleton = built-in support** — eliminates the need for dedicated support structures.
- ✅ **Layer thickness:** 0.05–0.5 mm (commonly **0.1 mm for paper**).
- ✅ **Laser type:** **CO₂ laser, 25–50 W, wavelength 10.6 μm.**
- ✅ **Adhesive activation temperature:** 80–110 °C; **Lamination pressure:** ~0.3 MPa.
- ✅ **Tolerance:** ±0.1 mm (X-Y), ±0.15 mm (Z).
- ✅ **Major tooling applications:** Sand casting patterns, investment casting patterns, stretch-forming dies, stamping dies, master patterns for silicone moulding.
- ✅ **Key formula:** $N_{layers} = H_{part} / \Delta z$ and $T_{build} = N_{layers} \times t_{layer} / 3600$.
- ✅ **Kerf rule:** Internal features → subtract kerf; External features → add kerf.
- ✅ **Biggest advantages:** No support structures, large build volume, low cost paper material, excellent for casting patterns.
- ✅ **Biggest limitations:** Poor Z-axis finish, high waste material (~60%), delamination risk, labour-intensive post-processing.
- ✅ **Comparative recall:** LOM is faster for **large parts** than SLA; cheaper than SLS for **casting patterns**; weaker than metal AM for **end-use tooling**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "LOM = LAYER, OVERLAP, MELT, CUT"**
> - **L**ayer feed → **O**verlap by heated roller → **M**elt adhesive & bond → **C**ut profile with CO₂ laser
> Repeat the four steps for every cross-section until the 3D LOM tool is complete.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Framework for Composite Manufacture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET832 - 3D Printing and Tooling
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Framework for Composite Manufacture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

A **Composite Material** is a heterogeneous, engineered material composed of two or more distinct constituent phases — a **matrix** (continuous phase) and a **reinforcement** (dispersed phase) — combined at a macroscopic level to produce a synergistic combination of properties unattainable by the individual constituents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard):** A composite is a multi-phase material engineered such that the reinforcement (fibers, particles, or whiskers) carries the primary load while the matrix binds the reinforcement, transfers stress, and protects it from environmental degradation.

The **Framework for Composite Manufacture** refers to the systematic methodology encompassing:
1. **Material Selection** — choosing matrix and reinforcement chemistry.
2. **Design & Lay-up Geometry** — fiber orientation, stacking sequence, ply thickness.
3. **Process Selection** — open-mold, closed-mold, or additive routes.
4. **Consolidation & Curing** — application of heat, pressure, or radiation.
5. **Quality Assurance & Testing** — NDT, mechanical characterization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Matrix Families used in Industry:**
> - **PMC** (Polymer Matrix Composites) — epoxy + carbon/glass fibers (most common)
> - **MMC** (Metal Matrix Composites) — aluminum + SiC particles (aerospace, automotive)
> - **CMC** (Ceramic Matrix Composites) — SiC + SiC fibers (turbine, re-entry vehicles)

---

## Conceptual Analogy — The "Reinforced Concrete" View

Imagine you are building a **concrete slab** for a building rooftop. Plain concrete is exceptionally strong under compression (standing weight) but catastrophically weak under tension (bending). To overcome this, construction engineers embed **steel rebar** into the wet concrete. The concrete is now called **reinforced concrete**.

| Concrete Slab Component | Composite Material Equivalent | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|
| Concrete (continuous body) | **Matrix** (epoxy, polyester, aluminum) | Holds shape, transfers load, protects reinforcement |
| Steel Rebar (embedded bars) | **Reinforcement** (fibers, whiskers, particles) | Carries tensile/compressive load |
| Pouring & Setting | **Manufacturing & Curing Process** | Establishes interfacial bond |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Builder:** The stronger the *interface* between rebar and concrete, the better the slab performs. **Same rule in composites** — the fiber-matrix interface dictates the strength of the finished composite.

This simple analogy makes composite manufacturing intuitive: you are not *inventing* a new material from scratch; you are **combining two materials intelligently** so each compensates for the other's weakness.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Building Blocks of the Composite Manufacturing Framework

The framework can be decomposed into **six logical pillars**, each forming an independent decision node in the engineering workflow:

1. **Constituent Material Selection**
   - Reinforcement: Carbon, Glass, Aramid (Kevlar), Boron, Natural fibers.
   - Matrix: Thermosets (epoxy, vinyl ester, polyester) vs. Thermoplastics (PA, PEEK, PPS).

2. **Reinforcement Form Factor**
   - **Continuous fibers** → highest strength; directional properties.
   - **Short/chopped fibers** → moderate strength; quasi-isotropic behavior.
   - **Whiskers & nanofibers** → nano-reinforced composites.
   - **Particulates** → isotropic; lower cost.

3. **Lay-up Architecture**
   - Unidirectional, Cross-ply, Angle-ply, Quasi-isotropic, Random mats.

4. **Manufacturing Process Family**
   - *Open-mold:* Hand lay-up, Spray-up, Filament winding.
   - *Closed-mold:* RTM, VARTM, Compression molding, Injection molding.
   - *Continuous:* Pultrusion.
   - *Additive:* FDM with fiber-filled filaments, Markforged continuous fiber 3D printing.

5. **Consolidation & Curing Kinetics**
   - Thermosets require crosslinking (exothermic reaction).
   - Thermoplastics require melting + solidification.
   - 3D-printed composites cure layer-by-layer via localized thermal/UV energy.

6. **Process Parameter Windows**
   - Temperature, Pressure, Time, Viscosity, Resin flow, Fiber volume fraction.

---

## 2.2 Why the Framework Matters (Engineering Justification)

- Composites behave **anisotropically** — properties depend on direction. A systematic framework ensures designers do not mistakenly assume isotropic behavior in structural analysis.
- Failure in composites is **progressive and multi-modal** (matrix cracking, fiber-matrix debonding, delamination, fiber pull-out, fiber breakage). The framework pre-defines inspection checkpoints to catch each mode.
- Manufacturing defects (voids, dry spots, fiber misalignment) propagate to in-service failures — **the framework is a defect-prevention doctrine**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Composite Manufacture)

> [!NOTE]
> All notations used are standard ASTM/KTU conventions. Symbols **without** the pipe symbol \vert are used to avoid markdown table breakage.

| **Formula** | **Name / Concept** | **Variables** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Volume fraction closure | $V_f$ = fiber vol. fraction, $V_m$ = matrix vol. fraction | Composite constitution check |
| $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Density rule-of-mixtures (Voigt for density) | $\rho_c, \rho_f, \rho_m$ = densities | Predicting composite density |
| $W_f = \dfrac{V_f \rho_f}{V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m}$ | Weight fraction ↔ volume fraction conversion | $W_f$ = fiber weight fraction | Resin-to-fiber ratio design |
| $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Longitudinal modulus (iso-strain, Voigt) | $E_L, E_f, E_m$ = Young's moduli | Stiffness along fiber direction |
| $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Transverse modulus (iso-stress, Reuss) | $E_T$ = transverse modulus | Stiffness perpendicular to fibers |
| $\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Longitudinal strength (rule of mixtures) | $\sigma_L, \sigma_f, \sigma_m$ = strengths | Strength along fiber direction |
| $V_{void} = 1 - (V_f + V_m)$ | Void content | $V_{void}$ = volume fraction of voids | Quality assessment |
| $\eta_l = \dfrac{E_L - E_m}{E_f - E_m}$ | Longitudinal reinforcement efficiency | $\eta_l$ = efficiency factor (0 to 1) | Comparing real vs. ideal composite |
| $\tau = \dfrac{r \sigma_f}{2 l}$ | Fiber pull-out stress (Kelly-Tyson) | $\tau$ = interfacial shear, $r$ = fiber radius, $l$ = fiber length | Interface strength design |
| $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$ | Critical fiber length | $l_c, d, \tau_i$ = critical length, fiber dia, interfacial shear | Determining short vs. long fiber regime |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real-World Practice

- **Rule of Mixtures** is the first tool used by design engineers to **estimate composite stiffness/strength** before committing to expensive testing.
- **Critical fiber length $l_c$** determines whether short fibers can achieve full reinforcement — if $l < l_c$, the fiber pulls out; if $l \gg l_c$, the fiber breaks (efficient reinforcement).
- **Void content** above **2%** by volume typically degrades mechanical performance by more than **10%**, making it a critical process control parameter.
- The **interfacial shear strength $\tau_i$** is engineered using *sizings* (e.g., silane coatings on glass fibers) — a key materials engineering concern.

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rule of Mixtures for Density (Composite Constitutive Model)

### Starting Premise

By the principle of **mass conservation**, the mass of the composite equals the sum of masses of its constituents.

$$M_c = M_f + M_m$$

### Step-by-Step Logical Expansion

**Step 1:** Express each mass as the product of density and volume.

$$\rho_c V_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

> Here $V_c$ is the total composite volume, $V_f$ is the volume occupied by fibers, and $V_m$ is the volume occupied by the matrix.

**Step 2:** Apply the **volume conservation** identity — the composite volume equals the sum of the constituent volumes (assuming negligible void content for the ideal case).

$$V_c = V_f + V_m$$

**Step 3:** Divide both sides of the mass equation by $V_c$.

$$\rho_c = \rho_f \left(\dfrac{V_f}{V_c}\right) + \rho_m \left(\dfrac{V_m}{V_c}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the volume ratios as **volume fractions**.

$$V_f^{(c)} = \dfrac{V_f}{V_c} \quad \text{and} \quad V_m^{(c)} = \dfrac{V_m}{V_c}$$

Hence we obtain:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f^{(c)} + \rho_m V_m^{(c)}$$

**Step 5:** Use the volume fraction closure relation $V_m = 1 - V_f$ to express the rule in a single-variable form.

$$\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Voigt (Iso-Strain) Model for Longitudinal Modulus

### Physical Assumption

When a unidirectional composite is loaded **parallel to the fiber direction**, both fiber and matrix experience **identical strain** (iso-strain condition).

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$$

### Force (Load) Balance

The total load carried by the composite cross-section equals the sum of loads in the fiber and matrix phases.

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

### Convert to Stresses

Substitute $F = \sigma A$ into the load balance.

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

### Apply Hooke's Law

Since $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ and $\varepsilon$ is identical:

$$\sigma_c = E_c \varepsilon, \quad \sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon, \quad \sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon$$

Therefore:

$$E_c \varepsilon A_c = E_f \varepsilon A_f + E_m \varepsilon A_m$$

### Simplify by Canceling $\varepsilon$

$$E_c A_c = E_f A_f + E_m A_m$$

### Divide by $A_c$ and Identify Area Fractions as Volume Fractions

For uniaxial loading with uniform cross-section, the **area fractions** equal the **volume fractions**:

$$\boxed{E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)}$$

This is the **Voigt upper-bound** — the maximum theoretical stiffness.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reuss (Iso-Stress) Model for Transverse Modulus

### Physical Assumption

When the same composite is loaded **perpendicular to the fibers**, both phases carry **identical stress** (iso-stress condition).

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$$

### Strain Compatibility

The total strain is the **volume-weighted average** of the strains in each phase.

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$$

### Apply Hooke's Law ($\varepsilon = \sigma / E$)

$$\dfrac{\sigma}{E_T} = \dfrac{\sigma}{E_f} V_f + \dfrac{\sigma}{E_m} V_m$$

### Cancel $\sigma$ and Rearrange

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}}$$

This is the **Reuss lower-bound** — the minimum theoretical stiffness.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Critical Fiber Length (Kelly-Tyson Model)

### Concept

A fiber shorter than a critical length $l_c$ will pull out of the matrix instead of fracturing, thereby wasting reinforcement potential.

### Force on Fiber at Fracture

The maximum tensile load a fiber can carry before breaking:

$$F_{fiber} = \sigma_f \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

### Force Transferred Through Interface

The load is transferred via interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$ acting along the lateral surface $\pi d (l/2)$:

$$F_{interface} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \dfrac{l}{2}$$

### Equate the Two Forces (At the Critical Length)

$$\sigma_f \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \dfrac{l_c}{2}$$

### Solve for $l_c$

$$\boxed{l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \tau_i}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:**
- If $l < l_c$ → fiber pulls out (inefficient).
- If $l \gg l_c$ → fiber breaks (efficient reinforcement).
- Typical values: for glass/epoxy, $l_c$ ranges from **0.5 mm to 5 mm**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A glass-fiber/epoxy composite has $V_f = 0.45$, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa. Compute $\rho_c$, $E_L$, $E_T$, and $W_f$.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Composite Density:**

$$\rho_c = (2.55)(0.45) + (1.20)(1 - 0.45)$$

$$\rho_c = 1.1475 + 0.66 = 1.808 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt):**

$$E_L = (72)(0.45) + (3.5)(0.55) = 32.4 + 1.925 = 34.325 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 3 — Transverse Modulus (Reuss):**

$$\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{0.45}{72} + \dfrac{0.55}{3.5} = 0.00625 + 0.15714 = 0.16339 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$

$$E_T = \dfrac{1}{0.16339} \approx 6.12 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 4 — Weight Fraction:**

$$W_f = \dfrac{(0.45)(2.55)}{(0.45)(2.55) + (0.55)(1.20)} = \dfrac{1.1475}{1.8075} \approx 0.6348$$

So the composite is **63.5% fiber by weight** and **45% fiber by volume**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Hack:** Note how $E_L = 34.3$ GPa is much higher than $E_T = 6.12$ GPa — a **5.6× anisotropy ratio**! This is why unidirectional composites must be **designed with the load direction in mind**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board-Ready)

| **Property** | **Voigt (Longitudinal)** | **Reuss (Transverse)** |
|---|---|---|
| Loading direction | Parallel to fibers | Perpendicular to fibers |
| Strain field | Uniform (iso-strain) | Non-uniform |
| Stress field | Non-uniform | Uniform (iso-stress) |
| Modulus formula | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ |
| Bound type | Upper bound | Lower bound |
| Sensitivity to $V_f$ | Highly sensitive | Less sensitive |
| Engineering use | Ply-level axial stiffness | Ply-level transverse stiffness |
