# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a composite material. List and briefly explain any three types of reinforcement used in polymer matrix composites.**

**Model Answer:**

A composite material is an engineered material made by combining two or more distinct constituents — a **matrix** (binder) and a **reinforcement** (load carrier) — to obtain properties superior to the individual components.

The three common types of reinforcement are:

1. **Continuous Fibers** — long, unbroken filaments (e.g., carbon, glass) aligned in a single direction or woven into fabric. Provide highest strength and stiffness in the fiber direction.
2. **Short/Chopped Fibers** — fibers of length 3–25 mm randomly dispersed in the matrix. Provide quasi-isotropic properties at lower cost.
3. **Particulates** — small particles (e.g., silica, calcium carbonate, micro-balloons) dispersed in the matrix. Provide isotropic property enhancement at the lowest cost; used in bulk applications.

*(Self-explanatory 3-mark answer — definition 1 mark + 3 types with explanations 2 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Briefly explain the hand lay-up process for composite manufacturing. State one advantage and one limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Hand Lay-up Process:**
- The mold surface is cleaned and a **release agent** is applied.
- A **gel coat** is sprayed onto the mold for a smooth surface finish.
- **Resin** is poured and **fiber mats** are manually placed on top.
- **Rollers** are used to consolidate and remove air bubbles.
- Layers are repeated until design thickness is achieved.
- Curing occurs at **room temperature** (or oven-assisted).
- The part is **demolded** and trimmed.

**Advantage:** Low tooling cost; suitable for large parts like boat hulls.

**Limitation:** Labor-intensive; low fiber volume fraction (typically 25–35%); high void content.

---

## Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Set A]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **various manufacturing methods for polymer matrix composites** with emphasis on open-mold and closed-mold techniques.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Open-Mold Processes:**

- **Hand Lay-up:** Manual placement of fiber mats and resin. Used for boat hulls, wind turbine blades. Simple, low-cost, low $V_f$.
- **Spray-up:** Chopped fibers and resin sprayed simultaneously from a gun. Lower mechanical properties than hand lay-up but faster.
- **Filament Winding:** Continuous fibers passed through a resin bath and wound onto a rotating mandrel under controlled tension. Used for pressure vessels, pipes, rocket motor casings.

**(ii) Closed-Mold Processes:**

- **Resin Transfer Molding (RTM):** Dry fiber preform placed in a closed mold; resin injected under pressure. Higher $V_f$ (up to 55%), better surface finish.
- **Vacuum-Assisted RTM (VARTM):** Same as RTM but resin drawn by vacuum; suitable for very large parts like wind blades.
- **Compression Molding:** Pre-impregnated charge (SMC/BMC) placed in heated mold and pressed. Used for high-volume automotive parts.
- **Injection Molding:** Short-fiber reinforced thermoplastic pellets injected into a heated mold. Suitable for mass production of small components.

**(iii) Continuous Process:**

- **Pultrusion:** Continuous fibers pulled through a resin bath and a heated die to produce constant-cross-section profiles (e.g., structural beams, ladder rails).

> *(Examiner marks distribution: Open-mold 2 marks + Closed-mold 3 marks + Pultrusion 1 mark + Process sketch 1 mark.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **neat sketch**, describe the **pultrusion process** for composite manufacturing. List four advantages of pultrusion.

**Model Solution:**

**Process Description:**
- Fiber rovings are pulled from a **creel** through a **resin impregnation bath**.
- The wet fibers pass through a **preformer** to remove excess resin and shape the bundle.
- They then enter a **heated steel die** where the composite cures due to elevated temperature.
- A **pulling system** (hydraulic or caterpillar-type) extracts the cured profile continuously.
- An **in-line cutoff saw** cuts the profile to required lengths.

**Sketch Reference (from Section 4.3):**
> Creel → Resin Bath → Preformer → Heated Die → Puller → Cutoff Saw → Profile

**Advantages of Pultrusion:**
1. Continuous process — high production rate.
2. Very high fiber volume fraction achievable (up to 65%).
3. Excellent longitudinal strength and stiffness.
4. Low labor cost; minimal material wastage.
5. Constant and repeatable cross-sectional quality.

> *(7 marks = Process steps 3 + Sketch 2 + Advantages 2.)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Set B]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** **Derive the rule of mixtures** for the density and longitudinal modulus of a fiber-reinforced composite material.

**Model Solution:**

*(Full derivations already presented in Section 3.1 and 3.2; reproduce as follows:)*

**For Density:**

Starting from mass conservation $M_c = M_f + M_m$ and using $\rho = M/V$:

$$\rho_c V_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

Dividing by $V_c$:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f \cdot \dfrac{V_f}{V_c} + \rho_m \cdot \dfrac{V_m}{V_c}$$

Recognizing volume fractions:

$$\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)}$$

**For Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt):**

Under iso-strain, $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$. From force balance:

$$E_c \varepsilon A_c = E_f \varepsilon A_f + E_m \varepsilon A_m$$

Simplifying:

$$\boxed{E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

**Significance:** Voigt model gives the **upper bound** for stiffness — useful for design optimization where maximum axial stiffness is desired.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** **Compare open-mold and closed-mold processes** for composite manufacturing. State the merits and demerits of each.

**Model Solution:**

| **Parameter** | **Open-Mold (e.g., Hand Lay-up)** | **Closed-Mold (e.g., RTM)** |
|---|---|---|
| Tooling cost | Very low | High |
| Fiber volume fraction | Low (25–35%) | High (45–55%) |
| Surface finish (both sides) | One side only | Both sides |
| Production rate | Slow | Faster |
| Void content | Higher (3–5%) | Lower (<2%) |
| Part size limitation | Very large parts possible | Limited by mold size and press capacity |
| Labor intensity | High | Lower |
| Resin emission control | Poor | Better (closed system) |
| Repeatability | Operator dependent | High |

**Open-Mold Merits:**
- Low capital investment.
- Simple tooling; suitable for prototypes and large structures.
- Easy to incorporate inserts and thickness changes.

**Open-Mold Demerits:**
- Poor reproducibility.
- High VOC emissions (environmental/health concern).
- Lower mechanical performance due to high void content.

**Closed-Mold Merits:**
- High $V_f$, low void content → better mechanical performance.
- Excellent surface finish on both sides.
- High repeatability; automation-friendly.

**Closed-Mold Demerits:**
- High tooling cost.
- Mold size limits part dimensions.
- Higher design complexity; skilled workforce required.

> *(7 marks = Comparison table 4 + Merits/Demerits 2 + Application example 1.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting volume fraction closure** $V_f + V_m = 1$ — using $V_f$ directly without subtracting from 1 for matrix term → wrong answer in numericals.
> 2. **Confusing Voigt and Reuss models** — using the longitudinal formula for transverse modulus is a **fatal error**; the examiner deducts **at least 2 marks** instantly.
> 3. **Skipping the unit in final answers** — writing "$E_L = 34.3$" instead of "$E_L = 34.3$ GPa" costs a mark in KTU board valuation.
> 4. **No sketch in process questions** — Even a hand-drawn block diagram with arrows earns at least **2 marks** as per KTU marking scheme; students often skip it.
> 5. **Mishandling 3D printing of composites** — failing to mention additive manufacturing (FDM with carbon-fiber filaments, Markforged continuous fiber deposition) when discussing modern composite frameworks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Composite** = matrix + reinforcement (heterogeneous, synergistic).
- [x] **Three matrix families:** PMC, MMC, CMC — PMC is most common in 3D printing.
- [x] **Reinforcement forms:** continuous fiber > short fiber > particulate in terms of strength.
- [x] **Rule of mixtures — Density:** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1-V_f)$.
- [x] **Rule of mixtures — Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt, iso-strain):** $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m (1-V_f)$.
- [x] **Rule of mixtures — Transverse Modulus (Reuss, iso-stress):** $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$.
- [x] **Voigt = upper bound, Reuss = lower bound.**
- [x] **Critical fiber length:** $l_c = \sigma_f d / (2\tau_i)$ — controls short vs. long fiber behavior.
- [x] **Void content > 2%** degrades composite properties significantly.
- [x] **Hand lay-up** = cheapest open-mold, low $V_f$, high void content.
- [x] **RTM/VARTM** = higher $V_f$, better finish, closed-mold.
- [x] **Pultrusion** = continuous, constant cross-section, highest $V_f$.
- [x] **Filament winding** = rotational symmetry parts (pipes, tanks).
- [x] **3D printing of composites:** FDM with fiber-filled filaments, Markforged continuous fiber, SLA with nanocomposite resins.
- [x] **Always** state units in numerical answers and include a process sketch for "explain with neat diagram" questions.
